title: Phase 4 Uses Three Independent AI Auditors

acceptance_criteria: Three independent AI auditors review the code against requirements.
skill_section: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph
citation_excerpt:
> - **Phase 4 (Spec Audit):** Three independent AI auditors check the code against requirements
> - **Phase 5 (Reconciliation):** Close the loop — TDD red-green verification for every bug
> - **Phase 6 (Verify):** Self-check benchmarks validate all generated artifacts
>
> After the numbered phases complete, you can run iteration strategies (gap, unfiltered, parity, adversarial) to find additional bugs — iterations typically add 40-60% more confirmed bugs on top of the baseline.
>
> The playbook works best when you provide documentation alongside the code — specs, API docs, design documents, community documentation. It also gets significantly better results when you run each phase separately rather than all at once.
>
> To get started, say: **"Run the quality playbook on this project."**

- Run the test (using the project's test runner). If the test PASSES, the bug is fixed. If it FAILS, the bug is still present.

title: Failing seeds must appear in this run's BUGS.md unconditionally
description: A seed that fails its assertion check is a confirmed carry-forward bug and must be included in the current run's BUGS.md regardless of whether any auditor independently discovers it during Phases 3–5. Auditor non-discovery does not override mechanical confirmation.
acceptance_criteria: A failing seed is a confirmed carry-forward bug — it must appear in this run's BUGS.md regardless of whether any auditor independently finds it.
skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically
citation_excerpt: **Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically.** For each seed, run the assertion against the current source tree. Record PASS (bug was fixed since last run) or FAIL (bug still present). A failing seed is a confirmed carry-forward bug — it must appear in this run's BUGS.md regardless of whether any auditor independently finds it. A passing seed means the bug was fixed — note it in PROGRESS.md as "SEED-NNN: resolved since prior run."

title: Passing seeds are noted in PROGRESS.md as resolved
description: A seed that passes its assertion check (bug no longer present) must be recorded in PROGRESS.md with the notation 'SEED-NNN: resolved since prior run', using the seed's identifier. These entries confirm the bug was fixed and prevent future runs from re-injecting it.
acceptance_criteria: A passing seed means the bug was fixed — note it in PROGRESS.md as 'SEED-NNN: resolved since prior run'.

title: Scan for Index File First When Chat History Provided
description: When the user provides a chat history folder, the skill's first action must be to look for an index or navigation file before reading any chat content. The skill must look for files named INDEX*, CONTEXT.md, README.md, or similar navigation aids.
acceptance_criteria: Look for files named INDEX*, CONTEXT.md, README.md, or similar navigation aids. If one exists, read it.
skill_section: Step 0, numbered list item 1, line ~455–457
citation_excerpt:
1. **Scan for an index file first.** Look for files named `INDEX*`, `CONTEXT.md`, `README.md`, or similar navigation aids. If one exists, read it — it will tell you what's there and how to find things.
2. **Search for quality-relevant conversations.** Look for messages mentioning: quality, testing, coverage, bugs, failures, incidents, crashes, validation, retry, recovery, spec, fitness, audit, review. Also search for the project name.
3. **Extract design decisions and incident history.** The most valuable content is: (a) incident reports — what went wrong, how many records affected, how it was detected, (b) design discussions — why a particular approach was chosen, what alternatives were rejected, (c) quality framework discussions — coverage targets, testing philosophy, model review experiences, (d) cross-model feedback — where different AI models disagreed about the code.
4. **Don't try to read everything.** Chat histories can be enormous. Use the index to find the most relevant conversations, then search within those for quality-related content. 10 minutes of targeted searching beats 2 hours of exhaustive reading.

citation_excerpt: **The scoping rule:** Do not narrow the audit scope to only the subsystems that have deep documentation. If the most complex or most failure-prone module has only shallow documentation, that is a **documentation gap to flag in PROGRESS.md**, not a reason to skip the module. The highest-risk code with the thinnest documentation is where bugs hide — auditing only well-documented areas produces a safe-looking report that misses real defects.

title: Identify Test Count from Existing Tests

title: Identify Coverage Patterns from Existing Tests

skill_section: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Identify Coverage Gaps from Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify gaps in the existing test coverage — areas of the codebase that have no or insufficient test coverage — and record these gaps for use in subsequent steps.
acceptance_criteria: Identify: test count, coverage patterns, gaps, and any coverage theater
skill_section: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1
citation_excerpt: [Test framework, test count, coverage areas, gaps]

title: Identify Coverage Theater in Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify coverage theater — tests that appear to provide meaningful coverage but do not actually catch real bugs. These tests must be flagged explicitly as part of the Step 3 analysis.
acceptance_criteria: any coverage theater (tests that look good but don't catch real bugs)
citation_excerpt: These patterns look like tests but don't catch real bugs:

title: Record the Import Pattern Used by Existing Tests
description: The agent must record the exact import pattern used by existing tests to import project modules. This is marked Critical in the specification. The recorded pattern must cover the language-specific mechanism (e.g., Python sys.path manipulation, Java/Scala package imports, TypeScript relative paths or aliases, Go package/module paths, Rust use crate:: or use myproject::).
acceptance_criteria: Critical: Record the import pattern. How do existing tests import project modules?
skill_section: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 2 (Critical callout)
citation_excerpt:
- **Match the existing import pattern exactly.** Read how existing tests import project modules and do the same thing. Getting this wrong means every test fails.
- **Read every function's signature before calling it.** Read the actual `def` line — parameter names, types, defaults. Read real data files from the project to understand data shapes. Do not guess at function parameters or fixture structures.
- **No placeholder tests.** Every test must import and call actual project code. If the body is `pass` or the assertion is trivial (`assert isinstance(x, list)`), delete it. A test that doesn't exercise project code inflates the count and creates false confidence.
- **Test count heuristic** = (testable spec sections) + (QUALITY.md scenarios) + (defensive patterns). For a medium project (5–15 source files), this typically yields 35–50 tests. Significantly fewer suggests missed requirements or shallow exploration. Significantly more is fine if every test is meaningful — don't pad to hit a number.
- **Cross-variant heuristic: ~30%** — If the project handles multiple input types, aim for roughly 30% of tests parametrized across all variants. The exact percentage matters less than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants.
- **Test outcomes, not mechanisms** — Assert what the spec says should happen, not how the code implements it.
- **Use schema-valid mutations** — Boundary tests must use values the schema accepts (from Step 5b), not values it rejects.

title: Use Exact Same Import Pattern in Functional Tests
description: The agent must use the exact same import pattern found in existing tests when writing functional tests. Deviating from the recorded pattern is explicitly prohibited because doing so causes every test to fail with import or resolution errors.
acceptance_criteria: You must use the exact same pattern in your functional tests — getting this wrong means every test fails with import/resolution errors.
skill_section: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 2 (Critical callout)
citation_excerpt:
Key rules:
- **Match the existing import pattern exactly.** Read how existing tests import project modules and do the same thing. Getting this wrong means every test fails.
- **Read every function's signature before calling it.** Read the actual `def` line — parameter names, types, defaults. Read real data files from the project to understand data shapes. Do not guess at function parameters or fixture structures.
- **No placeholder tests.** Every test must import and call actual project code. If the body is `pass` or the assertion is trivial (`assert isinstance(x, list)`), delete it. A test that doesn't exercise project code inflates the count and creates false confidence.
- **Test count heuristic** = (testable spec sections) + (QUALITY.md scenarios) + (defensive patterns). For a medium project (5–15 source files), this typically yields 35–50 tests. Significantly fewer suggests missed requirements or shallow exploration. Significantly more is fine if every test is meaningful — don't pad to hit a number.
- **Cross-variant heuristic: ~30%** — If the project handles multiple input types, aim for roughly 30% of tests parametrized across all variants. The exact percentage matters less than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants.
- **Test outcomes, not mechanisms** — Assert what the spec says should happen, not how the code implements it.
- **Use schema-valid mutations** — Boundary tests must use values the schema accepts (from Step 5b), not values it rejects.

acceptance_criteria: See references/functional_tests.md § "Import Pattern" for the full six-language matrix.

title: Process each spec document section by section

2. Walk through each linked requirement one by one

6. Move to the next use case

- [ ] If using inferred requirements: all `[Req: inferred — ...]` items are flagged for user review
- [ ] Code review protocol is self-contained

title: Read Actual Function Signatures, Never Guess
description: Before writing any test, the agent must read the actual function definition for each function it plans to test to obtain parameter names, types, and defaults. It must not infer or guess the function signature from usage context or call sites — the function definition itself is the authoritative source.
acceptance_criteria: Don't guess from usage context — read the function definition and any documentation
skill_section: Step 4b, item 1, first sentence
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Read Language-Specific Documentation for Signatures
description: When reading function signatures, the agent must also read the language-appropriate documentation attached to each function: Python docstrings, Java/Scala Javadoc or ScalaDoc, TypeScript type annotations, Go godoc comments, and Rust doc comments and type signatures. Skipping language-specific documentation when it is present is not acceptable.
acceptance_criteria: Python docstrings, Java/Scala Javadoc/ScalaDoc, TypeScript type annotations, Go godoc comments, Rust doc comments and type signatures
skill_section: Step 4b, item 1, parenthetical language list
citation_excerpt:
1. **Read the actual function signatures** — parameter names, types, defaults. Don't guess from usage context — read the function definition and any documentation (Python docstrings, Java/Scala Javadoc/ScalaDoc, TypeScript type annotations, Go godoc comments, Rust doc comments and type signatures).
2. **Read real data files** — If the project has items files, fixture files, config files, or sample data (in `pipelines/`, `fixtures/`, `test_data/`, `examples/`), read them. Your test fixtures must match the real data shape exactly.
3. **Read existing test fixtures** — How do existing tests create test data? Copy their patterns. If they build config dicts with specific keys, use those exact keys.
4. **Check library versions** — Check the project's dependency manifest (`requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`/`build.gradle`, `go.mod`, `Cargo.toml`) to see what's actually available. Don't write tests that depend on library features that aren't installed. If a dependency might be missing, use the test framework's skip mechanism — see `references/functional_tests.md` § "Library version awareness" for framework-specific examples.

title: Convert Each Defensive Pattern Into a Fitness-to-Purpose Scenario
description: Every defensive code pattern discovered during Step 5 must be converted into at least one fitness-to-purpose scenario. The section treats defensive patterns as direct evidence of past failures or known risks, and each such pattern must produce a corresponding fitness-to-purpose scenario that validates the system behaves correctly under the conditions the defensive code was written to handle.

Distinct from the fitness-to-purpose scenario requirement, this requires a separate boundary test artifact per pattern — verifying edge and failure conditions at the boundary the defensive code was written to guard.

This reference document is the normative guide for executing Step 5 and must not be skipped.
acceptance_criteria: Read references/defensive_patterns.md for the systematic search approach, grep patterns, and how to convert findings into fitness-to-purpose scenarios and boundary tests

title: Find At Least 2-3 Defensive Patterns Per Core Source File
description: The minimum acceptable yield for Step 5 is 2 to 3 defensive patterns per core source file. Falling below this threshold indicates insufficient depth of analysis; fewer findings are a signal that the reviewer is skimming rather than fully reading the source.
acceptance_criteria: Minimum bar: at least 2–3 defensive patterns per core source file. If you find fewer, you're skimming
skill_section: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, paragraph beginning 'Minimum bar'
citation_excerpt: Minimum bar: at least 2–3 defensive patterns per core source file. If you find fewer, you're skimming — read function bodies, not just signatures.

title: Read Function Bodies Not Just Signatures When Searching for Defensive Patterns
description: During the defensive pattern search, the reviewer must read full function bodies rather than relying solely on function signatures. Reading only signatures is explicitly characterized as skimming and will cause the minimum pattern-per-file threshold to be missed.

- Read function bodies, not just signatures
- If unsure: flag as QUESTION, not BUG

title: Search Status/State Fields to Identify State Machines
description: When performing state machine tracing, the reviewer must search for status and state fields in models, enums, or constants using field names such as `status`, `state`, `phase`, or `mode` to systematically locate all state machines present in the project.

skill_section: Step 5a section, 'How to find state machines' paragraph, first search directive
citation_excerpt: **How to find state machines:** Search for status/state fields in models, enums, or constants (e.g., `status`, `state`, `phase`, `mode`). Search for guards that check status before allowing actions (e.g., `if status == "running"`, `match self.state`). Search for state transitions (assignments to status fields).

title: Search Guards That Check Status to Identify State Machines
description: When performing state machine tracing, the reviewer must search for guards that check status fields before allowing actions — such as `if status == "running"` or `match self.state` patterns — to ensure all state-dependent control flow is captured.
acceptance_criteria: Search for guards that check status before allowing actions (e.g., `if status == "running"`, `match self.state`).

title: Search State Transition Assignments to Identify State Machines

title: Enumerate All Possible States for Each State Machine

skill_section: Step 5a section, numbered list item 1: 'Enumerate all possible states'
citation_excerpt:
1. **Enumerate all possible states.** Read the enum, the constants, or grep for every value the field is assigned. List them all.
2. **For each consumer of state** (UI handlers, API endpoints, control flow guards), check: does it handle every possible state? A `switch`/`match` without a meaningful default, or an `if/elif` chain that doesn't cover all states, is a gap.
3. **For each state transition**, check: can you reach every state? Are there states you can enter but never leave? Are there states that block operations that should be available?
4. **Record gaps as findings.** A status guard that allows action X for "running" but not for "stuck" is a real bug if the user needs to perform action X on stuck processes. A process that enters a terminal state but never triggers cleanup is a real bug.

title: Read Schema Definitions When Validation Layer Exists
description: When the project under review contains a validation layer — including Pydantic models in Python, JSON Schema definitions, TypeScript interfaces or Zod schemas, Java Bean Validation annotations, or Scala case class codecs — the skill must read those schema definitions during Step 5b before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: If the project has a validation layer (Pydantic models in Python, JSON Schema, TypeScript interfaces/Zod schemas, Java Bean Validation annotations, Scala case class codecs), read the schema definitions now.

For every field you found a defensive pattern for, record what the schema accepts vs. rejects.

title: Record Schema Accept/Reject Behavior for Each Defensively-Patterned Field
description: For every field for which a defensive pattern was identified in Step 5, the skill must record what the schema accepts versus what it rejects. This creates a per-field mapping that links defensive code findings to schema constraints and enables the generation of valid boundary tests in later passes.
acceptance_criteria: For every field you found a defensive pattern for, record what the schema accepts vs. rejects.
citation_excerpt: For every field you found a defensive pattern for in Step 5, record:

title: Consult references/schema_mapping.md for Mapping Format

acceptance_criteria: Read `references/schema_mapping.md` for the mapping format and why this matters for writing valid boundary tests.

citation_excerpt: **Read `references/schema_mapping.md`** for the mapping format and why this matters for writing valid boundary tests.

title: Complete Step 6 Before Structured Pattern Selection
description: Step 6 (Domain-Knowledge Risk Analysis) is the primary bug-hunting pass for library and framework codebases and must be completed before selecting any structured patterns. The orchestrator must enforce this ordering so that domain-risk findings can inform pattern selection.
acceptance_criteria: Complete it before selecting any structured patterns.
skill_section: Step 6 opening paragraph, sentence 2
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Write Quality Risks to EXPLORATION.md Immediately
description: Results of the domain-knowledge risk analysis must be written to the `## Quality Risks` section of EXPLORATION.md immediately upon generation. The orchestrator must not hold them in memory or defer writing until later in the workflow.
acceptance_criteria: Write the results to the `## Quality Risks` section of EXPLORATION.md immediately — do not hold them in memory.
skill_section: Step 6 opening paragraph, sentence 3
citation_excerpt: **This is the primary bug-hunting pass for library and framework codebases.** Complete it before selecting any structured patterns. Write the results to the `## Quality Risks` section of EXPLORATION.md immediately — do not hold them in memory.

title: Use Both Code Exploration and Domain Knowledge as Sources
description: Step 6 must draw on exactly two sources: direct code exploration of the project under review, and training-based domain knowledge about what goes wrong in systems of that type. Analysis that uses only one source (e.g., only code reading without domain reasoning, or only generic domain knowledge without code inspection) does not satisfy this step.
acceptance_criteria: This step uses two sources — not just code exploration, but your training knowledge of what goes wrong in similar systems.
skill_section: Step 6, paragraph 2
citation_excerpt: Every project has a different failure profile. This step uses **two sources** — not just code exploration, but your training knowledge of what goes wrong in similar systems.

title: Generate At Least Five Ranked Failure Scenarios
description: The output of Step 6 must include a minimum of five failure scenarios drawn from domain knowledge, even when the analyst has not directly observed these failures in the codebase. The scenarios must be ranked (e.g., by priority or severity). Fewer than five scenarios or an unranked list fails the gate.
acceptance_criteria: Generate at least 5 ranked failure scenarios from this knowledge.
skill_section: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions
citation_excerpt:
[At least 5 domain-driven failure scenarios ranked by priority.
Each must name a specific function, file, and line and explain the failure
mechanism using domain knowledge of what goes wrong in systems like this.
These are hypotheses, not confirmed bugs — they tell Phase 2 where to look.
Frame each as: "Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific
edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior]."
A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has does NOT belong here.]

title: Bug Hypotheses Must Include File-Path and Line-Number Citations
description: Every failure scenario produced in Step 6 must be expressed as a specific bug hypothesis that includes a file path and line number citation into the actual codebase. Abstract or module-level citations (e.g., 'somewhere in auth.py') are insufficient; the citation must be specific enough to direct a reviewer to an exact location.
acceptance_criteria: Write them as specific bug hypotheses with file-path and line-number citations, ranked by priority.
citation_excerpt:
1. Read the finding: note the specific file, line number, function, and failure condition
2. Read the test: identify which function it calls and what condition it asserts
3. Confirm alignment: the test must call the function cited in the finding, trigger the specific condition the finding describes, and assert on the behavior the finding says is wrong

title: Bug Hypotheses Must Be Ranked by Priority
description: The failure scenarios written in Step 6 must be ordered by priority so that the highest-risk hypotheses appear first. An unordered list of hypotheses, even if otherwise well-formed, does not satisfy the ranking requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Write them as specific bug hypotheses with file-path and line-number citations, ranked by priority.
citation_excerpt:
1. Read the finding: note the specific file, line number, function, and failure condition
2. Read the test: identify which function it calls and what condition it asserts
3. Confirm alignment: the test must call the function cited in the finding, trigger the specific condition the finding describes, and assert on the behavior the finding says is wrong

title: Each Scenario Must Follow the Prescribed Framing Format
description: Each failure scenario must be framed using the required template: 'Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior].' This format ensures each hypothesis is anchored to a specific code site, names a concrete triggering input, and articulates both the actual and expected output. Scenarios that omit any element of this structure are malformed.
acceptance_criteria: Frame each as: 'Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior].'
skill_section: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions
citation_excerpt:
Frame each as: "Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific
edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior]."
A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has does NOT belong here.]

title: Scenarios Must Be Grounded in Actually Explored Code
description: After framing each domain-knowledge hypothesis, the analyst must ground it in the actual code that was explored during the pass, for example by citing a specific function and line range with a note about what to verify. Hypotheses that are purely theoretical and not connected to any specific code artifact in the project under review do not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Then ground them in the actual code you explored: 'Read persistence.py line ~340 (save_state): verify temp file + rename pattern.'
citation_excerpt: 4. Ground it in code: "Read persistence.py line ~340: verify temp file + rename pattern"

title: Quality Risks Must Not List Defensive Patterns the Code Already Has
description: The `## Quality Risks` section must not enumerate defensive patterns that are already present in the codebase (i.e., things the code does right). Listing existing safeguards as risks is a reassurance exercise and will fail the quality gate. Only gaps, weaknesses, and hypothesized failure modes belong in the section.
acceptance_criteria: A Quality Risks section that lists defensive patterns the code already has (things the code does right) is not a risk analysis — it is a reassurance exercise and will not find bugs.
skill_section: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, item 1
citation_excerpt: A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has does NOT belong here.]

title: Quality Risks Must Not List Risky Modules Without Specific Failure Scenarios
description: Naming modules or components as risky without supplying a specific failure scenario for each is not actionable and fails the gate. Every entry in `## Quality Risks` must describe a concrete failure scenario, not merely identify a location or category of concern.
acceptance_criteria: A section that lists risky modules without specific failure scenarios is not actionable.
skill_section: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, item 2
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Quality Risks Must Not Conclude Mature Libraries Are Unlikely to Have Bugs
description: The `## Quality Risks` section must not contain a conclusion that a mature or well-tested library is unlikely to have basic bugs. Such a conclusion is actively harmful because mature libraries have the most subtle API-contract and edge-case bugs, which are exactly the class that Step 6 is designed to surface. Any framing that dismisses the risk profile of a library based on its maturity fails the gate.
acceptance_criteria: A section that concludes 'this is a mature, well-tested library so basic bugs are unlikely' is actively harmful — mature libraries have the most subtle API-contract and edge-case bugs, precisely because the obvious ones were found years ago.
skill_section: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, item 3
citation_excerpt: **What this stage must NOT produce:** A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has (things the code does RIGHT) is not a risk analysis. A section that lists risky modules without specific failure scenarios is not a risk analysis. A section that concludes "this is a mature, well-tested library so basic bugs are unlikely" is actively harmful — mature libraries have the most subtle bugs, precisely because the obvious ones were found years ago. The test: could a code reviewer read each scenario and immediately know what to check? If not, the scenario is too abstract.

title: Each Scenario Must Be Specific Enough to Direct a Reviewer to a Function and Test Input
description: The actionability test for every failure scenario in `## Quality Risks` is: could a code reviewer read it and immediately know what function to open and what input to test? If a scenario is too abstract to pass this test, it does not satisfy the step's requirements, regardless of whether it uses the correct framing template.
acceptance_criteria: The test: could a code reviewer read each scenario and immediately know what function to open and what input to test? If not, the scenario is too abstract.
skill_section: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, closing sentence
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase A contract extraction writes to quality/CONTRACTS.md
description: The first phase of the requirements pipeline (Phase A — Contract extraction) must read all source files, list every behavioral contract, and write the results to quality/CONTRACTS.md. This is a discovery phase where everything is listed, even obvious items.
acceptance_criteria: After Phase A completes, quality/CONTRACTS.md exists and contains behavioral contracts extracted from all source files; every behavioral contract found during exploration is listed.
citation_excerpt:
|------|---------|
| `quality/CONTRACTS.md` | Raw behavioral contracts extracted from source |
| `quality/REQUIREMENTS.md` | Testable requirements with narrative (the primary deliverable) |
| `quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md` | Contract-to-requirement traceability |
| `quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md` | Final completeness assessment with verdict |
| `quality/VERSION_HISTORY.md` | Review log with version table and provenance |
| `quality/REFINEMENT_HINTS.md` | Review progress and feedback (created during review) |

title: Phase B writes REQ records to quality/requirements_manifest.json as source of truth
description: Phase B (Requirement derivation) must write formal REQ records to quality/requirements_manifest.json, which is designated as the source of truth for requirements. The pipeline must treat this file as authoritative over any other rendered output.
acceptance_criteria: After Phase B completes, quality/requirements_manifest.json exists and contains all formal REQ records; it is designated and treated as the source of truth.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B bullet: 'Write REQ records to quality/requirements_manifest.json (source of truth) and render to quality/REQUIREMENTS.md.'
citation_excerpt:
- `quality/requirements_manifest.json` exists (v1.5.2 — authoritative REQ records, rendered to REQUIREMENTS.md)
- `quality/use_cases_manifest.json` exists (v1.5.2 — authoritative UC records, rendered to USE_CASES.md / the REQUIREMENTS.md narrative)
- `quality/citation_semantic_check.json` exists (v1.5.2 — Phase 4 Layer-2 output; empty `reviews[]` is valid for Spec Gap runs)
- If Phase 3 ran: `quality/code_reviews/` contains at least one `.md` file
- If Phase 4 ran: `quality/spec_audits/` contains a triage file AND individual auditor files
- If Phase 0 or 0b ran: `quality/SEED_CHECKS.md` exists as a standalone file (not inlined in PROGRESS.md)
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/bugs_manifest.json` exists (v1.5.2 — authoritative BUG records per schemas.md §8)
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/results/tdd-results.json` exists
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log` exists for every confirmed bug ID in `quality/BUGS.md`
- If confirmed bugs exist with fix patches: `quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log` exists for each bug that has a `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch`

title: Phase B renders requirements to quality/REQUIREMENTS.md
description: In addition to writing to requirements_manifest.json, Phase B must render the derived requirements to quality/REQUIREMENTS.md. Both artifacts must be produced.
acceptance_criteria: After Phase B completes, quality/REQUIREMENTS.md exists and contains rendered requirements derived from CONTRACTS.md and documentation.
citation_excerpt:
2. `quality/REQUIREMENTS.md` — Testable requirements derived during playbook generation
3. [Main architectural doc]
4. [Key design decisions doc]
5. [Any other essential context]

title: Every REQ must record a tier value (1–5)
description: Every requirement record in the pipeline must carry a tier integer from 1 to 5 per schemas.md §3.1. The tier is a required field on every REQ and must not be omitted.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ record in requirements_manifest.json carries a 'tier' field with an integer value in the range 1–5; any REQ missing 'tier' is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B bullet: 'For each requirement, record the tier (1–5 per schemas.md §3.1).'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Tier 1/2 REQs must include a citation block with document, document_sha256, locator, and citation_excerpt
description: When a requirement's tier is 1 or 2, the REQ must also carry a citation block containing: the document name, document_sha256, at least one of section or line, and a mechanically-extracted citation_excerpt. All four components are required.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ with tier ∈ {1, 2} carries a citation block; the block includes 'document', 'document_sha256', at least one of 'section'/'line', and 'citation_excerpt'; any Tier 1/2 REQ missing any of these fields is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B bullet: 'when tier ∈ {1, 2} — the citation block produced by bin/reference_docs_ingest invoking bin/citation_verifier per schemas.md §5.4 / §5.5... document, document_sha256, at least one of section/line, and a mechanically-extracted citation_excerpt.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: LLM must not shell out to citation_verifier directly
description: The LLM (model) must not invoke bin/citation_verifier directly. The citation_excerpt is a product of the ingest pipeline (bin/reference_docs_ingest invoking bin/citation_verifier); the LLM only consumes the excerpt from formal_docs_manifest.json.
acceptance_criteria: No shell invocation of bin/citation_verifier appears in the model's action trace for a run; citation excerpts are consumed from formal_docs_manifest.json, not generated by direct shell calls.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B bullet: 'The LLM does not shell out to citation_verifier directly; the excerpt is a product of the ingest pipeline.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Tier 3 REQs must cite source file:line in description; citations must not appear on Tier 3/4/5 REQs
description: For Tier 3 requirements (code-is-the-spec), the source file and line number must be cited in the requirement's description field. Furthermore, the formal citation block (used for Tier 1/2) must NOT appear on any Tier 3, 4, or 5 requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Every Tier 3 REQ description contains a 'file:line' citation; no Tier 3/4/5 REQ carries a 'citation' block; the citation block is exclusive to Tier 1 and Tier 2.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B bullet: 'For Tier 3 REQs (code-is-the-spec), cite the source file:line in the description; citations are for FORMAL_DOC references only and must not appear on Tier 3/4/5 REQs.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pattern: field must be one of whitelist/parity/compensation or absent; invalid value fails quality_gate.py
description: The optional Pattern: field on a REQ, when present, must take exactly one of three values: 'whitelist', 'parity', or 'compensation'. Any other value is invalid and must cause quality_gate.py to fail. Omitting the field is valid.
acceptance_criteria: A REQ with Pattern: set to any value other than 'whitelist', 'parity', or 'compensation' causes quality_gate.py to fail; a REQ with no Pattern: field passes the gate without error related to this field.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B optional Pattern field block: 'Pattern: whitelist', 'Pattern: parity', 'Pattern: compensation'; 'Setting an invalid value fails quality_gate.py.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pattern: field must be preserved from Phase-1 hypothesis to REQUIREMENTS.md and requirements_manifest.json
description: The preservation rule states that when a Phase-1 hypothesis (EXPLORATION.md) already carries a Pattern: field on a REQ, Phase 2 is REQUIRED to transcribe that field to both quality/REQUIREMENTS.md and quality/requirements_manifest.json. Silent omission of Pattern: during Phase 2 is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Every Pattern: value present on a Phase-1 hypothesis appears on the corresponding REQ in both quality/REQUIREMENTS.md and quality/requirements_manifest.json; any REQ that had Pattern: in Phase 1 but lacks it in Phase 2 artifacts is a non-conformant silent omission.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B preservation rule: 'it is REQUIRED to preserve when the Phase-1 hypothesis already carried it. Phase 2 must transcribe Pattern: from EXPLORATION.md to quality/REQUIREMENTS.md and quality/requirements_manifest.json whenever present.'
citation_excerpt:
transcribe `Pattern:` from EXPLORATION.md to `quality/REQUIREMENTS.md` and
   `quality/requirements_manifest.json` whenever present. Silent omission is a
   documented v1.4.5-regression vector — the Phase 5 cardinality gate cannot
   enforce coverage on a REQ it doesn't know is pattern-tagged. The gate's
   structural backstop (C13.7/Fix 2) cross-checks REQs that carry per-site UC
   references (`UC-N.a`/`UC-N.b` form emitted by Phase 1's Cartesian UC rule)
   and fails the gate if Pattern is missing on such a REQ.

title: Gate C13.7/Fix 2 must fail when Pattern is missing on REQs with per-site UC references
description: The structural backstop at gate C13.7/Fix 2 must cross-check REQs that carry per-site UC references (in UC-N.a/UC-N.b form emitted by Phase 1's Cartesian UC rule). If such a REQ is missing its Pattern: field, the gate must fail.
acceptance_criteria: quality_gate.py C13.7/Fix 2 fails when a REQ carries UC references in UC-N.a/UC-N.b form but has no Pattern: field; REQs with UC-N.a/UC-N.b references and a valid Pattern: field pass this check.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B preservation rule: 'The gate's structural backstop (C13.7/Fix 2) cross-checks REQs that carry per-site UC references (UC-N.a/UC-N.b form emitted by Phase 1's Cartesian UC rule) and fails the gate if Pattern is missing on such a REQ.'
citation_excerpt:
structural backstop (C13.7/Fix 2) cross-checks REQs that carry per-site UC
   references (`UC-N.a`/`UC-N.b` form emitted by Phase 1's Cartesian UC rule)
   and fails the gate if Pattern is missing on such a REQ.

title: Requirements asserting specific constants must distinguish spec-says vs code-actually-contains
description: When writing a requirement that asserts specific constants, values, or labels are handled by a specific function, the requirement must distinguish between what the spec says should be there and what the code actually contains. The requirement writer must extract actual contents from the code (case labels, map keys, if-else branches) and compare against the spec's list.
acceptance_criteria: No requirement is written as 'must preserve X, Y, and Z' without first extracting and comparing the actual code contents; each requirement distinguishes 'spec-asserted' from 'code-verified' when making constant-presence claims.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B primary-source extraction rule: 'When writing a requirement that asserts specific constants, values, or labels are handled by a specific function... the requirement must distinguish between what the spec says should be there and what the code actually contains.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Constants present in spec but absent from code must be recorded as [NOT IN CODE] with file:line
description: When a constant appears in a specification but does NOT appear in the actual code, the requirement must be written as '[NOT IN CODE]: defined in header.h:NN but absent from function() at file.c:NN-NN'. The requirement must not state 'must preserve X' without verifying X is actually present.
acceptance_criteria: Every requirement citing a constant that is in the spec but not in the code includes a '[NOT IN CODE]' marker with the spec file:line and the absent code location; no requirement asserts code presence without code verification.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B primary-source extraction rule: 'If a constant appears in the spec but NOT in the code, write the requirement as "must handle X — [NOT IN CODE]: defined in header.h:NN but absent from function() at file.c:NN-NN."'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Mechanical extraction command must be generated and executed before writing contract line for dispatch functions
description: When a contract asserts that a function handles, preserves, or dispatches a set of named constants (feature bits, enum values, opcode tables, event types, handler registries), the pipeline must generate and execute a shell command that mechanically extracts the actual case labels/branches from the function body BEFORE writing the contract line.
acceptance_criteria: For any contract asserting dispatch-function coverage, evidence of a prior shell command execution (mechanical extraction) exists before the corresponding contract line is written; no contract asserting dispatch coverage is written without a preceding mechanical extraction.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B mechanical verification artifact block: 'When a contract asserts that a function handles, preserves, or dispatches a set of named constants... you must generate and execute a shell command... that mechanically extracts the actual case labels/branches from the function body before writing the contract line.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Mechanical extraction raw output must be saved to quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt
description: The raw output of each mechanical extraction command must be saved to quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt where <function> is the name of the function being examined. This file is the authoritative record of what the function handles.
acceptance_criteria: For each dispatch function examined mechanically, a file exists at quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt containing the raw shell command output; the file is produced by shell redirect, not hand-written.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B mechanical verification: 'Save the raw output to quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt. The command must be a non-interactive pipeline... that cannot hallucinate — it reads file bytes and prints matches.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Contract asserting constant presence is forbidden without matching case in mechanical output
description: A contract line asserting that a function 'preserves constant X' is forbidden unless quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt contains a matching 'case X:' line. If the constant is in a spec or header but absent from the mechanical output, the contract must record it as absent.
acceptance_criteria: Every contract claiming 'function preserves constant X' has a corresponding 'case X:' in quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt; any constant absent from the mechanical file triggers an 'absent' notation in the contract instead of a presence claim.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B: 'A contract line asserting "function preserves constant X" is forbidden unless quality/mechanical/<function>_cases.txt contains a matching case X: line.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Each extraction command must be appended to quality/mechanical/verify.sh
description: For each mechanical extraction command executed, that same command must also be appended to quality/mechanical/verify.sh as a verification step. The script must re-run the extraction pipeline and diff the result against the saved file.
acceptance_criteria: quality/mechanical/verify.sh exists and contains one verification block per mechanical extraction; each block re-runs the exact extraction pipeline and diffs the output against the corresponding saved *_cases.txt file.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B mechanical artifact integrity: 'For each mechanical extraction command, also append it to quality/mechanical/verify.sh as a verification step. The script must re-run the same extraction pipeline and diff the result against the saved file.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase 6 must execute bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh; benchmark fails on any artifact mismatch
description: Phase 6 of the pipeline must execute 'bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh'. If any artifact comparison in verify.sh reports a mismatch, the benchmark fails. This prevents the failure mode where mechanical output was replaced with fabricated content.
acceptance_criteria: Phase 6 execution log shows 'bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh' was run; if any *_cases.txt does not match the fresh extraction output, the benchmark exits non-zero and is recorded as failed.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B: 'Phase 6 must execute bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh and the benchmark fails if any artifact mismatches.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: verify.sh must run immediately after writing *_cases.txt and before any downstream artifacts
description: The immediate integrity gate requires that 'bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh' be run immediately after each *_cases.txt file is written and BEFORE writing any contract, requirement, or prose artifact that cites the extraction. This gate prevents downstream artifacts from being built on a potentially forged extraction.
acceptance_criteria: In any run that writes a *_cases.txt file, verify.sh is executed before any contract line, REQUIREMENTS.md entry, or prose artifact citing that extraction is written; the execution ordering is verifiable from timestamps or audit log.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B immediate integrity gate: 'Run bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh immediately after writing each *_cases.txt file and before writing any contract, requirement, or prose artifact that cites the extraction.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Failed verify.sh must trigger stop, delete failed file, and re-run with fresh redirect
description: If verify.sh exits with a non-zero code, the pipeline must: (1) stop, (2) delete the failed *_cases.txt file, (3) re-run the extraction command with a fresh shell redirect (not hand-editing the output), and (4) re-verify. Hand-editing the output file is not permitted as a remediation.
acceptance_criteria: When verify.sh exits non-zero: the failed *_cases.txt is deleted; a fresh shell redirect is used to re-run the extraction command; verify.sh is re-run on the new file; the pipeline does not advance until verify.sh exits 0.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B immediate integrity gate: 'If exit code ≠ 0: stop, delete the failed *_cases.txt, re-run the extraction command with a fresh shell redirect (do not hand-edit the output), and re-verify.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pipeline must not advance to Phase 3/2c until verify.sh exits 0
description: The pipeline must not proceed to Phase 3 (or Phase 2c) until verify.sh exits with code 0. This gate prevents downstream phases from inheriting a potentially corrupted mechanical extraction.
acceptance_criteria: No Phase 3 or Phase 2c activity begins if verify.sh last exited non-zero; the gate is a hard stop, not a warning.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B immediate integrity gate: 'Do not advance to Phase 3/2c until verify.sh exits 0.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: verify.sh stdout must be saved to quality/results/mechanical-verify.log
description: The standard output of verify.sh must be saved to quality/results/mechanical-verify.log as a durable receipt file. This log serves as an audit trail for the mechanical verification gate.
acceptance_criteria: After verify.sh runs, quality/results/mechanical-verify.log exists and contains the stdout output from the verify.sh execution.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B immediate integrity gate: 'Save verify.sh stdout and exit code to quality/results/mechanical-verify.log and quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit as durable receipt files.'
citation_excerpt:
- `quality/results/mechanical-verify.log` — full stdout/stderr from `bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh`
- `quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit` — a single line containing the exit code (e.g., `0`)

title: verify.sh exit code must be saved to quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit
description: The exit code of verify.sh must be saved to quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit as a durable receipt file alongside the log. This enables gate checks to confirm verification was performed and passed.
acceptance_criteria: After verify.sh runs, quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit exists and contains the numeric exit code of the verify.sh execution.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B immediate integrity gate: 'Save verify.sh stdout and exit code to quality/results/mechanical-verify.log and quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit as durable receipt files.'
citation_excerpt: - `quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit` — a single line containing the exit code (e.g., `0`)

title: Mechanical artifacts are immutable after extraction and verification
description: Once a *_cases.txt file has been written by the shell redirect and verified by verify.sh, it must not be modified, overwritten, or regenerated for the remainder of the run. Downstream discrepancies between the mechanical artifact and a requirement or contract must be resolved by fixing the prose, not the extraction.
acceptance_criteria: No *_cases.txt file is modified after its initial verify.sh pass; when a discrepancy is found between a requirement and a mechanical artifact, the requirement is updated to match the artifact, not vice versa.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B: 'Once a *_cases.txt file has been written by the shell redirect and verified by verify.sh, it must not be modified, overwritten, or regenerated... Fix the prose, not the extraction.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Triage probes must not use mechanical artifact as sole evidence for source file contents
description: Triage probes, verification probes, and audit assertions must not use the mechanical artifact file (quality/mechanical/*.txt) as the sole evidence for what a source file contains. To verify that function F handles constant C, the probe must either read the source file directly with line-anchored assertions, or re-execute the same extraction pipeline used by verify.sh.
acceptance_criteria: No triage probe uses only 'open(quality/mechanical/...)' or 'cat quality/mechanical/...' to assert source file contents; probes either read the source file directly or re-execute the extraction pipeline.
citation_excerpt: Grep all triage and verification probe files (`quality/spec_audits/*`) for `open('quality/mechanical/` or `cat quality/mechanical/`. If any probe reads a `quality/mechanical/*.txt` file as sole evidence for what a source file contains, it is circular verification and the benchmark fails. Probes must read the source file directly or re-execute the extraction pipeline. This benchmark exists because v1.3.23 Probe C validated the forged mechanical artifact instead of the source code, passing with fabricated data.

title: quality/mechanical/ directory must not be created without dispatch/registry/enumeration contracts
description: The quality/mechanical/ directory must only be created if the project's contracts include dispatch functions, registries, or enumeration checks that require mechanical extraction. If no such contracts exist, the directory must be skipped entirely. Creating an empty mechanical/ directory (or one without verify.sh) is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: If the project has no dispatch-function, registry, or enumeration contracts, quality/mechanical/ is not created; if quality/mechanical/ exists, it contains at least one *_cases.txt and a verify.sh; an empty quality/mechanical/ directory is a non-conformant state.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B: 'Only create quality/mechanical/ if the project's contracts include dispatch functions, registries, or enumeration checks that require mechanical extraction. If no such contracts exist, skip the directory entirely... Creating an empty mechanical/ directory (or one without verify.sh) is non-conformant.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: If no dispatch contracts, record NOT APPLICABLE for mechanical verification in PROGRESS.md
description: When no dispatch/registry/enumeration contracts exist and quality/mechanical/ is skipped, the pipeline must record in PROGRESS.md: 'Mechanical verification: NOT APPLICABLE — no dispatch/registry/enumeration contracts in scope.' This provides explicit documentation rather than silent absence.
acceptance_criteria: When quality/mechanical/ is not created, PROGRESS.md contains a line stating 'Mechanical verification: NOT APPLICABLE — no dispatch/registry/enumeration contracts in scope' or equivalent explicit statement.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B: 'If no such contracts exist, skip the directory entirely and record in PROGRESS.md: Mechanical verification: NOT APPLICABLE — no dispatch/registry/enumeration contracts in scope.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Expected behavior claims must use normative language; descriptive language requires mechanical confirmation
description: Requirements and contracts must use normative language ('must preserve,' 'should handle') for expected behavior. Descriptive language ('preserves,' 'handles') may only be used when the mechanical verification artifact confirms the claim. A requirement stating 'the implementation preserves X' without a confirming mechanical artifact is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Every requirement using descriptive language ('preserves', 'handles') cites a confirming mechanical artifact; requirements without mechanical confirmation use normative language ('must preserve', 'must handle'); any requirement with descriptive language and no mechanical backing is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase B normative vs. descriptive split: 'Requirements and contracts must use normative language ("must preserve," "should handle") for expected behavior. They may only use descriptive language ("preserves," "handles") when the mechanical verification artifact confirms the claim.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase C coverage loop must run up to 3 iterations until 100% coverage is reached
description: Phase C (Coverage verification) must cross-reference every contract against every requirement and loop up to 3 times until coverage reaches 100%. The pipeline must not declare coverage complete before reaching 100% or exhausting 3 iterations.
acceptance_criteria: Phase C iterates until coverage is 100% or 3 iterations have been completed; partial coverage declared after fewer than 3 iterations when coverage is below 100% is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase C bullet: 'Fix gaps. Loop up to 3 times until coverage reaches 100%.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Coverage matrix must have one row per requirement, not grouped ranges
description: The coverage matrix written in Phase C must have exactly one row per requirement (REQ-001, REQ-002, etc.). Grouped ranges like 'C-001 to C-007 | REQ-001, REQ-003' are forbidden because they make machine verification impossible and hide gaps.
acceptance_criteria: quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md contains exactly one row per REQ-NNN entry; no row uses a range notation (e.g., 'REQ-001 to REQ-005'); every individual requirement has its own distinct row.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase C: 'The matrix must have one row per requirement (REQ-001, REQ-002, etc.) — not grouped ranges like "C-001 to C-007 | REQ-001, REQ-003". Grouped ranges make machine verification impossible and hide gaps.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase C must write coverage matrix to quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md
description: The output artifact for Phase C (Coverage verification) is quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md. This file must exist after Phase C completes and must contain the cross-reference matrix.
acceptance_criteria: After Phase C completes, quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md exists and contains the coverage cross-reference matrix mapping contracts to requirements.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase C bullet: 'Write to quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase D completeness report must be written to quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md without a Verdict section
description: Phase D must write to quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md as a baseline completeness report. Critically, this report must NOT include a '## Verdict' section — the verdict is deferred to Phase 5 post-reconciliation, which produces the only verdict that counts for closure.
acceptance_criteria: quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md exists after Phase D; the file does not contain a '## Verdict' section; the verdict is absent from this baseline report.
citation_excerpt:
| `quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md` | Final completeness assessment with verdict |
| `quality/VERSION_HISTORY.md` | Review log with version table and provenance |
| `quality/REFINEMENT_HINTS.md` | Review progress and feedback (created during review) |

title: Phase D must run up to 3 self-refinement iterations, short-circuiting below 3 changes
description: After producing the initial completeness report, Phase D must run up to 3 self-refinement iterations. Each iteration reads the report, fixes gaps, and re-checks. The loop short-circuits when fewer than 3 changes occur in an iteration.
acceptance_criteria: Phase D runs at least 1 and at most 3 self-refinement iterations; if an iteration produces fewer than 3 changes, the loop exits immediately regardless of remaining iteration budget.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase D bullet: 'Then run up to 3 self-refinement iterations: read the report, fix gaps, re-check. Short-circuit when fewer than 3 changes per iteration.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Deep documents with 'will cover' commitment must have at least one traced requirement before Phase D
description: Phase D must verify that every deep document listed in the coverage commitment table with a 'will cover' commitment has at least one requirement traced to it. Requirements must be added for any gap before Phase D can be completed.
acceptance_criteria: Before Phase D is marked complete, every deep document listed as 'will cover' in PROGRESS.md has at least one REQ record in requirements_manifest.json tracing to the subsystem it documents; gaps trigger requirement additions before Phase D proceeds.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase D bullet: 'Also verify that every deep document with a "will cover" commitment in the coverage commitment table has at least one requirement traced to it — if not, add requirements for the gap before continuing.'
citation_excerpt: Re-read the coverage commitment table from PROGRESS.md. For each deep document you committed to covering ("will cover in Phase 2"), verify that at least one requirement traces to the subsystem it documents. If your requirements cover only some committed subsystems, add requirements for the gaps before completing Step 7.

title: REQUIREMENTS.md must begin with a human-readable overview covering what/who/risk
description: REQUIREMENTS.md must begin with a human-readable overview that answers: What is this project? What does it do? Who are the actors (users, systems, hardware, protocols)? What are the highest-risk areas? The overview must be useful to someone who has never seen the project before.
acceptance_criteria: quality/REQUIREMENTS.md opens with a prose overview section that answers all four questions (what, does, actors, risks) before any requirement listings; a reviewer who has never seen the project can understand its purpose from this section alone.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E: 'REQUIREMENTS.md must begin with a human-readable overview that answers: What is this project? What does it do? Who are the actors (users, systems, hardware, protocols)? What are the highest-risk areas?'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: REQUIREMENTS.md must not begin with raw scope metadata or HTML comments
description: The opening of REQUIREMENTS.md must be a plain-language description of the project. It must not begin with raw scope metadata (structured data fields) or HTML comments. The overview must lead with prose.
acceptance_criteria: The first non-empty content in REQUIREMENTS.md is plain-language prose, not scope metadata fields or HTML comment blocks; any metadata or comments appear only after the overview.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E: 'Do not start with raw scope metadata or HTML comments — lead with a plain-language description.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Overview validation gate is mandatory before proceeding to use case derivation
description: After writing the REQUIREMENTS.md overview, the pipeline must perform the overview validation gate self-check before proceeding to use case derivation. This gate verifies the overview describes the project as actual users would recognize it.
acceptance_criteria: The overview validation gate self-check (four questions: ecosystem role, who depends on it, user recognition, known adoption) is executed and passed before any use case derivation begins; skipping the gate is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, overview validation gate: 'After writing the overview, perform this self-check before proceeding to use case derivation: Does this overview describe the project the way its actual users would recognize it?'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Overview must name project's ecosystem role, real-world significance, and who depends on it
description: The overview in REQUIREMENTS.md must specifically name the project's ecosystem role and real-world significance, identify who depends on it and for what. For well-known projects it must reflect publicly known adoption patterns. If the overview reads as written only from source code inspection, it must be revised before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: The overview names: (1) the project's ecosystem role, (2) real-world significance, (3) who depends on it, (4) for what purpose; for well-known projects, it reflects community-known adoption (e.g., Cobra → kubectl/Hugo/GitHub CLI); overviews failing this are revised before use case derivation.
skill_section: Step 7, overview validation gate: 'Does it name the project's ecosystem role and real-world significance? Does it identify who depends on it and for what?... For well-known projects, does it reflect publicly known adoption?'
citation_excerpt:
> - Does it name the project's ecosystem role and real-world significance?
> - Does it identify who depends on it and for what?
> - Would a developer who uses this project daily say "yes, that's what it is and why it matters"?
> - For well-known projects, does it reflect publicly known adoption (e.g., Cobra → kubectl/Hugo/GitHub CLI; Express → millions of Node.js API servers; Zod → form validation/tRPC; Serde → the default Rust serialization layer)?

title: Use case derivation is mandatory and runs after the overview gate
description: Use case derivation is a mandatory step that must run after the overview validation gate passes. It must not be skipped, and it must execute in the correct sequence (overview gate first, then use cases, then acceptance criteria span check).
acceptance_criteria: Every run produces use cases; use case derivation does not begin until the overview validation gate has passed; if the overview gate fails and requires revision, use case derivation is deferred until the revised overview passes.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E: 'Use case derivation (mandatory, runs after overview gate).'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pipeline must derive exactly 5–7 use cases
description: The use case derivation step must produce between 5 and 7 use cases. Fewer than 5 or more than 7 use cases is non-conformant. The target count is based on deriving the most important real user outcomes from the validated overview.
acceptance_criteria: The requirements artifact contains between 5 and 7 use cases (inclusive); a run producing fewer than 5 or more than 7 use cases fails the derivation gate.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E use case derivation: 'Derive 5–7 use cases from the validated overview and gathered documentation.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Each use case must describe a real user outcome, not a code feature
description: Every use case must describe a real user outcome — what a user accomplishes — not a code feature or framework capability. 'Developer builds a CLI tool with nested subcommands' qualifies; 'Framework supports command trees' does not.
acceptance_criteria: Each of the 5–7 use cases is framed as a user accomplishment; no use case is framed as a code capability or framework feature; a reviewer can identify a real person achieving something in each use case.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E use case derivation: 'Describe a real user outcome, not a code feature. "Developer builds a CLI tool with nested subcommands, persistent flags, and shell completion" — not "Framework supports command trees."'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Each use case must name a concrete actor and their goal
description: Every use case must identify a specific, named actor (end-user developer, system administrator, kernel maintainer, protocol peer, integrator, automated consumer, etc.) and what that actor is trying to accomplish. Generic actors without named roles are insufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Each use case identifies a named actor type from the list (end-user developers, system administrators, kernel maintainers, protocol peers, integrators, automated consumers) and states what they are trying to accomplish.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E: 'Name a concrete actor and what they are trying to accomplish. Actors include end-user developers, system administrators, kernel maintainers, protocol peers, integrators, and automated consumers.'
citation_excerpt:
- Name a **concrete actor** and what they are trying to accomplish. Actors include end-user developers, system administrators, kernel maintainers, protocol peers, integrators, and automated consumers.
- Be **recognizable to an actual user** of the software. For well-known projects, validate use cases against the model's own knowledge of the project, community docs, tutorials, and real-world adoption patterns.
- Connect to at least one requirement through testable conditions of satisfaction.

title: Each use case must connect to at least one requirement through testable conditions of satisfaction
description: Every use case must be connected to at least one requirement through testable conditions of satisfaction. A use case with no linked requirement has no mechanism for verification and is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Every use case has at least one REQ in requirements_manifest.json with a use_cases[] entry referencing that use case ID; a use case with no linked REQ is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E use case derivation: 'Connect to at least one requirement through testable conditions of satisfaction.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Use case validation against code must verify each use case against the actual codebase
description: After deriving use cases from the overview and docs, each use case must be verified against the codebase. If a use case describes something the code doesn't actually support, it must be revised or removed. If the code supports an important user outcome with no use case coverage, a new use case must be added.
acceptance_criteria: Each use case has documented code-validation evidence; use cases describing unsupported functionality are revised or removed; important code-supported outcomes without use cases trigger new use case additions.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E: 'After deriving use cases from the overview and docs, verify each one against the codebase. If a use case describes something the code doesn't actually support, revise or remove it.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Acceptance criteria span check is mandatory after use case derivation
description: After use cases are finalized and validated against code, the pipeline must perform an acceptance criteria span check: verifying that the conditions of satisfaction across all requirements collectively span the project's main behaviors. This check must run before declaring Phase E complete.
acceptance_criteria: The acceptance criteria span check is performed and documented after use cases are finalized; the check verifies every major user-facing behavior from the overview is covered by at least one requirement's conditions of satisfaction.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E acceptance criteria span check: 'After use cases are finalized and validated against code, check whether the conditions of satisfaction across all requirements collectively span the project's main behaviors (mandatory, runs after use case derivation).'
citation_excerpt: > Do these acceptance criteria, taken together, cover the project? Is there a major user-facing behavior described in the overview or use cases that no requirement's conditions of satisfaction would catch if it broke?

title: Each use case must have at least one linked specific (non-architectural-guidance) requirement
description: For each use case, at least one requirement's conditions of satisfaction must be traceable to it, and at least one such linked requirement must be classified as 'specific' (not 'architectural-guidance'). Use cases with only architectural-guidance requirements linked indicate a coverage gap.
acceptance_criteria: Every use case has at least one REQ with specificity='specific' in its use_cases[] link; use cases where all linked REQs have specificity='architectural-guidance' are flagged as coverage gaps requiring remediation.
skill_section: Step 7, Phase E acceptance criteria span check: 'For each use case, at least one requirement's conditions of satisfaction must be traceable to it, and at least one linked requirement must be specific (not architectural-guidance).'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Tier-1-wins-over-Tier-2: contradictions between project spec and external standard are recorded at Tier 1
description: When a project's own spec (Tier 1) and an external standard (Tier 2) contradict each other, the requirement must be recorded at Tier 1 citing the project's position. A project's documented deviation from an external standard is authoritative intent, not a defect. The upstream-spec-issue disposition applies only when the project's spec is silent.
acceptance_criteria: No requirement records a contradiction between project spec and external standard at Tier 2; all such contradictions are recorded at Tier 1 with the project's position cited; Tier 2 REQs are used only for external standards without a conflicting project-spec position.
skill_section: Step 7, Tier-1-wins-over-Tier-2 rule: 'When a project's own spec (Tier 1) and an external standard (Tier 2) contradict each other, record the REQ at Tier 1 citing the project's position.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Spec-Gap degradation must report '0 Tier 1/2 requirements' as a metric in completeness report
description: If formal_docs_manifest.json contains zero FORMAL_DOC records covering the project's own behavior, all REQs degrade to Tier 3/4/5. The pipeline must report the meta-finding '0 Tier 1/2 requirements' in the completeness report as a metric, not a failure. The run degrades gracefully into a Spec Gap Analyzer.
acceptance_criteria: When no Tier 1 or Tier 2 REQs exist, the completeness report contains a '0 Tier 1/2 requirements' metric entry; the run is not marked as failed due to the absence of formal documentation; no citations are fabricated to improve tier distribution.
skill_section: Step 7, Spec-Gap degradation: 'If formal_docs_manifest.json contains zero FORMAL_DOC records... Report the meta-finding "0 Tier 1/2 requirements" in the completeness report as a metric, not a failure.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: quality_gate.py must reject Tier 1/2 REQs whose citation_excerpt does not byte-equal fresh extraction
description: quality_gate.py re-invokes bin/citation_verifier (via extract_excerpt) per schemas.md §5.4 at verification time and must reject any Tier 1/2 REQ whose citation_excerpt does not byte-equal the fresh extraction. This is invariant #11 from schemas.md §10.
acceptance_criteria: quality_gate.py re-runs citation_verifier at gate time; any Tier 1/2 REQ whose stored citation_excerpt differs even one byte from the fresh extraction output is rejected by the gate.
skill_section: Step 7, Spec-Gap degradation: 'quality_gate.py re-invokes bin/citation_verifier (via extract_excerpt) per §5.4 at verification time and rejects any Tier 1/2 REQ whose citation_excerpt does not byte-equal the fresh extraction (schemas.md §10 invariant #11).'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Every REQ must carry a functional_section string
description: The functional_section field is required on every REQ. It is a short LLM-derived string (e.g., 'Authentication', 'Bus enumeration') that groups related REQs. There is no predefined ontology; the grouping is derived from code and documentation.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ record in requirements_manifest.json has a non-empty 'functional_section' field; any REQ missing this field is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7: 'functional_section is a required field. Every REQ carries a short functional_section string... This is LLM-derived from the code and documentation; there is no predefined ontology.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Phase 2 rendering must group REQs under functional sections with an intro paragraph per section
description: When rendering quality/REQUIREMENTS.md, Phase 2 must group requirements under their functional_section headings and include a short introductory paragraph for each section. This grouping is reviewed by the Phase 4 Council for coherence.
acceptance_criteria: quality/REQUIREMENTS.md groups REQs under functional_section headings; each functional section in REQUIREMENTS.md is preceded by a short introductory paragraph; requirements are not listed as a flat ungrouped sequence.
skill_section: Step 7: 'Phase 2's rendering groups REQs under these sections (with a short intro paragraph per section) and the Phase 4 Council reviews the grouping for coherence.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: REQ must carry use_cases[] list of UC-NN IDs as a one-way forward link
description: Each REQ record must carry a use_cases[] list containing the UC-NN IDs of the use cases this requirement participates in. This is a one-way forward link from REQ to UC. The UC record must NOT carry a requirements[] back-link.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ in requirements_manifest.json has a 'use_cases' array field (may be empty); no UC record carries a 'requirements' field; reverse direction is derived at render time by querying REQs.
skill_section: Step 7, Traceability is one-way: 'The REQ carries a use_cases[] list of UC-NN IDs. The UC record does NOT carry a requirements[] back-link — the reverse direction is derived at render time by querying REQ records.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.
