```
title: User story must include a mandatory 'so that' clause
description: Every REQ must include a user story framed as 'As a [role] doing [action], I expect [behavior] so that [outcome].' The 'so that' clause is mandatory — it forces articulation of the intent behind the requirement. User stories missing the 'so that' clause are non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ's user_story field follows the pattern 'As a [role]... I expect... so that [outcome]'; any user story without a 'so that' clause is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, required fields, User story: 'Frame it from the caller's perspective: "As a [role] doing [action], I expect [behavior] so that [outcome]." The "so that" clause is mandatory.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Conditions of satisfaction must include happy path, edge cases, and failure modes
description: Every REQ's conditions of satisfaction must be specific and testable, covering three categories: the happy path, edge cases, and failure modes. Conditions covering only the happy path are insufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Each REQ's conditions of satisfaction section contains at least one scenario for each of: happy path, edge case, and failure mode; a REQ with conditions covering only normal flow is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, required fields, Conditions of satisfaction: 'Specific, testable scenarios that prove this requirement is met. Include the happy path, edge cases, and failure modes.'
citation_excerpt:
- **Conditions of satisfaction**: Specific, testable scenarios that prove this requirement is met. Include the happy path, edge cases, and failure modes. Each individual contract from Phase A that was grouped into this requirement becomes a condition of satisfaction.
- **Alternative paths**: Multiple code paths, modes, or entry points that must all satisfy the requirement. Alternative paths are where bugs hide.
- **Use cases**: `use_cases[]` — list of `UC-NN` IDs this REQ participates in. One-way forward link.
- **References**: Cite the source — spec section, ChangeLog entry, config field definition, source comment, issue number, or domain knowledge. For Tier 1/2 REQs the `citation` block carries the authoritative locator; free-form references are supplementary.
- **Specificity**: **specific** (testable — must have conditions of satisfaction that a code reviewer can check against a specific code location or behavior; this is the default and counts toward coverage metrics) or **architectural-guidance** (not testable against individual code paths — covers cross-cutting properties like "remain lightweight and stdlib-compatible" or "no_std support"; informs the quality constitution but is not counted in coverage metrics; most projects should have 0–3 architectural-guidance requirements — more than 3 triggers the mandatory self-check below). The category "directional" is retired. Any requirement that would have been "directional" must either be made specific (with testable conditions) or explicitly classified as architectural-guidance.

title: Each Phase A contract grouped into a REQ must become a condition of satisfaction
description: When multiple Phase A contracts are grouped into a single REQ during Phase B, each individual contract from that grouping must appear as a distinct condition of satisfaction in the merged REQ. No contract may be silently dropped during grouping.
acceptance_criteria: The total number of conditions of satisfaction across all REQs equals the number of contracts in quality/CONTRACTS.md; every contract is traceable to exactly one condition of satisfaction in one REQ.
citation_excerpt: **B.3 — Write requirements.** Use the 7-field template from SKILL.md Step 7. Conditions of satisfaction come from the individual contracts in the group — each contract becomes a condition of satisfaction.

title: REQ specificity must be 'specific' or 'architectural-guidance'; 'directional' is retired
description: Every REQ must carry a specificity classification of either 'specific' (testable against code) or 'architectural-guidance' (cross-cutting, not testable against individual paths). The 'directional' category is retired and must not appear in new REQs.
acceptance_criteria: Every REQ in requirements_manifest.json has a specificity field valued 'specific' or 'architectural-guidance'; no REQ carries specificity='directional'; a REQ with specificity='directional' is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Specificity: 'specific (testable...) or architectural-guidance (not testable...). The category "directional" is retired. Any requirement that would have been "directional" must either be made specific... or explicitly classified as architectural-guidance.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Requirements formerly classified as 'directional' must be reclassified as specific or architectural-guidance
description: Any requirement that would previously have been classified as 'directional' must be explicitly reclassified. It must either be made 'specific' (with testable conditions of satisfaction that a code reviewer can verify) or classified as 'architectural-guidance'. No in-between category is permitted.
acceptance_criteria: When reviewing legacy or migrated requirements, any REQ previously tagged 'directional' is either (a) reclassified 'specific' with new testable conditions added, or (b) reclassified 'architectural-guidance' with explicit justification; no 'directional' REQs persist.
skill_section: Step 7, Specificity: 'The category "directional" is retired. Any requirement that would have been "directional" must either be made specific (with testable conditions) or explicitly classified as architectural-guidance.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Architectural-guidance self-check is mandatory after requirement derivation
description: After completing requirement derivation, the pipeline must execute the architectural-guidance self-check. This check counts requirements tagged 'architectural-guidance' and applies both a maximum bound (>3 triggers re-examination) and a minimum bound (0 on 15+ requirements triggers re-examination).
acceptance_criteria: The completeness report documents the result of the architectural-guidance self-check including the count and both bound checks; a run that omits this check is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, Architectural-guidance self-check: 'Mandatory, runs after requirement derivation. Count the requirements tagged architectural-guidance. Apply both bounds.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Architectural-guidance count exceeding 3 requires re-examination and per-excess justification
description: If the count of requirements tagged 'architectural-guidance' exceeds 3, the pipeline must stop and re-examine each one, asking whether testable conditions can be added to reclassify it as 'specific'. Any requirement that remains 'architectural-guidance' after this check when the count is above 3 requires explicit written justification explaining why it cannot be made specific.
acceptance_criteria: When architectural-guidance count > 3: evidence of re-examination per requirement exists in the run record; each requirement remaining as architectural-guidance after the count exceeds 3 has an explicit justification in the completeness report; no requirement above the 3-threshold remains architectural-guidance without justification.
skill_section: Step 7, architectural-guidance self-check: 'If the count exceeds 3, stop and re-examine each one... A final count above 3 requires an explicit justification per excess requirement explaining why it cannot be made specific.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Zero architectural-guidance on 15+ requirements requires re-examination and written explanation
description: If the total requirement count is 15 or more and the architectural-guidance count is 0, the pipeline must re-examine the requirements for cross-cutting design invariants. A single sentence in the completeness report must explain why no requirement qualified as architectural-guidance, or appropriate requirements must be reclassified.
acceptance_criteria: When total REQ count ≥ 15 and architectural-guidance count = 0: the completeness report contains a sentence explaining why no REQ qualified as architectural-guidance, OR at least one REQ has been reclassified to architectural-guidance; absence of both is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7, architectural-guidance self-check minimum bound: 'If the total requirement count is 15 or more and the architectural-guidance count is 0, re-examine the requirements for cross-cutting design invariants... Write one sentence in the completeness report explaining why no requirement qualified as architectural-guidance, or reclassify the appropriate requirements.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Completeness report must record architectural-guidance count and reclassifications
description: The completeness report (quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md) must record the final architectural-guidance count and any reclassifications made during the self-check. This serves as documentation that the self-check was performed and its outcomes.
acceptance_criteria: quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md contains a section recording: the architectural-guidance count, results of the max-bound check, results of the min-bound check, and any requirements reclassified as part of this process.
skill_section: Step 7, architectural-guidance self-check: 'Record the count and any reclassifications in the completeness report.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Requirement count must not be artificially capped
description: The pipeline must not impose an artificial upper limit on the number of requirements. Requirements must be derived until the project warrants no more — a small utility might have 20, a mature library might have 100+. The goal is completeness.
acceptance_criteria: No run record shows a requirement count artificially limited to a pre-set ceiling; the requirement count reflects the actual number of testable claims found in the project regardless of how large that number is.
skill_section: Step 7: 'Do not cap the requirement count. Derive as many as the project warrants. A small utility might have 20. A mature library might have 100+. The goal is completeness.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Step 7a: every committed subsystem must have at least one traced requirement before artifact generation
description: Step 7a requires re-reading the coverage commitment table from PROGRESS.md and verifying that each deep document listed as 'will cover in Phase 2' has at least one requirement tracing to the subsystem it documents. If gaps exist, requirements must be added before proceeding to artifact generation.
acceptance_criteria: Every subsystem listed as 'will cover' in PROGRESS.md has at least one REQ with a matching trace; no subsystem with a 'will cover' commitment has zero traced requirements when artifact generation begins.
skill_section: Step 7a: 'Re-read the coverage commitment table from PROGRESS.md. For each deep document you committed to covering ("will cover in Phase 2"), verify that at least one requirement traces to the subsystem it documents.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Step 7a: each subsystem must be recorded in PROGRESS.md with REQ IDs or explicit exclusion
description: For each subsystem reviewed in Step 7a, PROGRESS.md must be updated with either: the requirement IDs that cover it, OR an explicit exclusion with rationale, risk acknowledgment, and recommended follow-up. A subsystem with no documentation either way is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: PROGRESS.md contains an entry for each subsystem reviewed in Step 7a; each entry either lists REQ IDs or provides an explicit exclusion with rationale, risk acknowledgment, and follow-up recommendation; missing entries are non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7a: 'For each subsystem, record one of the following in PROGRESS.md: the requirement IDs that cover it, or an explicit exclusion with rationale, risk acknowledgment, and recommended follow-up.'
citation_excerpt:
For each subsystem, record one of the following in PROGRESS.md:
- the requirement IDs that cover it, or
- an explicit exclusion with rationale, risk acknowledgment, and recommended follow-up

title: Deep-documented subsystem with zero requirements after 'will cover' commitment is a process failure
description: A deep-documented subsystem that was committed to covering ('will cover in Phase 2') and has zero mapped requirements at the end of Step 7a is a process failure, not a legitimate scope choice. The pipeline must not proceed to artifact generation until every commitment is satisfied or explicitly converted to a justified exclusion.
acceptance_criteria: The pipeline halts before artifact generation if any 'will cover' subsystem has zero mapped requirements and no documented justified exclusion; the absence of requirements without explicit exclusion is detected and reported as a process failure.
skill_section: Step 7a: 'A deep-documented subsystem with a "will cover" commitment and zero mapped requirements is a process failure, not a legitimate scope choice. Do not proceed to artifact generation until every commitment is satisfied or explicitly converted to a justified exclusion.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Step 7b must execute after Phase E completes (post overview gate, use cases, and span check)
description: Step 7b (code-path reverse traceability audit) must be executed only after Phase E has completed — specifically after the overview validation gate, use case derivation, and acceptance criteria span check have all run. Executing Step 7b before Phase E is complete is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: The run record shows Step 7b execution timestamps after Phase E completion; use cases and overview are finalized before the reverse traceability audit begins.
skill_section: Step 7b: 'Timing: Execute Step 7a and 7b after Phase E completes (i.e., after the overview validation gate, use case derivation, and acceptance criteria span check have all run). The audit depends on finalized requirements AND finalized use cases.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Step 7b reverse traceability audit operates at path/branch/helper granularity, not file level
description: The reverse traceability audit in Step 7b operates at path, branch, and helper granularity — not at file level. The question is not 'does this file map to some requirement?' but 'does this significant branch map to a requirement clause that states what must be preserved here?' File-level coverage at 100% does not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Step 7b audit records identify specific code paths, branches, and helpers — not just files; a run showing only file-level mappings without path/branch/helper analysis does not satisfy Step 7b.
skill_section: Step 7b: 'This operates at path/branch/helper granularity, not file level. File-level coverage was 100% in v1.3.13 and still missed two real bugs. The question is not "does this file map to some requirement?" but "does this significant branch map to a requirement clause..."'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Alternative paths named in requirements must each have an explicit symmetry condition
description: When a requirement mentions fallback or alternative paths (primary vs. degraded mode, negotiated vs. default, sync vs. async), each alternative must have an explicit symmetry condition — a statement of what invariant must hold across both paths. A requirement saying 'the system handles both X and Y' without specifying invariants for each path is incomplete.
acceptance_criteria: Every requirement mentioning multiple paths includes at least one symmetry condition per alternative path stating what invariant must hold; a requirement like 'handles both X and Y' with no per-path invariant is flagged as incomplete in the Step 7b audit.
skill_section: Step 7b, category 1: 'Alternative paths already named in requirements... each alternative must have an explicit symmetry condition — a statement of what invariant must hold across both paths.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Helpers translating public constants to runtime behavior require a helper-specific requirement enumerating expected values
description: If a helper function whitelists, filters, or translates between defined constants and runtime behavior (feature flag gates, codec registry lookups, capability whitelist helpers), it must have a dedicated helper-specific requirement that enumerates the expected preserved or translated values.
acceptance_criteria: Every helper function identified by Step 7b as translating public constants to runtime behavior is covered by at least one REQ that specifically enumerates the expected constant values; a helper with no dedicated requirement is flagged as an orphaned path.
skill_section: Step 7b, category 2: 'Helpers that translate public constants into runtime behavior... must have a helper-specific requirement enumerating the expected preserved/translated values.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Capability-negotiation paths must have requirements covering both negotiated-up and negotiated-down cases
description: Code paths where the system negotiates capabilities with an external peer (protocol version negotiation, feature detection, graceful degradation) must have requirements covering both the negotiated-up path (higher capability accepted) and the negotiated-down path (fallback to lower capability). Coverage of only one direction is insufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Every capability-negotiation code path identified in Step 7b has at least two requirements: one for the negotiated-up case and one for the negotiated-down/fallback case; single-direction coverage is flagged as a gap.
skill_section: Step 7b, category 3: 'Capability-negotiation and fallback logic... must have requirements covering both the negotiated-up and negotiated-down paths.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Functions named in prior BUGS.md or VERSION_HISTORY.md must have explicit re-check evidence in requirements
description: If a previous run found a bug in a specific function (recorded in BUGS.md or VERSION_HISTORY.md), future runs must show explicit re-check evidence for that function as a 'known bug class sentinel' in the requirements. This prevents the 'lost requirement' regression class where previously learned requirements are silently absent.
acceptance_criteria: For every function named in quality/BUGS.md or quality/VERSION_HISTORY.md as a prior bug site, at least one REQ carries a condition explicitly addressing that function's known bug class; absence of such a condition is flagged as a sentinel violation.
skill_section: Step 7b, category 4: 'Functions named in prior BUGS.md, VERSION_HISTORY.md, or spec audit outputs. If a previous run found a bug in a specific function, future runs must show explicit re-check evidence for that function ("known bug class sentinels").'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Prior spec audit outputs must be read before running the Step 7b sentinel check
description: If prior spec audit outputs exist in quality/spec_audits/, the pipeline must read them before running the known bug class sentinel check in Step 7b. Cross-model findings from council reviews are designated as a high-value source of known bug surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: When quality/spec_audits/ contains files, Step 7b reads those files before compiling the sentinel check list; a Step 7b run that ignores existing spec_audits/ files is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7b, category 4: 'If prior spec audit outputs exist in quality/spec_audits/, read them before running the sentinel check — cross-model findings from council reviews are a high-value source of known bug surfaces.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Orphaned paths in Step 7b trigger a coverage gap marker in completeness report
description: When Step 7b identifies a significant code path (in one of the four categories) that has no requirement coverage, that path is classified as an orphaned path and triggers a 'coverage gap' marker in the completeness report. These gaps must be resolved before the completeness report can declare requirements sufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Every orphaned path found in Step 7b appears as a 'coverage gap' entry in quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md; a completeness report that declares requirements sufficient while unresolved orphaned paths are listed is non-conformant.
skill_section: Step 7b: 'Orphaned paths — significant code paths without requirement coverage — trigger a "coverage gap" marker in the completeness report. These gaps must be resolved (by adding requirement conditions or by providing explicit justification) before the completeness report can declare requirements sufficient.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Carry-forward: prior REQUIREMENTS.md must be read and checked for dropped conditions
description: When a prior run's REQUIREMENTS.md exists in the quality directory, the pipeline must read it and check whether any conditions from the prior version were dropped in the current derivation. This carry-forward rule prevents the regression class where previously learned requirements disappear between runs.
acceptance_criteria: When quality/REQUIREMENTS.md from a prior run exists, the current run includes evidence of reading it and comparing conditions; any condition present in the prior version but absent in the current derivation is either re-derived or explicitly documented.
skill_section: Step 7, Carry-forward rule: 'When a prior run's REQUIREMENTS.md exists in the quality directory, the pipeline must read it and check whether any conditions from the prior version were dropped.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Dropped conditions from prior REQUIREMENTS.md must be re-derived or explicitly documented; silent drops are forbidden
description: If the carry-forward check finds conditions from a prior REQUIREMENTS.md that are absent from the current derivation, those conditions must either be re-derived with updated justification (showing they are still relevant) or explicitly documented as no longer relevant with a reason. Silent drops are explicitly forbidden as a direct cause of regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Every condition dropped from a prior REQUIREMENTS.md has either (a) a re-derived version in the current requirements, or (b) an explicit statement in PROGRESS.md explaining why the condition is no longer relevant; any dropped condition without documentation is a non-conformant silent drop.
skill_section: Step 7, Carry-forward rule: 'If conditions were dropped, the pipeline must either: (a) re-derive them with updated justification, or (b) document why the condition is no longer relevant. Silent drops are not permitted — they are a direct cause of regressions where previously learned requirements are lost between runs.'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Create PROGRESS.md after Phase 1 exploration
description: After completing Phase 1 exploration, the agent must create the file `quality/PROGRESS.md`. This file serves as the skill's external memory, persisting state across phases so that context is never lost even if the session is interrupted and resumed.
acceptance_criteria: After completing Phase 1 exploration, create `quality/PROGRESS.md`.

title: Write phase completion entries immediately upon phase completion

title: PROGRESS.md must contain a cumulative BUG tracker table with required columns
description: PROGRESS.md must include a 'Cumulative BUG tracker' section with a table containing columns: #, Source, File:Line, Description, Severity, Closure Status, and Test/Exemption. Every confirmed bug from code review and spec audit must be recorded here with closure status tracked.

citation_excerpt:
## Cumulative BUG tracker
<!-- Every confirmed BUG from code review and spec audit goes here.
     Each entry tracks closure status: regression test reference or explicit exemption.
     The closure verification step reads this list to ensure nothing is orphaned. -->

title: BUG tracker Closure Status must use only defined values
description: Each entry in the cumulative BUG tracker must have a Closure Status drawn from the defined set: 'confirmed open (xfail)', 'TDD verified (FAIL→PASS)', 'fixed (test passes)', or 'exempt (reason)'. No other closure status values are permitted.
acceptance_criteria: Closure Status values: 'confirmed open (xfail)' — bug exists, regression test confirms it, fix pending; 'TDD verified (FAIL→PASS)' — full red-green cycle; 'fixed (test passes)' — bug fixed, regression test now passes, xfail marker removed; 'exempt (reason)' — no regression test possible, reason documented.
skill_section: Section 'Checkpoint: Initialize PROGRESS.md', PROGRESS.md template, BUG tracker Closure Status comment block
citation_excerpt:
language-appropriate term in parentheses, e.g. "confirmed open (@Disabled)"
     - "TDD verified (FAIL→PASS)" — full red-green cycle: test fails on unpatched, passes after fix patch
     - "fixed (test passes)" — bug fixed, regression test now passes, xfail marker removed
     - "exempt (reason)" — no regression test possible, reason documented -->

title: Use language-appropriate term in confirmed-open Closure Status
description: When a bug's Closure Status is 'confirmed open', the language-appropriate annotation marker must be used in parentheses rather than the generic 'xfail' label. The defined equivalents are: Python 'xfail', TypeScript/JS 'test.fails', Go 't.Skip', Java '@Disabled', Rust 'compile_fail' (for compile-time bugs).
acceptance_criteria: Language equivalents: Python 'xfail', TypeScript/JS 'test.fails', Go 't.Skip', Java '@Disabled', Rust 'compile_fail' (for compile-time bugs). Use the language-appropriate term in parentheses, e.g. 'confirmed open (@Disabled)'.
skill_section: Section 'Checkpoint: Initialize PROGRESS.md', PROGRESS.md template, BUG tracker Closure Status comment block
```

title: At least 3 Open Exploration Findings must trace behavior across 2+ functions
description: At least 3 findings in the Open Exploration Findings section must trace a behavior across 2 or more functions or 2 concrete code locations. A list of isolated single-function suspicions is not sufficient depth.
acceptance_criteria: At least 3 [findings] must trace a behavior across 2+ functions. ... A list of isolated single-function suspicions is not sufficient depth.
skill_section: Section 'Write exploration findings to disk', gate check 5
citation_excerpt:
5. **Open-exploration depth check:** At least 3 findings in `## Open Exploration Findings` must trace a behavior across 2 or more functions or 2 concrete code locations. A list of isolated single-function suspicions is not sufficient depth.
6. A section titled **exactly** `## Quality Risks` exists and contains at least 5 domain-driven failure scenarios ranked by priority. Each scenario must: (a) name a specific function, file, and line, (b) describe a domain-specific edge case or failure mode, and (c) explain why the code produces wrong behavior. These must come from domain knowledge about what goes wrong in systems like this one — not from structural analysis of the code alone. A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has (things the code does RIGHT) does not satisfy this gate. A section that lists risky modules without specific failure scenarios does not satisfy this gate. A section that concludes the library is mature and unlikely to have basic bugs does not satisfy this gate.
7. A section titled **exactly** `## Pattern Applicability Matrix` exists and evaluates all six patterns from `exploration_patterns.md`, marking each as `FULL` or `SKIP` with target modules and codebase-specific rationale.
8. Between 3 and 4 patterns (inclusive) are marked `FULL` in the applicability matrix.
9. There are between 3 and 4 sections (inclusive) whose titles begin with `## Pattern Deep Dive — `. Each must contain concrete file:line evidence, not just pattern-name placeholders. The count must match the number of `FULL` patterns in the matrix.
10. **Pattern depth check:** At least 2 of the pattern deep-dive sections must trace a code path across 2 or more functions. A section that says "function X at file:line has a gap" is a surface finding. A section that says "function X at file:line calls function Y at file:line, which does A but not B; compare with function Z which does both" is a depth finding.
11. A section titled **exactly** `## Candidate Bugs for Phase 2` exists and contains at least 4 prioritized bug hypotheses with file:line references, the stage that surfaced each one (open exploration, quality risks, or pattern), and what the code review should look for.
12. **Ensemble balance check:** At least 2 candidate bugs must originate from open exploration or quality risks, and at least 1 must originate from or be materially strengthened by a pattern deep dive. This ensures both domain-knowledge and structural-analysis findings flow into Phase 2.

acceptance_criteria: The Derived Requirements section contains at least one REQ-NNN with specific file paths and function names — not abstract subsystem descriptions.
skill_section: Section 'Write exploration findings to disk', gate check 3
citation_excerpt:
3. The Derived Requirements section contains at least one REQ-NNN with specific file paths and function names — not abstract subsystem descriptions.
4. A section titled **exactly** `## Open Exploration Findings` exists and contains at least 8 concrete bug hypotheses or suspicious findings, each with a file path and line number. These must come from domain-driven investigation, not just from applying patterns. At least 4 must reference different modules or subsystems.
5. **Open-exploration depth check:** At least 3 findings in `## Open Exploration Findings` must trace a behavior across 2 or more functions or 2 concrete code locations. A list of isolated single-function suspicions is not sufficient depth.
6. A section titled **exactly** `## Quality Risks` exists and contains at least 5 domain-driven failure scenarios ranked by priority. Each scenario must: (a) name a specific function, file, and line, (b) describe a domain-specific edge case or failure mode, and (c) explain why the code produces wrong behavior. These must come from domain knowledge about what goes wrong in systems like this one — not from structural analysis of the code alone. A section that lists defensive patterns the code already has (things the code does RIGHT) does not satisfy this gate. A section that lists risky modules without specific failure scenarios does not satisfy this gate. A section that concludes the library is mature and unlikely to have basic bugs does not satisfy this gate.
7. A section titled **exactly** `## Pattern Applicability Matrix` exists and evaluates all six patterns from `exploration_patterns.md`, marking each as `FULL` or `SKIP` with target modules and codebase-specific rationale.
8. Between 3 and 4 patterns (inclusive) are marked `FULL` in the applicability matrix.
9. There are between 3 and 4 sections (inclusive) whose titles begin with `## Pattern Deep Dive — `. Each must contain concrete file:line evidence, not just pattern-name placeholders. The count must match the number of `FULL` patterns in the matrix.
10. **Pattern depth check:** At least 2 of the pattern deep-dive sections must trace a code path across 2 or more functions. A section that says "function X at file:line has a gap" is a surface finding. A section that says "function X at file:line calls function Y at file:line, which does A but not B; compare with function Z which does both" is a depth finding.
11. A section titled **exactly** `## Candidate Bugs for Phase 2` exists and contains at least 4 prioritized bug hypotheses with file:line references, the stage that surfaced each one (open exploration, quality risks, or pattern), and what the code review should look for.
12. **Ensemble balance check:** At least 2 candidate bugs must originate from open exploration or quality risks, and at least 1 must originate from or be materially strengthened by a pattern deep dive. This ensures both domain-knowledge and structural-analysis findings flow into Phase 2.

```
title: QUALITY.md Must Contain Exactly Six Named Sections

acceptance_criteria: The constitution has six sections: (1) Purpose, (2) Coverage Targets, (3) Coverage Theater Prevention, (4) Fitness-to-Purpose Scenarios, (5) AI Session Quality Discipline, (6) The Human Gate.

title: Purpose Section Must Ground Quality in Deming, Juran, and Crosby
description: The Purpose section of QUALITY.md must explicitly ground its quality definitions in all three of the named theorists: Deming (built in, not inspected), Juran (fitness for use), and Crosby (quality is free). Generic quality statements that omit these foundations are non-compliant.
acceptance_criteria: Purpose — What quality means for this project, grounded in Deming (built in, not inspected), Juran (fitness for use), Crosby (quality is free).
skill_section: SKILL.md section 'File 1: quality/QUALITY.md — Quality Constitution', item 1 in the numbered list
citation_excerpt:
1. **Purpose** — What quality means for this project, grounded in Deming (built in, not inspected), Juran (fitness for use), Crosby (quality is free). Apply these specifically: what does "fitness for use" mean for *this system*? Not "tests pass" but the actual operational requirement.
2. **Coverage Targets** — Table mapping each subsystem to a target with rationale referencing real risks. Every target must have a "why" grounded in a specific scenario — without it, a future AI session will argue the target down.
3. **Coverage Theater Prevention** — Project-specific examples of fake tests, derived from what you saw during exploration. (Why: AI-generated tests often pad coverage numbers without catching real bugs — asserting that imports worked, that dicts have keys, or that mocks return what they were configured to return. Calling this out explicitly stops the pattern.)
4. **Fitness-to-Purpose Scenarios** — The heart of it. Each scenario documents a realistic failure mode with code references and verification method. Aim for 2+ scenarios per core module — typically 8–10 total for a medium project, fewer for small projects, more for complex ones. Quality matters more than count: a scenario that precisely captures a real architectural vulnerability is worth more than three generic ones. (Why: Coverage percentages tell you how much code ran, not whether it ran correctly. A system can have 95% coverage and still lose records silently. Fitness scenarios define what "working correctly" actually means in concrete terms that no one can argue down.)
5. **AI Session Quality Discipline** — Rules every AI session must follow
6. **The Human Gate** — Things requiring human judgment

title: Import Pattern Matches Existing Project Tests
description: All tests in the functional test file must use the same import pattern as existing project tests. The author must read how existing tests import project modules and replicate that exact pattern; getting this wrong causes every test to fail.
acceptance_criteria: Match the existing import pattern exactly. Read how existing tests import project modules and do the same thing. Getting this wrong means every test fails.
skill_section: File 2: Functional Tests section, key rule 'Match the existing import pattern exactly'
citation_excerpt:
- **Match the existing import pattern exactly.** Read how existing tests import project modules and do the same thing. Getting this wrong means every test fails.
- **Read every function's signature before calling it.** Read the actual `def` line — parameter names, types, defaults. Read real data files from the project to understand data shapes. Do not guess at function parameters or fixture structures.
- **No placeholder tests.** Every test must import and call actual project code. If the body is `pass` or the assertion is trivial (`assert isinstance(x, list)`), delete it. A test that doesn't exercise project code inflates the count and creates false confidence.
- **Test count heuristic** = (testable spec sections) + (QUALITY.md scenarios) + (defensive patterns). For a medium project (5–15 source files), this typically yields 35–50 tests. Significantly fewer suggests missed requirements or shallow exploration. Significantly more is fine if every test is meaningful — don't pad to hit a number.
- **Cross-variant heuristic: ~30%** — If the project handles multiple input types, aim for roughly 30% of tests parametrized across all variants. The exact percentage matters less than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants.
- **Test outcomes, not mechanisms** — Assert what the spec says should happen, not how the code implements it.
- **Use schema-valid mutations** — Boundary tests must use values the schema accepts (from Step 5b), not values it rejects.

title: Pass 1 flags uncertain findings as QUESTION not BUG
description: When the reviewer is unsure whether something is a defect, Pass 1 must classify the finding as QUESTION rather than BUG. Promoting an uncertain finding to BUG is prohibited.
acceptance_criteria: If unsure: flag as QUESTION, not BUG.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 1 mandatory guardrails bullet 3
citation_excerpt:
- If unsure: flag as QUESTION, not BUG
- Grep before claiming missing
- Do NOT suggest style changes — only flag things that are incorrect

title: Pass 1 must grep before claiming something is missing
description: Before recording any finding that asserts a symbol, check, or construct is absent from the code, Pass 1 must perform a grep search to confirm the absence. Assertions of absence without grep verification are not permitted.
acceptance_criteria: Grep before claiming missing.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 1 mandatory guardrails bullet 4
citation_excerpt:
- Grep before claiming missing
- Do NOT suggest style changes — only flag things that are incorrect

title: Pass 1 must not suggest style changes
description: Pass 1 is restricted to flagging things that are incorrect. Style recommendations are explicitly prohibited — only incorrect code may be flagged.
acceptance_criteria: Do NOT suggest style changes — only flag things that are incorrect.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 1 mandatory guardrails bullet 5
citation_excerpt: - Do NOT suggest style changes — only flag things that are incorrect

title: Pass 1 must explicitly address input validation and boundary handling
description: Pass 1 must include a dedicated scrutiny section on input validation and boundary handling, checking every trust boundary where external or caller-supplied data enters the code, including string parsers, enum lookups, and binary-format parsers.
acceptance_criteria: Input validation and boundary handling — check every trust boundary where external or caller-supplied data enters the code. Every string parser, enum lookup, and binary-format parser must reject input that shares a valid prefix with a valid token but contains additional characters.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Minimum required Pass 1 scrutiny areas, item 1
citation_excerpt:
1. **Input validation and boundary handling** — check every trust boundary where external or caller-supplied data enters the code. Every string parser, enum lookup, and binary-format parser must reject input that shares a valid prefix with a valid token but contains additional characters.
2. **Resource lifecycle** — allocation, refcount management, error-path cleanup, lock release on failure, file descriptor/handle lifetime. Every function that acquires a reference or resource must release it on every early-exit path, or must complete all validation before acquiring the resource.
3. **Concurrency and state management** — lock ordering, atomic operation correctness (every atomic modification of a shared state word must use read-modify-write semantics and preserve bits outside the intended modification), state machine completeness (all states handled at all consumers).
4. **Unit and encoding correctness** — every field read from hardware, protocol structures, or user input that has defined units must be converted correctly before use in calculations or comparisons.
5. **Enumeration and whitelist completeness** — when a function uses a `switch`/`case`, `match`, if-else chain, or any branching construct to handle a set of named constants (feature bits, enum values, event types, command codes, permission flags), perform a **mechanical enumeration check**:

title: Pass 1 must perform two-list enumeration check for switch/case constructs
description: Pass 1 must apply a mechanical two-list enumeration check when a function uses switch/case, match, or if-else chains on named constants. List A extracts every branch/case label from code with exact line numbers; List B lists every constant from the spec that should be handled; a diff marks each as FOUND or NOT IN CODE.
acceptance_criteria: Enumeration and whitelist completeness — perform a mechanical enumeration check: (a) List A: extract every branch/case label actually present in the code with its exact line number. (b) List B: list every constant defined in the relevant header, enum, or spec that should be handled. (c) Diff: compare the two lists. For each constant in List B, mark it as 'FOUND (line NNN)' or 'NOT IN CODE.'
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Minimum required Pass 1 scrutiny areas, item 5
citation_excerpt: (b) **List B (spec extraction):** List every constant defined in the relevant header, enum, or spec that *should* be handled.

title: Pass 1 List A must be extracted from code only with per-label line numbers
description: When performing the enumeration check, the code-side list (List A) must be extracted from the code only — it must not be copied from REQUIREMENTS.md, CONTRACTS.md, or any other generated artifact. If a case label cannot be cited with a line number, it is not present.
acceptance_criteria: Extract this list from the code only — do not copy from REQUIREMENTS.md, CONTRACTS.md, or any other generated artifact. If you cannot cite a line number for a case label, it is not present.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Minimum required Pass 1 scrutiny areas, item 5(a)
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Do not assert whitelist completeness without performing two-list comparison
description: Pass 1 is prohibited from asserting that a whitelist 'covers all values' or 'preserves supported bits' without performing the two-list enumeration comparison. Making such claims without the mechanical check is the specific hallucination pattern this rule prevents.
acceptance_criteria: Do not assert that a whitelist 'covers all values' or 'preserves supported bits' without performing this two-list comparison.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 1 scrutiny area 5, anti-hallucination note
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Five scrutiny areas must appear as labeled subsections in Pass 1 report
description: All five minimum required scrutiny areas must appear as explicitly labeled subsections in the Pass 1 report. Omitting or combining scrutiny areas is not permitted.
acceptance_criteria: These five areas must appear as labeled subsections in the Pass 1 report.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, paragraph after Pass 1 scrutiny areas list
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 1 must explicitly state if project has no meaningful concurrency
description: If the project under review has no meaningful concurrency, Pass 1 must state this explicitly in the concurrency section and document the reason, rather than omitting the section entirely.
acceptance_criteria: If a project has no meaningful concurrency, say so explicitly and document why rather than omitting the section.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, paragraph after Pass 1 scrutiny areas list
citation_excerpt: These five areas must appear as labeled subsections in the Pass 1 report. If a project has no meaningful concurrency, say so explicitly and document why rather than omitting the section. Add project-specific scrutiny areas beyond these five as warranted.

title: Pass 2 must cite at least one code location per requirement
description: Pass 2 must provide at least one code location citation (file:line or file:function) for every requirement it evaluates. The minimum evidence rule prohibits blanket satisfaction claims without per-requirement code citations.
acceptance_criteria: Pass 2 must cite at least one code location (file:line or file:function) per requirement.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Minimum evidence rule
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 2 blanket satisfaction claims without per-requirement citations are invalid
description: Blanket satisfaction claims such as 'REQ-003 through REQ-012 satisfied by client paths reviewed' without individual per-requirement code citations do not satisfy the Pass 2 minimum evidence rule and must be rejected.
acceptance_criteria: Blanket satisfaction claims like 'REQ-003 through REQ-012 — satisfied by the client paths reviewed during the pass' without per-requirement code citations do not satisfy Pass 2.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Minimum evidence rule
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Each requirement in Pass 2 must have individual SATISFIED/VIOLATED verdict
description: Every requirement evaluated in Pass 2 must appear individually with its own SATISFIED or VIOLATED verdict. Grouping requirements under a single unverified range claim is prohibited.
acceptance_criteria: Each requirement must appear individually with its own SATISFIED/VIOLATED verdict, not as part of an unverified range.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Minimum evidence rule
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 2 group of more than three requirements under single citation is suspicious
description: When two or three requirements are satisfied by the same function, each requirement may be listed under that function's citation, but each must still appear individually. A group of more than three requirements under a single citation signals that verification was superficial.
acceptance_criteria: A group of more than three requirements under a single citation is a sign that the verification was superficial.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Minimum evidence rule
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 2 enumeration completeness claims require mechanical two-list proof
description: When Pass 2 evaluates a requirement involving a whitelist, lookup table, feature-bit set, handler registry, or any claim that 'all X are covered by Y,' the reviewer must perform the two-list enumeration check: extract every item from code with line numbers, extract every item from the spec, and diff them.
acceptance_criteria: When evaluating a requirement that involves a whitelist, lookup table, feature-bit set, handler registry, or any claim of the form 'all X are covered by Y,' the reviewer must perform the two-list enumeration check from Pass 1 scrutiny area 5.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Enumeration completeness claims paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 2 code-side list must be extracted fresh from source not reused
description: In Pass 2's enumeration check, the code-side list must be extracted fresh from the source — it must not be reused from REQUIREMENTS.md, CONTRACTS.md, the code review prompt, or any other generated artifact.
acceptance_criteria: The code-side list must be extracted fresh from the source — do not reuse any list from REQUIREMENTS.md, CONTRACTS.md, the code review prompt, or any other generated artifact.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Enumeration completeness claims paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: If Pass 2 code-side list matches requirements list word-for-word the check must be redone
description: If the code-side enumeration list in Pass 2 matches the requirements list word-for-word, this is a sign the list was copied rather than extracted. In such cases the check must be redone by extracting from the source.
acceptance_criteria: If the code-side list matches the requirements list word-for-word, that is a sign the list was copied rather than extracted, and the check must be redone.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Pass 2 Enumeration completeness claims paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Pass 3 must compare pairs of requirements referencing shared fields or policies
description: Pass 3 must compare pairs of requirements that reference the same field, constant, range, or security policy, verifying that their constraints are mutually consistent. This includes checking whether numeric ranges match bit widths, security policies propagate to all connection types, and validation bounds agree across files.
acceptance_criteria: Compare pairs of requirements that reference the same field, constant, range, or security policy. For each pair, verify that their constraints are mutually consistent.
citation_excerpt: Compare pairs of requirements from `quality/REQUIREMENTS.md` that reference the same field, constant, range, or security policy. For each pair, check whether their constraints are mutually consistent.

title: Source code boundary rule prohibits modifications outside quality/
description: The playbook must never modify files outside the quality/ directory. All source-tree changes — bug fixes, test additions to the project's own test suite — must be expressed as git diff-format patch files saved under quality/patches/.
acceptance_criteria: The playbook must never modify files outside the `quality/` directory. All source-tree changes — bug fixes, test additions to the project's own test suite — must be expressed as `git diff`-format patch files saved under `quality/patches/`.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Source code boundary rule section
citation_excerpt: **Source code boundary rule:** The playbook must never modify files outside the `quality/` directory. All source-tree changes — bug fixes, test additions to the project's own test suite — must be expressed as `git diff`-format patch files saved under `quality/patches/`. This ensures the original source tree remains untouched, patches are reviewable and reversible, and the playbook's findings are cleanly separable from the code it audited.

title: BUGS.md must be generated after all review and audit phases
description: After all review and audit phases complete, the playbook must generate quality/BUGS.md as a consolidated bug report with full reproduction details for each confirmed bug.
acceptance_criteria: After all review and audit phases, generate `quality/BUGS.md` — a consolidated bug report with full reproduction details for each confirmed bug.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, BUGS.md section
citation_excerpt: **Step 1: Read the bug inventory.**

title: BUGS.md entries must include all required narrative fields
description: Every BUGS.md entry must include: bug ID, source (code review or spec audit), file:line, description, severity, minimal reproduction scenario (what input or sequence triggers the bug), expected vs actual behavior, references to the regression test and any proposed fix patch, and spec basis.
acceptance_criteria: For each bug, include: bug ID, source (code review or spec audit), file:line, description, severity, minimal reproduction scenario (what input or sequence triggers the bug), expected vs actual behavior, references to the regression test and any proposed fix patch, and spec basis.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, BUGS.md section
citation_excerpt: **BUGS.md:** After all review and audit phases, generate `quality/BUGS.md` — a consolidated bug report with full reproduction details for each confirmed bug. For each bug, include: bug ID, source (code review or spec audit), file:line, description, severity, minimal reproduction scenario (what input or sequence triggers the bug), expected vs actual behavior, references to the regression test and any proposed fix patch, and **spec basis**.

title: BUG record id must be BUG-NNN zero-padded three-digit sequence
description: Every BUG record written to quality/bugs_manifest.json must carry an id field formatted as BUG-NNN with a zero-padded three-digit sequence number.
acceptance_criteria: `id` — `BUG-NNN` zero-padded three-digit sequence.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, BUGS.md — v1.5.2 BUG record fields section, id field
citation_excerpt:
- `id` — `BUG-NNN` zero-padded three-digit sequence.
- `divergence_description` — one-paragraph summary of the divergence between documented intent and code behavior.
- `documented_intent` — direct quote or close paraphrase of the REQ / spec language.
- `code_behavior` — what the code actually does, with `file:line` references.
- `disposition` — enum from schemas.md §3.2: `code-fix` | `spec-fix` | `upstream-spec-issue` | `mis-read` | `deferred`. Required, non-null. Do NOT invent new values.
- `disposition_rationale` — one paragraph explaining why THIS disposition and not an adjacent one (e.g., why `code-fix` and not `upstream-spec-issue`, or why `spec-fix` and not `mis-read`). Formulaic rationales ("code is wrong because spec says so") fail the Council review.
- `req_id` — singular. The primary REQ that revealed the divergence. If a bug appears to touch multiple REQs, split into one bug per REQ sharing a root cause and cross-link in `disposition_rationale` (schemas.md §8.1). Do NOT smuggle multiple REQ IDs into one entry.
- `proposed_fix` — required unless `disposition == "mis-read"`. Patch-shaped when `fix_type` ∈ {`code`, `both`}; textual redline when `fix_type == "spec"`. For `mis-read` records the field is optional and, when present, documents the re-read (what the playbook misread and how the correct reading was established), not a shipped change.
- `fix_type` — enum from schemas.md §3.4: `code` | `spec` | `both`. The combination of `disposition × fix_type` is constrained by the legal-combinations matrix in §3.4 (enforced by §10 invariant #12). Illegal pairings: `code-fix` × `spec`, `spec-fix` × `code`, `upstream-spec-issue` × `code`, `mis-read` × `both`. Consult the matrix before authoring the record — the gate rejects illegal combinations.

For `mis-read` records the field is optional and, when present, documents the re-read (what the playbook misread and how the correct reading was established), not a shipped change.
- `fix_type` — enum from schemas.md §3.4: `code` | `spec` | `both`. The combination of `disposition × fix_type` is constrained by the legal-combinations matrix in §3.4 (enforced by §10 invariant #12). Illegal pairings: `code-fix` × `spec`, `spec-fix` × `code`, `upstream-spec-issue` × `code`, `mis-read` × `both`. Consult the matrix before authoring the record — the gate rejects illegal combinations.

title: Code-path trace with file:line references is sufficient to confirm a bug
description: A code-path trace demonstrating a specific behavioral violation with file:line references is sufficient evidence to confirm a bug. Executed request-level evidence, a running test, or integration-level reproduction are not required to promote a finding from candidate to confirmed.
acceptance_criteria: A code-path trace showing function A calls function B which does X but should do Y, with file:line references — sufficient to confirm.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, What counts as sufficient evidence to confirm a bug section
citation_excerpt:
- A code-path trace showing function A calls function B which does X but should do Y, with file:line references — **sufficient to confirm**.
- A missing case/branch identified by enumeration comparison (spec says X should be handled, code has no handler for X) — **sufficient to confirm**.
- A requirement violation identified in Pass 2 where the code demonstrably does not implement the specified behavior — **sufficient to confirm**.
- A domain-knowledge finding where you can trace from input through specific code to wrong output — **sufficient to confirm**.

title: BUG-NNN regression test patch is mandatory for every confirmed bug
description: For each confirmed bug, a regression test patch at quality/patches/BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch must be generated. This patch is mandatory, not optional. A confirmed bug without a regression test patch is incomplete and will cause quality_gate.py to FAIL.
acceptance_criteria: `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch` — a `git diff` that adds a test demonstrating the bug. This patch is mandatory, not optional. A confirmed bug without a regression-test patch is incomplete and will cause `quality_gate.py` to FAIL.

citation_excerpt:

A confirmed bug without a regression-test patch is incomplete and will cause `quality_gate.py` to FAIL. Generate this patch immediately after confirming the bug, before moving to the next bug.
- `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch` (optional but strongly encouraged) — a `git diff` with the proposed fix. For bugs where the fix is a single-line or few-line change (e.g., adding a case label, fixing an argument), generate the fix patch — these are low-effort and high-value.

3. **Compile check:** After applying both patches, run the project's build/compile command (e.g., `go build ./...`, `mvn compile`, `cargo check`, `tsc --noEmit`). Must succeed.

title: Every confirmed bug must have fix patch or explicit justification for its absence
description: Every confirmed bug must have either a quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch that passes the validation gate, or an explicit justification in BUGS.md explaining why no fix patch is provided. A bug with a regression test but no fix patch and no justification is incomplete.
acceptance_criteria: Every confirmed bug must have either: a `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch` that passes the validation gate above, OR an explicit justification in BUGS.md explaining why no fix patch is provided.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Fix patch requirement section
citation_excerpt:
**Fix patch requirement.** Every confirmed bug must have either:
- A `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch` that passes the validation gate above, OR
- An explicit justification in BUGS.md explaining why no fix patch is provided (e.g., "fix requires architectural change beyond patch scope," "multiple valid fix strategies — deferring to maintainer judgment," "bug is in upstream dependency").

title: Regression tests must use the same test framework as functional tests
description: Regression tests written in quality/test_regression.* must use the same test framework as the project's functional tests. If functional tests use pytest, regression tests use pytest; if functional tests use unittest, regression tests use unittest.
acceptance_criteria: Use the same test framework as `test_functional.*` — if functional tests use pytest, regression tests use pytest (with `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)`); if functional tests use unittest, regression tests use unittest (with `@unittest.expectedFailure`).
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, After all three passes section
citation_excerpt: **After all three passes:** Combine findings. Write regression tests in `quality/test_regression.*` that reproduce each confirmed bug. Use the same test framework as `test_functional.*` — if functional tests use pytest, regression tests use pytest (with `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)`); if functional tests use unittest, regression tests use unittest (with `@unittest.expectedFailure`). Report results as a confirmation table (BUG CONFIRMED / FALSE POSITIVE / NEEDS INVESTIGATION). See `references/review_protocols.md` for the full three-pass template and regression test protocol.

title: Skip guards mandatory and must be earliest syntactic guard for the framework
description: Every regression test in quality/test_regression.* must include a skip/xfail guard. The guard must be the earliest syntactic guard for the framework — a decorator or annotation where idiomatic, otherwise the first executable line in the test body.
acceptance_criteria: Every regression test in `quality/test_regression.*` must include a skip/xfail guard so that running the full test suite on unpatched code does not produce unexpected failures. The guard must be the earliest syntactic guard for the framework.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Regression test skip guards (mandatory) section
citation_excerpt: **Regression test skip guards (mandatory).** Every regression test in `quality/test_regression.*` must include a skip/xfail guard so that running the full test suite on unpatched code does not produce unexpected failures. The guard must be the **earliest syntactic guard for the framework** — a decorator or annotation where idiomatic, otherwise the first executable line in the test body. Use the language-appropriate mechanism:

title: Python pytest regression tests use xfail strict=True as decorator above def
description: Python pytest regression tests must use @pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason='BUG-NNN: [description]') placed as a decorator above the def test_...(): line, not inside the function body.
acceptance_criteria: Python (pytest): `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason="BUG-NNN: [description]")` — placed as a decorator above `def test_...():`, not inside the function body.
citation_excerpt:
- **Python (pytest):** `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason="BUG-NNN: [description]")` — decorator above `def test_...():`. When the bug is present: XFAIL (expected). When the bug is fixed but marker not removed: XPASS → strict mode fails, signaling the guard should be removed.
- **Python (unittest):** `@unittest.expectedFailure` — decorator above the test method.
- **Go:** `t.Skip("BUG-NNN: [description] — unskip after applying quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch")` — first line inside the test function. Note: Go's `t.Skip` hides the test (reports SKIP, not FAIL), which is weaker than Python's xfail.
- **Rust:** `#[ignore]` attribute on the test function — the standard "don't run in default suite" mechanism. Use `#[should_panic]` only for panic-shaped bugs.
- **Java (JUnit 5):** `@Disabled("BUG-NNN: [description]")` — annotation above the test method.
- **TypeScript/JavaScript (Jest):** `test.failing("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })`
- **TypeScript/JavaScript (Vitest):** `test.fails("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })`
- **JavaScript (Mocha):** `it.skip("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })` or `this.skip()` inside the test body for conditional skipping.

title: Python unittest regression tests use expectedFailure decorator
description: Python unittest regression tests must use @unittest.expectedFailure as a decorator above the test method.
acceptance_criteria: Python (unittest): `@unittest.expectedFailure` — decorator above the test method.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, Regression test skip guards, Python (unittest) entry
citation_excerpt:
- **Python (unittest):** `@unittest.expectedFailure` — decorator above the test method.
- **Go:** `t.Skip("BUG-NNN: [description] — unskip after applying quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch")` — first line inside the test function. Note: Go's `t.Skip` hides the test entirely (reports SKIP, not FAIL), which is weaker evidence than Python's xfail. This is a known limitation of Go's test primitives.
- **Java (JUnit 5):** `@Disabled("BUG-NNN: [description]")` — annotation above the test method.
- **Rust:** `#[ignore]` attribute on the test function (the standard "don't run in default suite" mechanism). Use `#[should_panic]` only for bugs that manifest as panics; use `compile_fail` doctest annotation only for compile-time bugs.
- **TypeScript/JavaScript (Jest):** `test.failing("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })`
- **TypeScript/JavaScript (Vitest):** `test.fails("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })`
- **JavaScript (Mocha):** `it.skip("BUG-NNN: [description]", () => { ... })` or `this.skip()` inside the test body for conditional skipping.

title: Source-inspection regression tests must execute and must not use run=False

acceptance_criteria: For these tests, use `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)` with execution enabled. Do not use `run=False` unless the test would mutate external state, hang, or require unavailable infrastructure.

title: TDD red phase must remove skip guard and run test against unpatched code and test must fail
description: During the TDD verification cycle, the red phase must remove or disable the skip/xfail guard, then run the test against unpatched code. The test must fail during the red phase. The guard must be re-enabled after recording the result.
acceptance_criteria: Red phase (NEVER SKIPPED): Remove or disable the skip/xfail guard, then run the test against unpatched code. It must fail. Re-enable the guard after recording the result.
skill_section: RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md, TDD red/green interaction with skip guards section, red phase
citation_excerpt:
- **Green phase:** Remove or disable the guard, apply the fix patch, run the test. It must pass. If the fix will be reverted, re-enable the guard. **If no fix patch exists, record `green_phase: "skipped"` — but the red phase must still have run.**
- **After TDD cycle:** The guard remains in the committed regression test file. It is only permanently removed when the fix is merged into the source tree.

title: TDD red phase is mandatory for every confirmed bug
description: The red phase is mandatory for every confirmed bug, even when no fix patch exists. A bug without red-phase evidence is unverified. If the red phase cannot execute, record red_phase: 'error' with explanation.
acceptance_criteria: The red phase is mandatory for every confirmed bug, even when no fix patch exists. If the red phase cannot execute for a documented reason, record `red_phase: 'error'` with an explanation in `notes`.

citation_excerpt:
- **Red phase (NEVER SKIPPED):** Remove or disable the skip/xfail guard, then run the test against unpatched code. It must fail. If the red phase cannot execute for a documented reason (compilation failure, environment unavailable), record `red_phase: "error"` with an explanation in `notes`.

- **After TDD cycle:** The guard remains in the committed regression test file. It is only permanently removed when the fix is merged into the source tree.

3. The first line of each log file is one of: `RED`, `GREEN`, `NOT_RUN`, `ERROR`.
If any check fails, stop and generate the missing logs now using the language-aware test execution commands from the TDD execution enforcement section. title: Log file first line must be status tag RED GREEN NOT_RUN or ERROR
description: Each BUG-NNN.red.log and BUG-NNN.green.log file must have a status tag on the first line: RED (test failed as expected), GREEN (test passed after fix), NOT_RUN (test could not be executed with explanation), or ERROR (test infrastructure failed with explanation). This tag is machine-readable and checked by quality_gate.py.
acceptance_criteria: Each log file's first line must be a status tag: `RED` (test failed as expected), `GREEN` (test passed after fix), `NOT_RUN` (test could not be executed — with explanation), or `ERROR` (test infrastructure failed — with explanation).

citation_excerpt: **TDD execution enforcement (mandatory).** Regression tests must be actually executed during the TDD verification cycle, not just generated as patch files. For every confirmed bug, the red-phase test run must produce a log file at `quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log` capturing the test output. The green-phase (if a fix patch exists) must produce `quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log`. Each log file's first line must be a status tag: `RED` (test failed as expected), `GREEN` (test passed after fix), `NOT_RUN` (test could not be executed — with explanation), or `ERROR` (test infrastructure failed — with explanation).

citation_excerpt: **TDD execution gate.** Before the terminal gate in Phase 5, verify that for every confirmed bug in `quality/BUGS.md`, a corresponding `quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log` exists. Bugs without red-phase logs are incomplete — the regression test patch exists but was never proven to detect the bug. This gate exists because v1.3.45 benchmarking showed that most repos generate regression test patches but never execute them, leaving the TDD verdict unverified.
   ```

- **Every contract must map to at least one requirement.**
- **One requirement per distinct behavioral concern.** Don't merge "thread safety" with "null handling" just because they're in the same class.
- **Do not modify CONTRACTS.md.** Only read it.

title: Multi-UC commands must be split into separate groups with targeted test selectors
description: If a single test command would exercise multiple use cases, it must be split into separate groups using targeted test selectors (e.g., -Dtest=, -run, -k, --tests, -- test_name) so each group isolates 1-2 use cases.
acceptance_criteria: If a single test command (e.g., mvn test, go test ./...) would exercise multiple use cases, split it into separate groups with targeted test selectors (-Dtest=, -run, -k, --tests, -- test_name, etc.) so each group isolates 1–2 UCs.
skill_section: Section 23, 'Per-UC group splitting (mandatory)' paragraph 1
citation_excerpt: **Per-UC group splitting (mandatory).** Each integration test group must map to at most **2 use cases**. A group that maps to 3+ UCs is too coarse — it can't distinguish which use case failed when a test breaks. If a single test command (e.g., `mvn test`, `go test ./...`) would exercise multiple use cases, split it into separate groups with targeted test selectors (`-Dtest=`, `-run`, `-k`, `--tests`, `-- test_name`, etc.) so each group isolates 1–2 UCs. Groups covering all UCs in one undifferentiated command are explicitly prohibited — they provide no diagnostic value when a failure occurs.

title: Groups covering all UCs in one undifferentiated command are prohibited
description: Integration test groups that exercise all use cases through a single undifferentiated command are explicitly prohibited because they cannot distinguish which use case failed when a test breaks.

title: Pre-flight validation must verify each group's test command discovers and runs tests
description: Before finalizing RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md, the protocol author must verify that each group's test command actually discovers and runs tests using the framework's dry-run or list mode.
acceptance_criteria: Before finalizing RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md, verify that each group's test command actually discovers and runs tests. Use the framework's dry-run or list mode to confirm.
skill_section: Section 23, 'Pre-flight command validation (mandatory)' paragraph 1
citation_excerpt:
**Pre-flight command validation (mandatory).** Before finalizing `RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md`, verify that each group's test command actually discovers and runs tests. Use the framework's dry-run or list mode to confirm:
- **Python:** `pytest --collect-only -q <selector>` — must list at least one test
- **Go:** `go test -list "." <package>` — must list at least one test name
- **Java/Kotlin:** `mvn -Dtest=<selector> test -pl <module> --batch-mode -DfailIfNoTests=true`
- **TypeScript (Vitest):** `vitest list <file> --config <config>` — must list at least one test
- **TypeScript (Jest):** `jest --listTests <pattern>` — must list at least one file
- **Rust:** `cargo test <selector> -- --list` — must list at least one test
- **JavaScript (Mocha):** `mocha --dry-run <file>` — must list at least one test

**Infrastructure group definition.** A single `[Infrastructure]` group may cover build validation, race detection, static analysis, and platform compatibility checks without UC mapping. Infrastructure tests verify build toolchain and platform support, not user-observable behavior. Infrastructure groups:
- Do **not** count toward use-case coverage (the UC coverage check ignores them)

```
title: uc_coverage must map every use case to covered_pass, covered_fail, or not_mapped

skill_section: Section 23, uc_coverage values paragraph
citation_excerpt: Valid `result` values: `"pass"`, `"fail"`, `"skipped"`, `"error"`. Valid `recommendation` values: `"SHIP"` (all groups pass), `"FIX BEFORE MERGE"` (failures in non-blocking groups), `"BLOCK"` (failures in critical groups). The `uc_coverage` section maps every use case from REQUIREMENTS.md to one of: `"covered_pass"` (at least one mapped group passed), `"covered_fail"` (groups mapped but all failed), or `"not_mapped"` (no integration test group maps to this use case). The distinction between `"covered_fail"` and `"not_mapped"` matters: the first means the test exists but the code is broken; the second means the test is missing.

title: Field Reference Table must be built before writing quality gates
description: Before writing quality gates, the protocol author must build a Field Reference Table by re-reading each schema file immediately before writing each table row, to avoid field name drift.
acceptance_criteria: Build a Field Reference Table before writing quality gates. This is the most important step for protocol accuracy.
citation_excerpt: ### The Field Reference Table (Required Before Writing Quality Gates)

title: Each schema file must be re-read immediately before writing each Field Reference Table row
description: When building the Field Reference Table, each schema file must be re-read immediately before writing each table row — not relying on earlier reads from the conversation, because field name memory drifts over thousands of tokens.
acceptance_criteria: Re-read each schema file IMMEDIATELY before writing each table row. Do not rely on what you read earlier in the conversation — your memory of field names drifts over thousands of tokens.
citation_excerpt:
1. **Re-read each schema file IMMEDIATELY before writing each table row.** Do not write any row from memory. The file read and the table row must be adjacent — read the file, write the row, read the next file, write the next row. If you read all schemas earlier in the conversation, that doesn't count — you must read them AGAIN here because your memory of field names drifts over thousands of tokens.
2. **Copy field names character-for-character from the file contents.** Do not retype them. `document_id` is not `doc_id`. `sentiment_score` is not `sentiment`. `classification` is not `category`. Even small differences break quality gates.
3. **Include ALL fields from the schema, not just the ones you think are important.** If the schema has 8 required fields, the table has 8 rows. If you wrote fewer rows than the schema has fields, you skipped fields.
4. Write quality gates by copying field names from the completed table.
5. After writing, count fields: if the quality gates mention a field that isn't in the table, you hallucinated it. Remove it.

skill_section: Section 23, 'Build a Field Reference Table before writing quality gates' paragraph

actually verified each check against the file contents.]
```

3. **Include ALL fields from the schema, not just the ones you think are important.** If the schema has 8 required fields, the table has 8 rows. If you wrote fewer rows than the schema has fields, you skipped fields.
4. Write quality gates by copying field names from the completed table.
5. After writing, count fields: if the quality gates mention a field that isn't in the table, you hallucinated it. Remove it.

title: Council of Three uses exactly three independent AI models

> - **Phase 4 (Spec Audit):** Three independent AI auditors check the code against requirements
> - **Phase 5 (Reconciliation):** Close the loop — TDD red-green verification for every bug
> - **Phase 6 (Verify):** Self-check benchmarks validate all generated artifacts
>
> After the numbered phases complete, you can run iteration strategies (gap, unfiltered, parity, adversarial) to find additional bugs — iterations typically add 40-60% more confirmed bugs on top of the baseline.
>
> The playbook works best when you provide documentation alongside the code — specs, API docs, design documents, community documentation. It also gets significantly better results when you run each phase separately rather than all at once.
>
> To get started, say: **"Run the quality playbook on this project."**
