title: Triage must not require runtime evidence for code-path-traced findings
description: Triage may not raise the evidentiary bar above code-path analysis. If a finding includes a code-path trace (function calls, missing branches, wrong return values with file:line references) showing a behavioral violation, triage must confirm it rather than deferring pending runtime evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Triage must not raise the evidentiary bar above code-path analysis. If a finding includes a code-path trace showing a behavioral violation (function calls, missing branches, wrong return values with file:line references), the triage should confirm it.
skill_section: Section 24, triage evidentiary standard paragraph: 'Triage must not raise the evidentiary bar above code-path analysis.'
citation_excerpt: **Triage must not raise the evidentiary bar above code-path analysis.** The triage step confirms or rejects findings — it does not defer them pending runtime evidence. If a finding includes a code-path trace showing a behavioral violation (function calls, missing branches, wrong return values with file:line references), the triage should confirm it. Do not demote code-path-traced findings to "candidate" or "needs runtime verification." The TDD protocol (Phase 5) provides runtime evidence AFTER confirmation. See "What counts as sufficient evidence to confirm a bug" in the BUGS.md section for the full evidentiary standard.

title: Triage must not demote code-path-traced findings to candidate status

citation_excerpt: **Code review vs spec audit conflicts:** If the code review and spec audit disagree on the same finding, the spec audit finding is not automatically correct. Deploy a verification probe — read the specific code location and determine which assessment is accurate. Record the resolution in the BUG tracker. A code review BUG not flagged by any spec auditor is still confirmed but should be verified with a targeted probe before closure.

citation_excerpt:
3. Record the resolution in the Post-Review Reconciliation section:
   ```
   ### Conflicts resolved
   - [finding description]: Code review said [X], spec audit said [Y].
     Verification probe: [what the code actually does].
     Resolution: [BUG CONFIRMED / FALSE POSITIVE / DESIGN CHOICE]. [Explanation.]
   ```
4. If the resolution confirms a BUG, ensure it has a regression test. If the resolution overturns a BUG, clean up the regression test per `review_protocols.md` § "Cleaning up after spec audit reversals."

title: Verification probes must produce executable evidence not prose alone
description: When a triage step confirms or rejects a finding via verification probe, prose reasoning alone is insufficient. The probe must produce a test assertion that mechanically proves the determination.
acceptance_criteria: When the triage step confirms OR rejects a finding via verification probe, prose reasoning alone is not sufficient. The probe must produce a test assertion that mechanically proves the determination.
skill_section: Section 24, verification probes paragraph: 'prose reasoning alone is not sufficient. The probe must produce a test assertion that mechanically proves the determination'
citation_excerpt: **Verification probes must produce executable evidence.** When the triage step confirms OR rejects a finding via verification probe, prose reasoning alone is not sufficient. The probe must produce a test assertion that mechanically proves the determination:

title: Rejection probe must be a passing assertion proving finding is wrong
description: When rejecting a finding as a false positive, the probe must take the form of an assertion that PASSES, proving the finding is wrong. For example, if rejecting a 'missing null check' finding, the assertion must confirm the null check exists at a specific line.
acceptance_criteria: For rejections (finding is false positive): Write an assertion that PASSES, proving the finding is wrong.
citation_excerpt:
**For rejections** (finding is false positive): Write an assertion that PASSES, proving the auditor's claim is wrong:
```python
# Rejection proof: function X does check for null at line 247
assert "if (ptr == NULL)" in source_of("X"), "X has null check at line 247"
```
If you cannot write a passing assertion, **do not reject the finding**. The inability to produce mechanical proof is itself evidence that the finding may be real.

title: Cannot reject a finding without a passing assertion proving it wrong
description: If a reviewer cannot write a passing assertion that proves a rejection, the finding must not be rejected. Instead, it must be escalated to confirmed status or flagged for manual review.
acceptance_criteria: If you cannot write a passing assertion that proves your rejection, do not reject the finding — escalate it to confirmed or flag it for manual review.
citation_excerpt: If you cannot write a passing assertion, **do not reject the finding**. The inability to produce mechanical proof is itself evidence that the finding may be real.

title: Confirmation probe must be a failing assertion proving bug exists
description: When confirming a finding as a real bug, the probe must take the form of an assertion that FAILS (expected-failure), proving the bug exists. For example, confirming a missing switch case requires an assertion that fails because the case is absent.
acceptance_criteria: For confirmations (finding is a real bug): Write an assertion that FAILS (expected-failure), proving the bug exists.
citation_excerpt:
**For confirmations** (finding is a real bug): Write an assertion that FAILS (expected-failure), proving the bug exists:
```python
# Confirmation proof: RING_RESET is not a case label in the whitelist
assert "case VIRTIO_F_RING_RESET:" in source_of("vring_transport_features"), \
    "RING_RESET should be in the switch but is not — cleared by default at line 3527"
```

title: Every assertion must cite an exact line number with content
description: Every verification probe assertion must cite an exact line number for the evidence it references, showing what the line actually contains. Citing a range ('lines 3527-3528') or citing a line without showing its content is insufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Every assertion must cite an exact line number for the evidence it references. Not 'lines 3527-3528' but 'line 3527: `default:`' — showing what the line actually contains. Assertions without line-number citations are insufficient.
skill_section: Section 24, verification probes bullet: 'Every assertion must cite an exact line number for the evidence it references.'
citation_excerpt: - **Every assertion must cite an exact line number** for the evidence it references. Not "lines 3527-3528" but "line 3527: `default:`" — showing what the line actually contains. Assertions without line-number citations are insufficient.

title: Triage evidence must be written to a file on disk
description: Verification probe assertions must appear in a file on disk — either appended to `quality/mechanical/verify.sh` or written to `quality/spec_audits/triage_probes.sh`. Assertions that are only described in triage report prose are not considered executable evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Verification probe assertions must appear in a file on disk — either appended to `quality/mechanical/verify.sh` or written to a dedicated `quality/spec_audits/triage_probes.sh`. Assertions described in the triage report prose but never written to an executable file are not executable evidence.
skill_section: Section 24, triage evidence on disk paragraph: 'Verification probe assertions must appear in a file on disk — either appended to quality/mechanical/verify.sh or written to a dedicated quality/spec_audits/triage_probes.sh.'
citation_excerpt: **Triage evidence must be written to disk.** Verification probe assertions must appear in a file on disk — either appended to `quality/mechanical/verify.sh` or written to a dedicated `quality/spec_audits/triage_probes.sh`. Assertions described in the triage report prose but never written to an executable file are not executable evidence. The gate checks for the existence of probe assertions in the triage output; a triage report that says "verification probe confirms..." without a corresponding assertion in an executable file is non-conformant. This prevents the failure mode where the model narrates what a probe *would* show without actually running it.

title: Read spec basis field for each bug

skill_section: Section item 1, first sub-bullet
citation_excerpt:
1. **Spec-grounded test requirements.** For each bug in `quality/BUGS.md`, the protocol instructs the agent to:
   - Read the bug's **spec basis** field to identify the documentation passage that defines the expected behavior
   - Read the gathered doc (from `docs_gathered/` or the project's own docs) at the cited section
   - Write test assertions using **language from the spec** — variable names, constants, function names, and assertion messages should echo the spec's terminology, not the code's internal naming
   - Include a comment block in each test citing: the requirement ID (from REQUIREMENTS.md), the bug ID (from BUGS.md), and the spec passage (doc name, section, and a ≤15-word quote of the behavioral contract)

title: Red phase: test must fail on unpatched source
description: During the red phase of the TDD cycle, the regression test must be run against unpatched source and it must fail. A passing test in the red phase means the test does not detect the bug.
acceptance_criteria: Red: Run the regression test against unpatched source. It must fail.
skill_section: Section item 2, Red sub-bullet
citation_excerpt:
- **Red:** Run the regression test against unpatched source. It must fail. If it passes, the test doesn't detect the bug — rewrite it using the spec basis to understand what behavior to assert.
   - **Green:** Apply the fix patch (`git apply quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch`), run the same test. It must pass.
   - **Record:** Log both results in the BUG tracker with closure status "TDD verified (FAIL→PASS)".

title: Red phase pass triggers test rewrite

acceptance_criteria: If it passes, the test doesn't detect the bug — rewrite it using the spec basis to understand what behavior to assert.

citation_excerpt: Every row must be fully populated. A bug without a spec doc entry is a code inconsistency, not a spec violation — note this in the table and adjust the upstream reporting language accordingly.

title: JUnit XML generated via framework-native flags per language
description: For each red-green phase, the test must be run with the framework's native JUnit XML output flag: pytest uses --junitxml, Go uses gotestsum --junitxml, Java/Kotlin copies Maven Surefire reports, TypeScript uses jest-junit with JEST_JUNIT_OUTPUT_DIR, Rust uses cargo2junit when available.
acceptance_criteria: For each red-green phase, run the test with the framework's native JUnit XML output flag: Python: pytest --junitxml=quality/results/tdd-red-BUG-NNN.xml; Go: gotestsum --junitxml quality/results/tdd-red-BUG-NNN.xml; Java/Kotlin: copy relevant XML to quality/results/; TypeScript: jest --reporters=jest-junit with JEST_JUNIT_OUTPUT_DIR=quality/results/
skill_section: Section item 6, JUnit XML output paragraph
citation_excerpt:
**JUnit XML output:** Each test group should run with the framework's native JUnit XML reporter:
- Python: `pytest --junitxml=quality/results/integration-group-N.xml`
- Go: `gotestsum --junitxml quality/results/integration-group-N.xml -- -run "TestPattern"`
- Java/Kotlin: Copy Surefire XML reports to `quality/results/`
- TypeScript: `jest --reporters=jest-junit` with `JEST_JUNIT_OUTPUT_DIR=quality/results/`
- Rust: `cargo test 2>&1 | cargo2junit > quality/results/integration-group-N.xml` (if available)

title: tdd-results.json generated by copying template verbatim

acceptance_criteria: Generate quality/results/tdd-results.json by copying the template below verbatim and filling in only the values. Copy-paste the template into your editor first, then fill in the values. Do not write the JSON from memory.

citation_excerpt: **Sidecar JSON (strict schema enforcement):** Generate `quality/results/tdd-results.json` by copying the template below **verbatim** and filling in only the values. Do not invent fields, rename keys, or restructure the schema. The template is the schema — any deviation (extra keys, missing keys, renamed keys, restructured nesting) makes the output non-conformant. Copy-paste the template into your editor first, then fill in the values. Do not write the JSON from memory.

title: tdd-results.json must not invent fields or restructure schema
description: The sidecar JSON must not contain invented fields, renamed keys, or restructured nesting. Any deviation from the template schema makes the output non-conformant.

title: tdd-results.json requires six top-level fields
description: The sidecar JSON must contain all six required top-level fields: schema_version, skill_version, date, project, bugs, and summary. A file missing any of these is non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Required top-level fields: schema_version, skill_version, date, project, bugs, summary. If any required field is missing, the result is non-conformant.

citation_excerpt: **Required top-level fields:** `schema_version`, `skill_version`, `date`, `project`, `recommendation`, `groups`, `summary`, `uc_coverage`. If any of these fields are missing from your output, the result is non-conformant.

title: Each bugs[] entry requires seven per-bug fields
description: Each entry in the bugs array of tdd-results.json must contain all seven required per-bug fields: id, requirement, red_phase, green_phase, verdict, fix_patch_present, and writeup_path. Missing any field makes the entry non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: Required per-bug fields: id, requirement, red_phase, green_phase, verdict, fix_patch_present, writeup_path. If any required field is missing, the result is non-conformant.
citation_excerpt:
- **tdd-results.json:** `schema_version`, `skill_version`, `date`, `project`, `bugs`, `summary`. Per-bug: `id`, `requirement`, `red_phase`, `green_phase`, `verdict`, `fix_patch_present`, `writeup_path`.
- **integration-results.json:** `schema_version`, `skill_version`, `date`, `project`, `recommendation`, `groups`, `summary`, `uc_coverage`. Per-group: `group`, `name`, `use_cases`, `result`.

title: Summary object requires exactly five sub-keys
description: The summary object in tdd-results.json must contain exactly these five keys: total, verified, confirmed_open, red_failed, green_failed. Omitting any of them — especially red_failed or green_failed — makes the summary non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: The summary object must contain exactly these keys: total, verified, confirmed_open, red_failed, green_failed. All five are required — omitting any of them (especially red_failed or green_failed) makes the summary non-conformant.
skill_section: Section item 6, Required summary sub-keys paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Required summary sub-keys:** The `summary` object must contain exactly these keys: `total`, `verified`, `confirmed_open`, `red_failed`, `green_failed`. All five are required — omitting any of them (especially `red_failed` or `green_failed`) makes the summary non-conformant.

title: Patch files use canonical naming pattern
description: Regression test patches must be named BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch and fix patches must be named BUG-NNN-fix.patch. The gate script globs for these exact patterns, so creative variants will not be counted.
acceptance_criteria: Regression test patches must be named BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch. Fix patches must be named BUG-NNN-fix.patch. The gate script globs for these exact patterns — creative variants like BUG-001-regression.patch or BUG-001-test.patch will not be counted.
skill_section: Section item 6, Canonical patch file names paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Canonical patch file names:** Regression test patches must be named `BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch`. Fix patches must be named `BUG-NNN-fix.patch`. The gate script globs for these exact patterns — creative variants like `BUG-001-regression.patch` or `BUG-001-test.patch` will not be counted.

title: Date field uses actual ISO 8601 session date, not placeholder
description: The date field in tdd-results.json must use the actual date of the session as a real ISO 8601 date string (e.g., '2026-04-12'). Placeholder strings and future dates are rejected by the gate.
acceptance_criteria: Use the actual date of this session (e.g., "2026-04-12"), not the template placeholder "YYYY-MM-DD". The gate validates that the date is a real ISO 8601 date and rejects placeholder strings and future dates.
skill_section: Section item 6, Date field paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Date field:** Use the actual date of this session (e.g., `"2026-04-12"`), not the template placeholder `"YYYY-MM-DD"`. The gate validates that the date is a real ISO 8601 date and rejects placeholder strings and future dates.

title: Only enumerated verdict values are valid
description: The verdict field in tdd-results.json must use only one of the five valid enumerated values: 'TDD verified', 'red failed', 'green failed', 'confirmed open', or 'deferred'. No other values are permitted.
acceptance_criteria: Valid verdict values: "TDD verified" (FAIL→PASS), "red failed" (test passed on unpatched code), "green failed" (test still fails after fix), "confirmed open" (red phase ran and confirmed the bug, no fix patch available), "deferred" (TDD cannot execute in this environment).
skill_section: Section item 6, Valid verdict values paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: 'skipped' verdict is deprecated and must not be used
description: The value 'skipped' must not be used as a verdict in tdd-results.json. It is deprecated; 'confirmed open' with red_phase: 'fail' and green_phase: 'skipped' must be used instead.
acceptance_criteria: Do not use "skipped" as a verdict — every confirmed bug must have a red-phase result.
skill_section: Section item 6, Valid verdict values paragraph
citation_excerpt:
**Fix patch requirement.** Every confirmed bug must have either:
- A `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch` that passes the validation gate above, OR
- An explicit justification in BUGS.md explaining why no fix patch is provided (e.g., "fix requires architectural change beyond patch scope," "multiple valid fix strategies — deferring to maintainer judgment," "bug is in upstream dependency").

title: confirmed open verdict requires red_phase fail and green_phase skipped
description: A bug with verdict 'confirmed open' must have red_phase set to 'fail' (confirming the bug exists) and green_phase set to 'skipped' (no fix to apply).
acceptance_criteria: A bug with verdict: "confirmed open" must have red_phase: "fail" (red ran and confirmed the bug) and green_phase: "skipped" (no fix to apply).
skill_section: Section item 6, Valid verdict values paragraph
citation_excerpt:
- `"verdict": "skipped"` — this value is deprecated; use `"confirmed open"` with `red_phase: "fail"` and `green_phase: "skipped"`.
   - Missing `"schema_version"` at the root — every tdd-results.json must include this field.

title: red_phase is never skipped
description: The red_phase field must never be set to 'skipped'. Valid red_phase values are 'fail', 'pass', and 'error'. Only green_phase may be set to 'skipped'.
acceptance_criteria: Valid red_phase/green_phase values: "fail", "pass", "error" (compile/apply failure), "skipped" (green only — red is never skipped).
skill_section: Section item 6, Valid red_phase/green_phase values sentence
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: patch_gate_passed records patch validation gate outcome
description: The patch_gate_passed field must record whether the patch validation gate (apply-check plus compile) succeeded: false if the gate failed and the patch was repaired, null if no fix patch exists.
acceptance_criteria: The patch_gate_passed field records whether the patch validation gate (apply-check + compile) succeeded — false if the gate failed and the patch was repaired, null if no fix patch exists.
skill_section: Section item 6, patch_gate_passed field description

title: Post-write validation mandatory after writing tdd-results.json
description: After writing tdd-results.json, the agent must reopen the file and verify: (1) every required top-level field is present, (2) every required per-bug field is present in each bugs[] entry, (3) all verdict/red_phase/green_phase values use only allowed enum values, (4) no extra undocumented root keys exist. Any failed check requires fixing the file before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: After writing tdd-results.json, reopen the file and verify: (1) every required top-level field is present, (2) every required per-bug field is present in each bugs[] entry, (3) all verdict, red_phase, and green_phase values use only the allowed enum values listed above, (4) no extra undocumented root keys exist. If any check fails, fix the file before proceeding.
skill_section: Section item 6, Post-write validation paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Post-write validation (mandatory).** After writing `tdd-results.json`, reopen the file and verify: (1) every required top-level field is present, (2) every required per-bug field is present in each `bugs[]` entry, (3) all `verdict`, `red_phase`, and `green_phase` values use only the allowed enum values listed above, (4) no extra undocumented root keys exist. If any check fails, fix the file before proceeding. This step catches the most common failure mode: the agent paraphrases the schema from memory instead of copying the template, producing plausible but non-conformant output.

title: tdd-results.json is mandatory when BUGS.md has confirmed bugs
description: If quality/BUGS.md contains any confirmed bugs, quality/results/tdd-results.json is mandatory — not optional. A run that confirms bugs but produces no tdd-results.json is incomplete and the phase cannot close.
acceptance_criteria: If quality/BUGS.md contains any confirmed bugs, quality/results/tdd-results.json is mandatory — not optional. A run that confirms bugs but produces no tdd-results.json is incomplete — the phase cannot close.
citation_excerpt: If `quality/BUGS.md` contains any confirmed bugs, `quality/results/tdd-results.json` is mandatory. If any bug has a red-phase result, `quality/TDD_TRACEABILITY.md` is also mandatory. Zero-bug repos may omit both files. For repos where TDD cannot execute, tdd-results.json must exist with `verdict: "deferred"` and a `notes` field explaining why.

title: TDD_TRACEABILITY.md mandatory when any bug has red-phase result

acceptance_criteria: Zero-bug repos may omit both files.

title: Deferred verdict with notes when TDD cannot execute
description: For repos where TDD cannot execute (environment blocked, no test infrastructure), the protocol must still generate tdd-results.json with verdict 'deferred' and a notes field explaining why, rather than silently omitting the file.
acceptance_criteria: For repos where TDD cannot execute (environment blocked, no test infrastructure), generate tdd-results.json with verdict: "deferred" and a notes field explaining why (e.g., "environment_blocked: missing workspace Cargo.toml", "no_test_infrastructure: kernel C code without userspace harness").
skill_section: TDD artifact closure gate paragraph, last two sentences
citation_excerpt: **TDD artifact closure gate (mandatory).** If `quality/BUGS.md` contains any confirmed bugs, `quality/results/tdd-results.json` is mandatory — not optional. If any bug has a red-phase result (whether TDD-verified or confirmed-open), `quality/TDD_TRACEABILITY.md` is also mandatory. Zero-bug repos may omit both files. A run that confirms bugs but produces no tdd-results.json is incomplete — the phase cannot close. For repos where TDD cannot execute (environment blocked, no test infrastructure), generate tdd-results.json with `verdict: "deferred"` and a `notes` field explaining why (e.g., `"environment_blocked: missing workspace Cargo.toml"`, `"no_test_infrastructure: kernel C code without userspace harness"`). The deferred verdict makes the gap visible instead of silently omitting the file.

title: Writeup generated for every confirmed bug at quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md
description: After a successful red→green cycle or confirmation without a fix, a self-contained writeup must be generated at quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md for every confirmed bug — both TDD-verified and confirmed-open.
acceptance_criteria: Generate a self-contained writeup at quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md. Generate a writeup at quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md for every confirmed bug — both TDD-verified and confirmed-open.
citation_excerpt:
- **Phase 5 (Reconciliation):** If bugs were confirmed: `quality/results/tdd-results.json` exists, a writeup at `quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md` exists for every confirmed bug, and a red-phase log exists at `quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log` for every confirmed bug.

title: Writeup includes version stamp at top

title: Writeup must contain required sections 1 through 4 plus 6 and 7

citation_excerpt: **Template (sections 1–4, 6, 7 are required in every writeup; add 5 when the depth judgment fires; add 8 when related bugs exist):**

title: Section 5 depth judgment included only when warranted
description: Section 5 (Depth judgment) must be included in the writeup only when expansion is warranted — when a reader would reasonably ask 'why hasn't anyone noticed this?' or 'does this affect all configurations equally?'. It must be omitted for bugs with obvious consequences.
acceptance_criteria: add 5 when the depth judgment fires... assess: is the consequence self-evident from the code and test alone? If a reader would reasonably ask "why hasn't anyone noticed this?"... expand the analysis.

citation_excerpt: 8. **Related issues** *(include only when related bugs exist)* — Other bugs in the same class, if any. Flag them even if they're not confirmed yet. Omit this section if no related issues were identified.

title: Every writeup must contain an inline diff block (gate-enforced)
description: Every bug writeup must contain a fenced ```diff code block with an inline diff. The quality_gate.py script checks for this and will FAIL any writeup that lacks it.
acceptance_criteria: The quality_gate.py script checks that every writeup contains a ```diff block. A writeup without an inline diff will cause the gate to FAIL.

title: Writeup diff must be inline, not a reference to patch file
description: The fix diff in a writeup must be pasted inline inside a fenced ```diff code block within the writeup body. Writing 'see patch file' instead of including the actual diff is not acceptable.
acceptance_criteria: Do not write "see patch file" — paste the actual diff inline in the writeup body, inside a fenced ```diff code block. This is the single most important element of the writeup because it makes the bug actionable for a maintainer reading just the writeup.
skill_section: Section item 7, Inline diff is gate-enforced paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Inline diff is gate-enforced.** The `quality_gate.py` script checks that every writeup contains a ` ```diff ` block. A writeup without an inline diff will cause the gate to FAIL. Do not write "see patch file" — paste the actual diff inline in the writeup body, inside a fenced ` ```diff ` code block. This is the single most important element of the writeup because it makes the bug actionable for a maintainer reading just the writeup.

title: Confirmed-open bugs require proposed fix diff even without patch file
description: Bug writeups for confirmed-open bugs — those without a separate .patch file — must still include a proposed fix as an inline diff in section 6. A writeup that says 'No fix patch is included' is incomplete.

skill_section: Section item 7, Section 6 The fix description, and Writeup generation for all confirmed bugs paragraph
citation_excerpt: **Writeup generation for all confirmed bugs (mandatory).** Generate a writeup at `quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md` for every confirmed bug — both TDD-verified and confirmed-open. Use the numbered section template above (sections 1–8). For confirmed-open bugs, follow the same template including a proposed fix diff in section 6 (the diff is always required even without a separate `.patch` file). The writeup threshold is bug confirmation, not TDD completion. A run with confirmed bugs and no writeups directory is incomplete.

title: writeup_path in sidecar JSON points to per-bug writeup file

title: Run all three passes of the code review protocol
description: Phase 3 requires running the full code review protocol as described in `references/review_protocols.md`, which specifies a three-pass protocol. All three passes must be completed; partial execution does not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: The skill runs all three passes of the code review protocol as described in references/review_protocols.md.
skill_section: SKILL.md Phase 3 section: 'Run the code review protocol (all three passes) as described in File 3.'
citation_excerpt:
1. **Run the Post-Review Reconciliation** as described in `references/requirements_pipeline.md`. Update COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md.
2. **Run closure verification:** For every row in the BUG tracker, verify it has either a regression test reference or an explicit exemption. If any BUG lacks both, write the test or exemption now.
3. **Triage-to-BUGS.md sync gate (mandatory).** Re-read the triage report (`quality/spec_audits/*-triage.md`). For every finding confirmed as a code bug, verify it appears in `quality/BUGS.md`. If BUGS.md does not exist, create it now. If BUGS.md exists but is missing confirmed bugs from the triage, append them. A triage report with confirmed code bugs and no corresponding BUGS.md entries is non-conformant — the phase cannot be marked complete until they are synced. This gate exists because in v1.3.21 benchmarking, javalin's triage confirmed 2 bugs but BUGS.md was never created.
4. **Clean up after spec-audit reversals:** If the spec audit reclassified any code review BUG as a design choice or false positive, remove or relocate the corresponding regression test per `references/review_protocols.md`.
5. **Resolve CR vs spec-audit conflicts:** If the code review and spec audit disagree on the same finding (one says BUG, the other says design choice), deploy a verification probe per `references/spec_audit.md` and record the resolution in the BUG tracker.

title: Incomplete council cannot conclude no confirmed defects for enumeration checks without mechanical proof
description: When the effective council is fewer than 3/3 auditors and the run includes any whitelist, enumeration, dispatch-function, or carried-forward seed checks, the audit may not conclude 'no confirmed defects' for those checks unless executed mechanical proof artifacts are present.

skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 4 — Effective council gating for enumeration checks
citation_excerpt: **Effective council gating for enumeration checks.** If the effective council is less than 3/3 (fewer than three auditors returned usable reports) and the run includes any whitelist/enumeration/dispatch-function checks or any carried-forward seed checks, the audit may not conclude "no confirmed defects" for those checks without executed mechanical proof artifacts. An incomplete council with mechanical verification is acceptable. An incomplete council relying on prose-only validation for code-presence claims is not — escalate to "NEEDS VERIFICATION" and run the mechanical check before closing.

title: Incomplete council with prose-only validation must escalate to NEEDS VERIFICATION

citation_excerpt:

3. Record verdicts in `quality/challenge/BUG-NNN-challenge.md`.
4. Apply verdicts: CONFIRMED bugs proceed normally. DOWNGRADED bugs get their severity adjusted. REJECTED bugs are removed from the BUG tracker and relocated to a "Reviewed and dismissed" appendix in BUGS.md with the challenge reasoning.

title: Executed evidence outranks prose artifacts when contradictions exist
description: Before running the terminal gate, the agent must check for contradictions between executed evidence (mechanical verification artifacts, verification receipt files, regression test results with xfail outcomes, TDD red-phase log files, saved shell command output) and prose artifacts (REQUIREMENTS.md, CONTRACTS.md, code reviews, spec audit triage, BUGS.md). If any contradiction exists, the executed result wins and the contradictory prose artifact must be re-opened and corrected. Specifically, if mechanical-verify.exit contains a non-zero value, PROGRESS.md may not claim 'Mechanical verification: passed' and the terminal gate may not pass.
acceptance_criteria: No prose artifact claims a passing status that contradicts an executed artifact showing failure; if mechanical-verify.exit is non-zero PROGRESS.md does not claim passed mechanical verification and the terminal gate does not pass.
skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 5 — 'Executed evidence outranks narrative artifacts (contradiction gate)'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Enforce version stamp consistency across all artifacts
description: The agent must read the version field from the SKILL.md metadata (using reference file resolution order) and then check every generated artifact: PROGRESS.md's 'Skill version:' field, every '> Generated by' attribution line, every code file header stamp, and every sidecar JSON skill_version field. Every version stamp must match the SKILL.md metadata exactly; a single mismatch is a benchmark failure.
acceptance_criteria: Every version stamp in PROGRESS.md, attribution lines, code file headers, and sidecar JSON skill_version fields exactly matches the version field from SKILL.md metadata.
skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 5 — 'Version stamp consistency check (mandatory)'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Require verify.sh in non-empty mechanical directory

title: Do not create mechanical directory when no dispatch contracts exist

title: Enforce verification receipt gate before terminal gate
description: If quality/mechanical/ exists, the files quality/results/mechanical-verify.log (full stdout/stderr from bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh) and quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit (a single line containing the exit code) must both exist before the terminal gate runs. If either is missing, the agent must run: bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh > quality/results/mechanical-verify.log 2>&1; echo $? > quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit

citation_excerpt:
bash quality/mechanical/verify.sh > quality/results/mechanical-verify.log 2>&1
echo $? > quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit
```

title: Fail terminal gate when mechanical-verify.exit is non-zero
description: If quality/results/mechanical-verify.exit contains a non-zero value, the terminal gate fails. The agent must not proceed until the mechanical mismatch is resolved by fixing the extraction, not by editing verify.sh or the receipt file. PROGRESS.md may not claim 'Mechanical verification: passed' unless mechanical-verify.exit contains 0.
acceptance_criteria: The terminal gate does not pass when mechanical-verify.exit contains a non-zero value; PROGRESS.md does not contain 'Mechanical verification: passed' unless mechanical-verify.exit contains exactly '0'.

title: Enforce TDD Log Closure Gate red.log requirement for all confirmed bugs
description: Before proceeding to the terminal gate, the agent must enumerate all confirmed bug IDs from quality/BUGS.md and verify that quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log exists for every confirmed bug. If any is missing, the agent must stop and generate the missing log using language-aware test execution commands.
acceptance_criteria: quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log exists for every confirmed bug ID listed in quality/BUGS.md before the terminal gate runs.

citation_excerpt:

- If confirmed bugs exist with fix patches: `quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log` exists for each bug that has a `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch`

title: Enforce TDD Log Closure Gate green.log requirement for patched bugs
description: Before the terminal gate, for every confirmed bug that has a quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch file, quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log must also exist. Missing green logs must be generated before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log exists for every confirmed bug that has a corresponding quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch file.

title: Enforce first-line format of TDD log files
description: The first line of each TDD log file (BUG-NNN.red.log and BUG-NNN.green.log) must be exactly one of: RED, GREEN, NOT_RUN, ERROR. Files not beginning with one of these values are non-conformant.
acceptance_criteria: The first line of every quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log and BUG-NNN.green.log is exactly one of: RED, GREEN, NOT_RUN, ERROR.
skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 5 — 'TDD Log Closure Gate (mandatory before terminal gate)' check 3
citation_excerpt:
3. The first line of each log file is one of: `RED`, `GREEN`, `NOT_RUN`, `ERROR`.
If any check fails, stop and generate the missing logs now using the language-aware test execution commands from the TDD execution enforcement section. Do not proceed to the terminal gate with missing TDD logs — a bug with a "TDD verified" verdict in tdd-results.json but no corresponding red-phase log is a contradiction.

title: Require Phase 3 and Phase 4 completion as terminal gate prerequisite
description: The terminal gate may run only if Phase 3 (code review) and Phase 4 (spec audit) are both complete, or explicitly marked skipped with rationale in PROGRESS.md. A zero-bug outcome is valid only if code review and spec audit artifacts exist (quality/code_reviews/ and quality/spec_audits/ directories contain report files) or the phases are explicitly skipped.
acceptance_criteria: The terminal gate runs only when quality/code_reviews/ contains at least one report file and quality/spec_audits/ contains report files, OR Phase 3 and Phase 4 are explicitly marked skipped with rationale in PROGRESS.md.
skill_section: SKILL.md §Phase 5 — 'Terminal gate (mandatory)' — 'Prerequisite check'
citation_excerpt:
- If Phase 3 ran: `quality/code_reviews/` contains at least one `.md` file
- If Phase 4 ran: `quality/spec_audits/` contains a triage file AND individual auditor files
- If Phase 0 or 0b ran: `quality/SEED_CHECKS.md` exists as a standalone file (not inlined in PROGRESS.md)
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/bugs_manifest.json` exists (v1.5.2 — authoritative BUG records per schemas.md §8)
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/results/tdd-results.json` exists
- If confirmed bugs exist: `quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log` exists for every confirmed bug ID in `quality/BUGS.md`
- If confirmed bugs exist with fix patches: `quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log` exists for each bug that has a `quality/patches/BUG-NNN-fix.patch`

title: Require BUGS.md for every completed run regardless of bug count
description: Every completed run must produce quality/BUGS.md. A completed run with no BUGS.md is non-conformant. If code review and spec audit confirmed zero source-code bugs, BUGS.md must be created with a '## Summary' section stating 'No confirmed source-code bugs found' and listing how many candidates were evaluated and eliminated.
acceptance_criteria: quality/BUGS.md exists for every completed run; when no bugs are confirmed it contains a '## Summary' section stating 'No confirmed source-code bugs found' with counts of evaluated and eliminated candidates.

title: Enforce ### BUG-NNN heading format in BUGS.md

acceptance_criteria: Every confirmed bug entry in BUGS.md uses the heading level ### BUG-NNN where NNN is a numeric or severity-prefixed ID; no other heading level or formatting is used for bug entries.

title: Print mandatory BUG tracker count statement to the user
description: As part of the terminal gate, the agent must print the following statement to the user: 'BUG tracker has N entries. N have regression tests, N have exemptions, N are unresolved. Code review confirmed M bugs. Spec audit confirmed K code bugs (L net-new). Expected total: M + L.' This print is mandatory, not optional.

title: Write BUG tracker count statement into PROGRESS.md Terminal Gate section

title: Reconcile BUG tracker count with M + L expected total

title: Enforce artifact file-existence gate for standard required artifacts
description: Before writing the Phase 5 completion checkbox, the agent must verify that every standard required artifact exists as a file on disk: quality/BUGS.md, quality/REQUIREMENTS.md, quality/QUALITY.md, quality/PROGRESS.md, quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md, quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md, quality/formal_docs_manifest.json, quality/requirements_manifest.json, quality/use_cases_manifest.json, and quality/citation_semantic_check.json.
acceptance_criteria: All of the following files exist on disk before Phase 5 is marked complete: quality/BUGS.md, quality/REQUIREMENTS.md, quality/QUALITY.md, quality/PROGRESS.md, quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md, quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md, quality/formal_docs_manifest.json, quality/requirements_manifest.json, quality/use_cases_manifest.json, quality/citation_semantic_check.json.
citation_excerpt:
# Required artifacts exist and are non-empty
for f in quality/QUALITY.md quality/REQUIREMENTS.md quality/CONTRACTS.md \
         quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md \
         quality/PROGRESS.md quality/RUN_CODE_REVIEW.md \
         quality/RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md quality/RUN_SPEC_AUDIT.md; do
    [ -s "$f" ] || echo "FAIL: $f missing or empty"
done

title: Enforce artifact file-existence gate for conditional artifacts
description: The artifact file-existence gate must also verify conditional artifacts: if Phase 3 ran, quality/code_reviews/ must contain at least one .md file; if Phase 4 ran, quality/spec_audits/ must contain a triage file and individual auditor files; if Phase 0 or 0b ran, quality/SEED_CHECKS.md must exist; if confirmed bugs exist, quality/bugs_manifest.json, quality/results/tdd-results.json, and quality/results/BUG-NNN.red.log for every confirmed bug must exist; if confirmed bugs have fix patches, quality/results/BUG-NNN.green.log must exist for each.
acceptance_criteria: All conditional required artifacts exist when their conditions are met: code_reviews .md files when Phase 3 ran, spec_audits triage and auditor files when Phase 4 ran, SEED_CHECKS.md when Phase 0/0b ran, bugs_manifest.json and tdd-results.json and per-bug red.log files when bugs are confirmed, per-bug green.log files when fix patches exist.

title: Validate tdd-results.json required keys after writing
description: After writing quality/results/tdd-results.json, the agent must immediately reopen the file and verify it contains all required keys. Root keys required: schema_version, skill_version, date, project, bugs, summary. Each entry in bugs must have: id, requirement, red_phase, green_phase, verdict, fix_patch_present, writeup_path. The summary object must include confirmed_open. If any key is missing, the agent must add it.
acceptance_criteria: quality/results/tdd-results.json contains root keys schema_version, skill_version, date, project, bugs, summary; each bugs entry contains id, requirement, red_phase, green_phase, verdict, fix_patch_present, writeup_path; summary contains confirmed_open.

- **integration-results.json:** `schema_version`, `skill_version`, `date`, `project`, `recommendation`, `groups`, `summary`, `uc_coverage`. Per-group: `group`, `name`, `use_cases`, `result`.

title: Validate integration-results.json required keys after writing

acceptance_criteria: quality/results/integration-results.json contains all required root keys: schema_version, skill_version, date, project, recommendation, groups, summary, uc_coverage.

title: Run quality_gate.py and fix all FAIL results before Phase 5 completion

If the script reports any FAIL results, the agent must fix each failing check before proceeding. Most common FAILs include: missing quality/patches/BUG-NNN-regression-test.patch files, non-canonical JSON field names like bug_id instead of id, missing confirmed_open in TDD summary, and writeups without inline fix diffs.

citation_excerpt: **Phase 6 execution model: incremental, not monolithic.** Phase 6 runs as a series of independent verification steps, each reading only the file(s) it needs, checking one thing, and writing its result to `quality/results/phase6-verification.log` before moving to the next step. Do NOT load all artifacts into context at once. Do NOT try to hold the full verification checklist in memory while reading artifacts. Each step below is self-contained — read the file, check the condition, append the result, drop the context.

title: Python projects use quality/conftest.py for pytest fixtures

   - Rust: `quality/regression_tests.rs` or a `tests/regression_*.rs` file in the relevant crate
   - Java: `quality/RegressionTest.java`
   - TypeScript: `quality/regression.test.ts`

title: Python prefers inline fixtures over shared fixtures when using tmp_path

- **Java:** A test class with `@BeforeEach`/`@BeforeAll` setup methods, or a shared test utility class.
- **Scala:** A trait mixed into test specs (e.g., `trait FunctionalTestFixtures`), or inline data builders.

- **Go:** Helper functions in the same `_test.go` file or a shared `testutil_test.go`. Use `t.Helper()` for test helpers. Go convention prefers inline test setup over shared fixtures.
- **Rust:** Helper functions in a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block, or a shared `test_utils.rs` module. Use builder patterns for test data.

title: Challenge Gate Is Mandatory Prerequisite for Writeup and Regression Test
description: Every confirmed bug must survive the challenge gate before it is permitted to receive a writeup and a regression test. The gate is not optional or advisory; it is a hard prerequisite that blocks both artifacts until the bug passes.
acceptance_criteria: every confirmed bug must survive [the challenge gate] before receiving a writeup and regression test

title: Challenge Gate Supports Exactly Two Invocation Modes
description: The challenge gate must be invocable in exactly two distinct ways: automatically during a playbook run and manually as a standalone operation. No other invocation modes are defined.
acceptance_criteria: The gate can be invoked two ways: (1) During a playbook run and (2) Standalone
citation_excerpt: The gate can be invoked two ways:

title: Playbook-Run Mode Applies Gate Automatically to Trigger-Matched Bugs
description: When the challenge gate is invoked during a playbook run, it must be applied automatically to all bugs that match defined trigger patterns. The application is not manual; it fires without explicit per-bug invocation.
acceptance_criteria: During a playbook run — automatically applied to bugs matching trigger patterns
citation_excerpt:
1. **During a playbook run** — automatically applied to bugs matching trigger patterns (see below).
2. **Standalone** — pointed at a `quality/` directory from a prior run to challenge specific bugs. Example: `"Read quality/writeups/BUG-042.md and the source code it references. Run the challenge gate on this bug."`

title: Standalone Mode Targets a quality/ Directory from a Prior Run
description: When the challenge gate is invoked in standalone mode, it must be pointed at a quality/ directory produced by a prior run in order to challenge specific bugs. The standalone invocation is scoped to individual bugs within that directory.
acceptance_criteria: Standalone — pointed at a quality/ directory from a prior run to challenge specific bugs
citation_excerpt: 2. **Standalone** — pointed at a `quality/` directory from a prior run to challenge specific bugs. Example: `"Read quality/writeups/BUG-042.md and the source code it references. Run the challenge gate on this bug."`

title: Run exactly two challenge rounds per bug
description: The challenge gate must execute exactly two rounds for each bug under review — not one, not three, but precisely two. This fixed count is a protocol constraint defining the structure of the challenge gate.
acceptance_criteria: For each bug under review, run exactly two rounds.
citation_excerpt: For each bug under review, run exactly two rounds. Each round uses a fresh sub-agent so the challenger has no investment in the finding.

title: Each challenge round uses a fresh sub-agent
description: Each of the two challenge rounds must instantiate a fresh sub-agent rather than reusing the same agent across rounds. The sub-agent must not carry state or prior exposure to the finding from one round to the next, ensuring the challenger has no investment in the finding.
acceptance_criteria: Each round uses a fresh sub-agent so the challenger has no investment in the finding.
citation_excerpt: For each bug under review, run exactly two rounds. Each round uses a fresh sub-agent so the challenger has no investment in the finding.

title: Auto-apply challenge gate to pattern-matching bugs during playbook run
description: During a playbook run, the challenge gate must be automatically applied to any bug that matches one or more of the defined auto-trigger patterns. The automatic application must not require manual invocation by the user.
acceptance_criteria: During a playbook run, automatically apply the challenge gate to any bug matching one or more of these patterns.
citation_excerpt: During a playbook run, automatically apply the challenge gate to any bug matching one or more of these patterns. These patterns are where false positives concentrate:

title: Security-class findings auto-trigger the challenge gate
description: Any bug classified as a security-class finding — including credential leaks, authentication bypasses, and injection vulnerabilities — must automatically trigger the challenge gate. These findings auto-trigger because severity calibration tends to escalate them and the model is incentivized to defend them, producing historically high false-positive rates.
acceptance_criteria: Security-class finding (credential leak, auth bypass, injection) triggers the challenge gate automatically.
skill_section: challenge_gate.md, 'Auto-trigger patterns' table, row 1: 'Security-class finding'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Design-decision comments within 10 lines of cited code auto-trigger challenge gate
description: Any bug where the cited code location contains design-decision comments within 10 lines — including WHY comments, OODA references, TODO-with-explanation, or design decision docs — must automatically trigger the challenge gate. The presence of such comments suggests the developer made a conscious choice that the finding may be mischaracterizing as a defect.
acceptance_criteria: Code contains design-decision comments at the cited location (WHY comments, OODA references, TODO-with-explanation, or design decision docs within 10 lines of the cited code) triggers the challenge gate.
citation_excerpt:
| **Code contains design-decision comments at the cited location** | WHY comments, OODA references, TODO-with-explanation, or design decision docs within 10 lines of the cited code suggest the developer made a conscious choice | BUG-007/008: `// WHY-OODA81: Batch upload uses "default" workspace` |
| **The "expected behavior" has no spec basis** | Bug's spec_basis field says "code inconsistency" rather than citing a spec document, or the requirement was invented by the auditor (Tier 3 / REQ-NNN created during the run) | BUG-041: REQ-019 was created by the auditor, not derived from project docs |
| **Another code path handles the same concern differently** | If text_upload does X but file_upload doesn't, that might be a real inconsistency — or it might be intentional divergence. The challenge sorts out which. | BUG-001/002: text_upload merges source_ids, file_upload overwrites — challenge confirms this is a real bug because text_upload has an explicit fix comment |
| **The finding is about missing functionality rather than incorrect behavior** | "This handler doesn't do X" is often a feature gap, not a bug. The challenge checks whether X was ever promised. | BUG-009/029: batch upload "missing" graph writes that were never part of the batch upload's documented scope |

title: Spec_basis of 'code inconsistency' auto-triggers challenge gate
description: Any bug whose spec_basis field contains 'code inconsistency' rather than a reference to a spec document must automatically trigger the challenge gate. This pattern flags bugs where the expected behavior has no authoritative specification basis.

The challenge sorts out which. | BUG-001/002: text_upload merges source_ids, file_upload overwrites — challenge confirms this is a real bug because text_upload has an explicit fix comment |
| **The finding is about missing functionality rather than incorrect behavior** | "This handler doesn't do X" is often a feature gap, not a bug. The challenge checks whether X was ever promised. | BUG-009/029: batch upload "missing" graph writes that were never part of the batch upload's documented scope |

title: Read bug writeup from canonical path on standalone invocation
description: When the challenge gate is invoked standalone, it must read the bug writeup from the canonical path quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md, where NNN is the bug identifier specified by the user.

citation_excerpt:
1. Reads the specified bug writeup from `quality/writeups/BUG-NNN.md`
2. Reads the source code at the cited file:line (fresh read, not from the writeup)
3. Runs both rounds as described above
4. Writes the verdict to `quality/challenge/BUG-NNN-challenge.md`
5. If the verdict is REJECTED, suggests removing the bug from BUGS.md and tdd-results.json

title: Token budget per bug is approximately two sub-agent calls
description: The challenge gate consumes approximately two sub-agent calls per bug processed — one for Round 1 and one for Round 2. This per-bug cost estimate governs capacity planning for runs of varying size and establishes the baseline against which batching strategies are evaluated.
acceptance_criteria: Each bug costs roughly 2 sub-agent calls
skill_section: Token budget section, paragraph 1
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Batching is recommended when auto-triggered security findings exceed 15
description: When the number of auto-triggered bugs in a run exceeds 15, the challenge gate documentation specifies that batching should be considered rather than running all bugs through both rounds simultaneously. The threshold of greater than 15 auto-triggered findings is the explicit trigger for the batching recommendation.
acceptance_criteria: For runs with many security findings (>15 auto-triggered), consider batching
citation_excerpt: For runs with many security findings (>15 auto-triggered), consider batching: run Round 1 on all triggered bugs first, then only run Round 2 on bugs where Round 1 was ambiguous or where the confidence was low.

title: Batching runs Round 1 on all bugs before selectively running Round 2
description: When batching is applied, Round 1 must be executed across all triggered bugs before Round 2 begins. Round 2 is then run only on the subset of bugs where Round 1 produced an ambiguous result or where the confidence score was low. This sequential, filtered approach concentrates the second-round sub-agent cost on the cases that most need it.
acceptance_criteria: run Round 1 on all triggered bugs first, then only run Round 2 on bugs where Round 1 was ambiguous or where the confidence was low
citation_excerpt: For runs with many security findings (>15 auto-triggered), consider batching: run Round 1 on all triggered bugs first, then only run Round 2 on bugs where Round 1 was ambiguous or where the confidence was low.

title: QUALITY.md Purpose section must contain 2–3 paragraphs grounding quality in three named principles
description: A valid Quality Constitution's Purpose section must contain exactly 2–3 paragraphs that explicitly ground quality in the Deming principle ('quality is built in, not inspected in'), the Juran principle ('fitness for use'), and the Crosby principle ('quality is free'). All three principles must be represented.
acceptance_criteria: 2–3 paragraphs grounding quality in three principles: Deming ('quality is built in, not inspected in'), Juran ('fitness for use'), Crosby ('quality is free')

title: Low Scenario Count Triggers Deeper Exploration
description: When very few scenarios are found, the pass must treat this as a signal of shallow exploration rather than project simplicity, and must re-examine function bodies more carefully before concluding. A very low scenario count alone is not a valid terminal state without evidence of deeper reading.

title: Self-Critique Step Required After Drafting All Scenarios
description: After drafting all scenarios, the producer must perform a self-critique review of each one before the pass is considered complete. This review is not optional — it is a required final step in the scenario drafting workflow.
acceptance_criteria: After drafting all scenarios, review each one and ask the three self-critique questions defined in the section.
citation_excerpt: After drafting all scenarios, review each one and ask:

title: Standards Must Include Numbers, Consequences, and Detection Difficulty to Resist Erosion
description: Each standard's 'why' must be concrete enough that a future AI session would not argue it down. If the rationale can be rationalized away, it must be strengthened by adding specific numbers, cascading consequences, and explicit detection difficulty. A standard that passes self-critique question 1 must therefore be quantified and grounded.
acceptance_criteria: "Would an AI session argue this standard down?" If yes, the "why" isn't concrete enough. Add numbers, consequences, and detection difficulty.
citation_excerpt:
1. **"Would an AI session argue this standard down?"** If yes, the "why" isn't concrete enough. Add numbers, consequences, and detection difficulty.
2. **"Does the 'What happened' read like a vulnerability analysis or an abstract spec?"** If it reads like a spec, rewrite it with specific quantities, cascading consequences, and grounding in actual code.
3. **"Is there a scenario I'm not seeing?"** Think about what a different AI model would flag. Architecture models catch data flow problems. Edge-case models catch boundary conditions. What are you blind to?

title: Scenario 'What Happened' Must Read as Vulnerability Analysis Not Abstract Spec
description: Each scenario's narrative section must read like a vulnerability analysis rather than an abstract specification. If a scenario reads like a spec (generic, unquantified), it must be rewritten to include specific quantities, cascading consequences, and grounding in actual code. This is a measurable quality bar checked during self-critique.
acceptance_criteria: "Does the 'What happened' read like a vulnerability analysis or an abstract spec?" If it reads like a spec, rewrite it with specific quantities, cascading consequences, and grounding in actual code.
citation_excerpt:
2. **"Does the 'What happened' read like a vulnerability analysis or an abstract spec?"** If it reads like a spec, rewrite it with specific quantities, cascading consequences, and grounding in actual code.
3. **"Is there a scenario I'm not seeing?"** Think about what a different AI model would flag. Architecture models catch data flow problems. Edge-case models catch boundary conditions. What are you blind to?

title: Self-Critique Must Check for Missing Scenarios Using Multiple Analysis Perspectives
description: The self-critique step must explicitly ask whether any scenarios are missing by considering what different model archetypes would flag. Specifically, architecture-oriented analysis should surface data flow problems, and edge-case-oriented analysis should surface boundary conditions. A scenario set that has not been stress-tested against these perspectives fails the self-critique.
acceptance_criteria: "Is there a scenario I'm not seeing?" Think about what a different AI model would flag. Architecture models catch data flow problems. Edge-case models catch boundary conditions.
citation_excerpt: 3. **"Is there a scenario I'm not seeing?"** Think about what a different AI model would flag. Architecture models catch data flow problems. Edge-case models catch boundary conditions. What are you blind to?

title: Each scenario's How-to-Verify maps to an automated test
description: Every scenario produced by the skill-derivation pipeline must include a 'How to verify' subsection that maps to at least one automated test in the functional test file. A scenario whose verification section has no corresponding automated test violates this rule.
acceptance_criteria: Each scenario's 'How to verify' section must map to at least one automated test in the functional test file.
skill_section: Critical Rule section, sentence 1
citation_excerpt: Every scenario's "How to verify" must map to at least one test in the functional test file.

title: Non-automatable scenarios must document why
description: When a scenario cannot be covered by an automated test, the scenario record must include an explicit note explaining why automation is not possible. Acceptable reasons include referencing the Human Gate. Omitting the note when no automated test exists is a violation.
acceptance_criteria: If a scenario can't be automated, note why (it may require the Human Gate).
citation_excerpt: Each scenario's "How to verify" section must map to at least one automated test in the functional test file. If a scenario can't be automated, note why (it may require the Human Gate) — but most scenarios should be testable.

title: Majority of scenarios must be automatable
description: Across the full set of scenarios produced for a skill derivation run, most scenarios must have automated tests rather than falling back to Human Gate exemptions. A run where the majority of scenarios are marked non-automatable fails this rule.
acceptance_criteria: Most scenarios should be testable (i.e., the count of scenarios with automated tests must exceed the count of Human-Gate-exempt scenarios).
skill_section: Critical Rule section, sentence 2 ('but most scenarios should be testable')

title: Identify Fixed Bugs via Git History and TODO Comments
description: The skill must surface previously fixed bugs by examining git history, TODO comments, and workaround code. Defensive code that checks for conditions that 'shouldn't happen' is a strong signal of a prior bug or known edge case and must be flagged for scenario generation.
acceptance_criteria: Bugs that were fixed — Git history, TODO comments, workarounds, defensive code that checks for things that 'shouldn't happen'
citation_excerpt:
- **Bugs that were fixed** — Git history, TODO comments, workarounds, defensive code that checks for things that "shouldn't happen"
- **Design decisions** — Comments explaining "why" not just "what." Configuration that could have been hardcoded but isn't. Abstractions that exist for a reason.
- **External data quirks** — Any place the code normalizes, validates, or rejects input from an external system
- **Parsing functions** — Every parser (regex, string splitting, format detection) has failure modes. What happens with malformed input? Empty input? Unexpected types?
- **Boundary conditions** — Zero values, empty strings, maximum ranges, first/last elements, type boundaries

title: Identify Design Decision Comments Explaining Why
description: The skill must identify comments that explain design rationale ('why' not just 'what') as indicators of non-obvious constraints that may define testable requirements. These comments signal decisions that could regress silently.
acceptance_criteria: Design decisions — Comments explaining 'why' not just 'what.'
citation_excerpt:
- **Design decisions** — Comments explaining "why" not just "what." Configuration that could have been hardcoded but isn't. Abstractions that exist for a reason.

title: Scenario must include requirement tag in canonical format

title: What happened field must cite actual function, file, and line
description: The 'What happened' field of each scenario must reference the actual function, file, and line number where the defensive pattern lives. Generic or hypothetical descriptions are not sufficient; the field must anchor the failure mode in a concrete, verifiable code location.

title: What happened field must be framed as vulnerability analysis not fabricated incident
description: The 'What happened' field in each scenario must frame the described failure mode as a vulnerability analysis derived from the code itself, rather than as a fabricated or invented incident. This prevents scenarios from including fictional history that cannot be traced back to actual code behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Frame as a vulnerability analysis, not a fabricated incident.

title: How to verify field must contain a concrete regression-detecting test
description: The 'How to verify' field of each scenario must specify a concrete test that would fail if the documented defensive behavior regressed. Vague or qualitative verification descriptions do not meet this requirement; the test must be specific enough that its failure would be detectable.

title: Produce a boundary test for each defensive pattern
description: The skill instructs that every defensive pattern identified in source code must be converted into a corresponding boundary test. This ensures full traceability from defensive code to test coverage — no defensive pattern should be left without a matching test that exercises its guard path.
acceptance_criteria: Each defensive pattern also maps to a boundary test
citation_excerpt: Each defensive pattern also maps to a boundary test:

title: Req tag format: [Req: inferred — from <guard>() guard]
description: The requirement annotation embedded in every boundary test must use the canonical format '[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard]' (or the language-appropriate casing variant). This format ties the test back to the specific guard function that motivated it and marks the requirement tier as 'inferred'.
acceptance_criteria: "[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X" — this exact bracketed format (with inferred tier and named guard source) must appear in the test annotation across all language templates
citation_excerpt:
"""[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X."""
    # Mutate fixture to trigger the defensive code path
    # Assert the system handles it gracefully
```

title: Python boundary test: req tag in docstring
description: For Python (pytest) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear inside the test function's docstring, following the format '[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X.' so that introspection and test-report tooling can surface the tag.
acceptance_criteria: """[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X.""" — the req tag is placed in the function docstring

"""[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X."""
    # Mutate fixture to trigger the defensive code path
    # Assert the system handles it gracefully
```

title: Java boundary test: req tag in @DisplayName annotation
description: For Java (JUnit 5) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear as the value of the @DisplayName annotation on the test method, using the format '[Req: inferred — from methodName() guard] guards against X'. This makes the tag visible in test reports that render display names.
acceptance_criteria: @DisplayName("[Req: inferred — from methodName() guard] guards against X")

title: Scala boundary test: req tag in comment and test description
description: For Scala (ScalaTest) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear both in a line comment above the test block and be reflected in the human-readable test description string, using the format '// [Req: inferred — from methodName() guard]' and 'defensive pattern: methodName()' should 'guard against X'.
acceptance_criteria: // [Req: inferred — from methodName() guard] ... "defensive pattern: methodName()" should "guard against X" in { ... }

title: TypeScript boundary test: req tag in test name string
description: For TypeScript (Jest) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear as part of the first argument to the test() call — the test name string — using the format '[Req: inferred — from functionName() guard] guards against X'. This makes the tag visible in Jest's standard console and CI output.
acceptance_criteria: test('[Req: inferred — from functionName() guard] guards against X', () => { ... })

title: Go boundary test: req tag in inline comment
description: For Go (testing package) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear as an inline comment inside the test function body, using the format '// [Req: inferred — from FunctionName() guard] guards against X'. Note that Go uses PascalCase for the guard function name in the tag, consistent with Go's exported-identifier convention.
acceptance_criteria: // [Req: inferred — from FunctionName() guard] guards against X

title: Rust boundary test: req tag in inline comment
description: For Rust (cargo test) boundary tests, the requirement annotation must appear as an inline comment inside the test function body, using the format '// [Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X'. The function uses snake_case consistent with Rust naming conventions.
acceptance_criteria: // [Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X

title: Boundary test body must mutate fixture to trigger defensive code path
description: The body of every boundary test must include a step that mutates the test fixture (or constructs an input) specifically to trigger the defensive code path under test. Simply calling the function with a valid fixture does not count — the mutation must force execution of the guard branch.
acceptance_criteria: Mutate fixture to trigger the defensive code path — present in all six language templates as a required test-body step
citation_excerpt:
1. Mutate input to trigger the defensive code path (using a value the schema accepts)
2. Process the mutated input
3. Assert graceful handling — the result is valid despite the edge-case input

title: Boundary test must assert graceful handling of triggered path
description: After triggering the defensive code path, the boundary test must contain an assertion that the system handled the condition gracefully rather than crashing, returning an error, or producing incorrect output. The assertion form varies by language but is required in all templates.
acceptance_criteria: Assert the system handles it gracefully — present in all six language templates (assertNotNull, result should equal defined, expect(result).toBeDefined(), assert!(result.is_ok()), etc.)
