title: Create shared setup file before writing tests
description: When tests use shared fixtures or test data, the setup file must be created before any test code is written. Test frameworks do not auto-discover fixtures from other directories, so the setup must be explicitly created and in place prior to test authoring.
acceptance_criteria: If your tests use shared fixtures or test data, you MUST create the setup file before writing tests.
citation_excerpt: Every test framework has a mechanism for shared setup. If your tests use shared fixtures or test data, you MUST create the setup file before writing tests. Test frameworks do not auto-discover fixtures from other directories.

title: Setup mechanism must follow existing project conventions
description: The test author must identify the framework's setup mechanism (fixtures, @BeforeEach, beforeAll, helper functions, builder patterns, etc.) and follow the conventions already used in the project's existing tests, not invent a new pattern.
acceptance_criteria: Identify your framework's setup mechanism and follow the conventions already used in the project's existing tests.
citation_excerpt: Identify your framework's setup mechanism (fixtures, `@BeforeEach`, `beforeAll`, helper functions, builder patterns, etc.) and follow the conventions already used in the project's existing tests.

title: Every referenced fixture or test helper must be defined
description: Any fixture or test helper that a test references must have a corresponding definition. Tests that depend on shared setup that does not exist will error during setup rather than fail during assertion, producing broken tests that may appear to pass.
acceptance_criteria: Every fixture or test helper referenced must be defined.
skill_section: Section 'Create Test Setup BEFORE Writing Tests', Rule paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Prefer inline test data over shared cross-file fixtures
description: The preferred approach across all languages is to write tests that create their own data inline within each test function. This eliminates cross-file dependencies and reduces the risk of setup errors caused by missing or mislocated fixture files.
acceptance_criteria: Write tests that create their own data inline. This eliminates cross-file dependencies.
citation_excerpt: **Preferred approach across all languages:** Write tests that create their own data inline. This eliminates cross-file dependencies. Create test data directly in each test function using the framework's temporary directory support and literal data structures.

title: Inline test data must use temp directory support and literal data structures
description: When creating test data directly inside each test function, the implementation must use the framework's temporary directory support and literal data structures rather than shared files or external data sources.
acceptance_criteria: Create test data directly in each test function using the framework's temporary directory support and literal data structures.
citation_excerpt: **Preferred approach across all languages:** Write tests that create their own data inline. This eliminates cross-file dependencies. Create test data directly in each test function using the framework's temporary directory support and literal data structures.

title: Run test suite after writing tests and check for setup errors
description: After all tests have been written, the test suite must be executed and the output inspected specifically for setup errors such as fixture-not-found or import failures, not only for assertion failures.
acceptance_criteria: After writing all tests, run the test suite and check for setup errors.
citation_excerpt: **After writing all tests, run the test suite and check for setup errors.** Setup errors (fixture not found, import failures) count as broken tests regardless of how the framework categorizes them.

title: Setup errors count as broken tests regardless of framework categorization
description: Errors that occur during test setup — including fixture-not-found and import failures — must be treated as broken tests even if the test framework categorizes them differently (e.g., as errors rather than failures or as skipped). The framework's categorization does not exempt them from counting as broken.
acceptance_criteria: Setup errors (fixture not found, import failures) count as broken tests regardless of how the framework categorizes them.
citation_excerpt: **After writing all tests, run the test suite and check for setup errors.** Setup errors (fixture not found, import failures) count as broken tests regardless of how the framework categorizes them.

title: Tests Must Import and Call Actual Project Code
description: Every test in the suite must import and call actual project code. A test that does not exercise real project functions or methods is not acceptable — regardless of whether it syntactically passes — because it inflates the test count and creates false confidence without verifying any real behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Every test must import and call actual project code.
skill_section: No Placeholder Tests section, paragraph 1, sentence 1
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Delete or Replace Tests Whose Body Is `pass`
description: A test whose entire body is the `pass` statement is a placeholder and must be deleted or replaced with a real test that exercises project code. Leaving such a test in place is prohibited because it contributes nothing to verification while counting against coverage metrics.
acceptance_criteria: If a test body is `pass`, delete it and write a real test or drop it entirely.
skill_section: No Placeholder Tests section, paragraph 1, sentence 2
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Delete or Replace Tests Whose Only Assertion Is `assert isinstance(errors, list)`
description: A test whose sole assertion is `assert isinstance(errors, list)` — checking only that a return value is a list — is a placeholder. Such tests must be deleted or replaced with real tests that verify meaningful properties of the returned data, not merely its type.
acceptance_criteria: If a test's only assertion is `assert isinstance(errors, list)`, delete it and write a real test or drop it entirely.
skill_section: No Placeholder Tests section, paragraph 1, sentence 2
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Delete or Replace Tests That Check Only Trivial Properties via `hasattr`
description: A test that checks only a trivial structural property such as `assert hasattr(cls, 'validate')` — verifying attribute existence rather than behavior — is a placeholder. Such tests must be deleted or replaced with tests that exercise the attribute or method in a meaningful way.
acceptance_criteria: If a test checks a trivial property like `assert hasattr(cls, 'validate')`, delete it and write a real test or drop it entirely.
citation_excerpt: Every test must import and call actual project code. If a test body is `pass`, or its only assertion is `assert isinstance(errors, list)`, or it checks a trivial property like `assert hasattr(cls, 'validate')`, delete it and write a real test or drop it entirely. A test that doesn't exercise project code is worse than no test — it inflates the count and creates false confidence.

title: Mark Untestable Defensive Patterns with a Comment Instead of a Placeholder
description: When a meaningful test genuinely cannot be written for a defensive code path — for example, because it requires a running server or an external service — the test file must not contain a placeholder test for that path. Instead, the untestable scenario must be documented with a comment stating it is untestable and why.
acceptance_criteria: If you genuinely cannot write a meaningful test for a defensive pattern (e.g., it requires a running server or external service), note it as untestable in a comment rather than writing a placeholder.
citation_excerpt: If you genuinely cannot write a meaningful test for a defensive pattern (e.g., it requires a running server or external service), note it as untestable in a comment rather than writing a placeholder.

title: Read function/method signature before writing tests
description: Before writing a test for any function, the agent must read the function or method signature—not just the name, but every parameter, its type, and default value. This ensures that test calls use the real signature rather than guessed parameter names or types, preventing test failures caused by incorrect calling conventions.
acceptance_criteria: Before writing a single test, read the function/method signature — not just the name, but every parameter, its type, and default value.
citation_excerpt:
1. **Read the function/method signature** — not just the name, but every parameter, its type, and default value.
2. **Read the documentation** — docstrings, Javadoc, TSDoc, ScalaDoc. They often specify return types, exceptions, and edge case behavior.
3. **Read one existing test that calls it** — existing tests show you the exact calling convention, fixture shape, and assertion pattern.
4. **Read real data files** — if the function processes configs, schemas, or data files, read an actual file from the project. Your test fixtures must match this shape exactly.

title: Read function documentation before writing tests
description: Before writing a test for any function, the agent must read the available documentation—docstrings, Javadoc, TSDoc, ScalaDoc, or equivalent. Documentation often specifies return types, exceptions thrown, and edge case behavior that must be reflected in test assertions.
acceptance_criteria: Read the documentation — docstrings, Javadoc, TSDoc, ScalaDoc. They often specify return types, exceptions, and edge case behavior.
citation_excerpt:
2. **Read the documentation** — docstrings, Javadoc, TSDoc, ScalaDoc. They often specify return types, exceptions, and edge case behavior.
3. **Read one existing test that calls it** — existing tests show you the exact calling convention, fixture shape, and assertion pattern.
4. **Read real data files** — if the function processes configs, schemas, or data files, read an actual file from the project. Your test fixtures must match this shape exactly.

title: Read one existing test before writing new tests for a function
description: Before writing tests for a function, the agent must read at least one existing test that calls the same function. Existing tests reveal the exact calling convention, fixture shape, and assertion pattern that must be followed to produce valid tests.
acceptance_criteria: Read one existing test that calls it — existing tests show you the exact calling convention, fixture shape, and assertion pattern.
citation_excerpt:
3. **Read one existing test that calls it** — existing tests show you the exact calling convention, fixture shape, and assertion pattern.
4. **Read real data files** — if the function processes configs, schemas, or data files, read an actual file from the project. Your test fixtures must match this shape exactly.

title: Read real data files when function processes configs or schemas
description: If the function under test processes configuration files, schemas, or data files, the agent must read an actual file from the project before writing tests. Test fixtures must match the shape of real project data files exactly; invented or approximated fixture shapes will cause tests to fail or test incorrect behavior.
acceptance_criteria: If the function processes configs, schemas, or data files, read an actual file from the project. Your test fixtures must match this shape exactly.
citation_excerpt: 4. **Read real data files** — if the function processes configs, schemas, or data files, read an actual file from the project. Your test fixtures must match this shape exactly.

title: Tests must not use guessed function parameters
description: Tests must call functions using their real parameter signatures as found in the source code, not guessed or inferred parameter names. A common failure mode is writing a test call with invented parameters (e.g., `(items, seed, strategy)`) when the real signature is different (e.g., `(config, items_data, limit)`), causing immediate test failure. Reading the actual signature takes negligible time and prevents this entirely.
acceptance_criteria: The agent explores the architecture, understands conceptually what a function does, then writes a test call with guessed parameters. The test fails because the real function takes `(config, items_data, limit)` not `(items, seed, strategy)`. Reading the actual signature takes 5 seconds and prevents this entirely.
citation_excerpt: **Common failure pattern:** The agent explores the architecture, understands conceptually what a function does, then writes a test call with guessed parameters. The test fails because the real function takes `(config, items_data, limit)` not `(items, seed, strategy)`. Reading the actual signature takes 5 seconds and prevents this entirely.

title: Check dependency manifest before using libraries in tests
description: Before relying on any library or framework feature in a test, the agent must check the project's dependency manifest to verify what is available. Dependency manifests include `requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, and `Cargo.toml`. Tests must only use libraries confirmed to be present.
acceptance_criteria: Check the project's dependency manifest (`requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `Cargo.toml`) to verify what's available.
citation_excerpt: **Library version awareness:** Check the project's dependency manifest (`requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `Cargo.toml`) to verify what's available. Use the test framework's skip mechanism for optional dependencies (e.g., `pytest.importorskip()`, `Assumptions.assumeTrue()`, `t.Skip()`, `#[ignore]`, etc.).

title: Use framework skip mechanism for optional dependencies
description: When a test depends on an optional dependency that may not be present in all environments, the agent must use the test framework's built-in skip mechanism rather than letting the test fail with an import or runtime error. Each language and framework provides a standard skip mechanism: `pytest.importorskip()` in Python, `Assumptions.assumeTrue()` in JUnit, `t.Skip()` in Go, `#[ignore]` in Rust, and equivalent mechanisms in other frameworks.
acceptance_criteria: Use the test framework's skip mechanism for optional dependencies (e.g., `pytest.importorskip()`, `Assumptions.assumeTrue()`, `t.Skip()`, `#[ignore]`, etc.).
citation_excerpt: **Library version awareness:** Check the project's dependency manifest (`requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `Cargo.toml`) to verify what's available. Use the test framework's skip mechanism for optional dependencies (e.g., `pytest.importorskip()`, `Assumptions.assumeTrue()`, `t.Skip()`, `#[ignore]`, etc.).

title: Test Must Include Setup Phase
description: Every spec-derived test must contain a setup phase that loads a fixture, creates test data, or configures the system before any execution occurs. This ensures the test establishes the necessary preconditions before exercising the system under test.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should: 1. Set up — Load a fixture, create test data, configure the system
citation_excerpt:
Each test should:
1. **Set up** — Load a fixture, create test data, configure the system
2. **Execute** — Call the function, run the pipeline, make the request
3. **Assert specific properties** the spec requires

title: Test Must Include Execute Phase
description: Every spec-derived test must contain an execution phase that calls the function, runs the pipeline, or makes the request being tested. This ensures the test actually exercises the system under test rather than only performing setup or assertion.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should: 2. Execute — Call the function, run the pipeline, make the request
citation_excerpt:
2. **Execute** — Call the function, run the pipeline, make the request
3. **Assert specific properties** the spec requires

title: Test Must Assert Specific Spec-Required Properties
description: Every spec-derived test must assert specific properties that the spec requires, not merely that execution completed or that a result exists. Vague or trivial assertions do not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should: 3. Assert specific properties the spec requires
citation_excerpt: 3. **Assert specific properties** the spec requires

title: Test Must Include Traceability Annotation Citing Spec Section
description: Every spec-derived test must include a traceability annotation — implemented via docstring, display name, or comment — that cites the spec section the test verifies. The annotation must follow the prescribed format identifying the requirement and expected behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should include a traceability annotation (via docstring, display name, or comment) citing the spec section it verifies, e.g., `[Req: formal — Design Doc §N] X should produce Y`
citation_excerpt: Each test should include a traceability annotation (via docstring, display name, or comment) citing the spec section it verifies, e.g., `[Req: formal — Design Doc §N] X should produce Y`.

title: Each test must include a traceability annotation citing the spec requirement it verifies
description: Every functional test must include a traceability annotation — via test name, display name, or documentation comment — that explicitly states which spec requirement the test verifies. Tests that do not link themselves to a requirement are not traceable and fail this quality criterion.
acceptance_criteria: Test name, display name, or documentation comment says which spec requirement it verifies
citation_excerpt:
- **Traceable** — Test name, display name, or documentation comment says which spec requirement it verifies
- **Specific** — Checks a specific property, not just "something happened"
- **Robust** — Uses real data (fixtures from the actual system), not synthetic data
- **Cross-variant** — If the project handles multiple input types, test all of them
- **Tests at the right layer** — Test the *behavior* you care about. If the requirement is "invalid data doesn't produce wrong output," test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.

title: Each test must assert a specific property, not merely that something happened
description: A functional test must check a specific, named property of the system under test. Assertions that only confirm that some event occurred or that an object was returned — without inspecting the actual values — do not satisfy this criterion. The assertion must be concrete and falsifiable.
acceptance_criteria: Checks a specific property, not just 'something happened'
citation_excerpt:
- **Specific** — Checks a specific property, not just "something happened"
- **Robust** — Uses real data (fixtures from the actual system), not synthetic data
- **Cross-variant** — If the project handles multiple input types, test all of them
- **Tests at the right layer** — Test the *behavior* you care about. If the requirement is "invalid data doesn't produce wrong output," test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.

title: Tests must use real data fixtures from the actual system, not synthetic data
description: Functional tests must be robust by using real data — fixtures drawn from the actual project system — rather than hand-crafted synthetic data. Synthetic fixtures may diverge from real data shapes, masking bugs that only surface with authentic input.
acceptance_criteria: Uses real data (fixtures from the actual system), not synthetic data
citation_excerpt:
- **Robust** — Uses real data (fixtures from the actual system), not synthetic data
- **Cross-variant** — If the project handles multiple input types, test all of them
- **Tests at the right layer** — Test the *behavior* you care about. If the requirement is "invalid data doesn't produce wrong output," test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.

title: When the project handles multiple input types, tests must cover all variants
description: If the project under test accepts or processes multiple input types (variants), the test suite must include tests for every variant. Testing only a single input type while ignoring others leaves silent bugs uncovered in the untested variants.
acceptance_criteria: If the project handles multiple input types, test all of them
citation_excerpt:
- **Cross-variant** — If the project handles multiple input types, test all of them
- **Tests at the right layer** — Test the *behavior* you care about. If the requirement is "invalid data doesn't produce wrong output," test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.

title: Tests must target the behavioral layer the requirement describes, not an underlying mechanism
description: A test must be written at the layer where the requirement is actually expressed. For example, if the requirement is that invalid data does not produce wrong output, the test must assert on the pipeline output — not merely that a schema validator or guard clause rejects the input. Testing the enforcement mechanism instead of the required behavior is insufficient.
acceptance_criteria: Test the behavior you care about. If the requirement is 'invalid data doesn't produce wrong output,' test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.
citation_excerpt: - **Tests at the right layer** — Test the *behavior* you care about. If the requirement is "invalid data doesn't produce wrong output," test the pipeline output — don't just test that the schema validator rejects the input.

title: Aim for 30% cross-variant test coverage
description: When a project handles multiple input types, the test suite should target approximately 30% of tests that exercise all variants. The exact percentage is less important than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants, but 30% serves as a concrete threshold.
acceptance_criteria: Aim for roughly 30% of tests exercising all variants — the exact percentage matters less than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants.
citation_excerpt: If the project handles multiple input types, cross-variant coverage is where silent bugs hide. Aim for roughly 30% of tests exercising all variants — the exact percentage matters less than ensuring every cross-cutting property is tested across all variants.

title: Use parametrization to run assertions across all variants
description: The test suite should use the project's testing framework's parametrization mechanism to run the same assertion logic across all input variants. Examples of supported mechanisms include @pytest.mark.parametrize, @ParameterizedTest, test.each, table-driven tests, or iterating over cases.
acceptance_criteria: Use your framework's parametrization mechanism (e.g., @pytest.mark.parametrize, @ParameterizedTest, test.each, table-driven tests, iterating over cases) to run the same assertion logic across all variants.
citation_excerpt: Use your framework's parametrization mechanism (e.g., `@pytest.mark.parametrize`, `@ParameterizedTest`, `test.each`, table-driven tests, iterating over cases) to run the same assertion logic across all variants.

title: Use explicit loops when parametrization is unavailable
description: When the test framework's parametrization mechanism is not a good fit for the scenario, the same cross-variant coverage must still be achieved by looping explicitly within a single test rather than skipping cross-variant testing.
acceptance_criteria: If parametrization doesn't fit, loop explicitly within a single test.
citation_excerpt: If parametrization doesn't fit, loop explicitly within a single test.

title: Apply cross-variant testing to properties that hold regardless of input type
description: Any test verifying a property that should hold regardless of input type must be implemented as a cross-variant test. Qualifying properties include entity identity, structural properties, required links, temporal fields, and domain-specific semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Any test verifying a property that should hold regardless of input type: entity identity, structural properties, required links, temporal fields, domain-specific semantics.
citation_excerpt: **Which tests should be cross-variant?** Any test verifying a property that *should* hold regardless of input type: entity identity, structural properties, required links, temporal fields, domain-specific semantics.

title: Perform cross-variant audit after writing all tests
description: After the full test suite has been written, a cross-variant audit must be performed by counting cross-variant tests divided by the total number of tests. This audit is a required step, not an optional quality check.
acceptance_criteria: After writing all tests, do a cross-variant audit. Count cross-variant tests divided by total.
citation_excerpt: **After writing all tests, do a cross-variant audit.** Count cross-variant tests divided by total. If below 30%, convert more.

title: Convert tests to cross-variant when ratio is below 30%
description: If the cross-variant audit reveals that the ratio of cross-variant tests to total tests is below 30%, additional single-variant tests must be converted to cross-variant tests until the threshold is met.
acceptance_criteria: If below 30%, convert more.
skill_section: Cross-Variant Testing Strategy section, 'After writing all tests' paragraph
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Avoid Existence-Only Checks
description: A test that finds one correct result in the output does not verify the full set of results. Tests must not rely solely on finding a single matching element; they must also check count or verify comprehensively so that extra, missing, or duplicated results are caught.
acceptance_criteria: Finding one correct result doesn't mean all are correct. Also check count or verify comprehensively.
citation_excerpt:
- **Existence-only checks** — Finding one correct result doesn't mean all are correct. Also check count or verify comprehensively.
- **Presence-only assertions** — Asserting a value exists only proves presence, not correctness. Assert the actual value.
- **Single-variant testing** — Testing one input type and hoping others work. Use parametrization.
- **Positive-only testing** — You must test that invalid input does NOT produce bad output.
- **Incomplete negative assertions** — When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
- **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Avoid Presence-Only Assertions
description: Asserting that a value exists in the output proves only presence, not correctness. Tests must assert the actual value of the property under test, not merely that the property is non-null or non-absent.
acceptance_criteria: Asserting a value exists only proves presence, not correctness. Assert the actual value.
citation_excerpt:
- **Presence-only assertions** — Asserting a value exists only proves presence, not correctness. Assert the actual value.
- **Single-variant testing** — Testing one input type and hoping others work. Use parametrization.
- **Positive-only testing** — You must test that invalid input does NOT produce bad output.
- **Incomplete negative assertions** — When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
- **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Avoid Single-Variant Testing
description: Testing one input type and assuming all other variants behave correctly is an anti-pattern. Tests must exercise all input variants using parametrization (or explicit loops) rather than relying on a single variant to represent the whole.
acceptance_criteria: Testing one input type and hoping others work. Use parametrization.
citation_excerpt:
- **Single-variant testing** — Testing one input type and hoping others work. Use parametrization.
- **Positive-only testing** — You must test that invalid input does NOT produce bad output.
- **Incomplete negative assertions** — When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
- **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Avoid Positive-Only Testing
description: A test suite that only verifies correct inputs produce correct outputs is incomplete. Tests must also verify that invalid input does NOT produce bad output, covering the negative path explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: You must test that invalid input does NOT produce bad output.
citation_excerpt:
- **Positive-only testing** — You must test that invalid input does NOT produce bad output.
- **Incomplete negative assertions** — When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
- **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Avoid Incomplete Negative Assertions
description: When a test verifies that something is rejected or absent, asserting only one consequence is insufficient. Tests must assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one, when testing rejection or negative outcomes.
acceptance_criteria: When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
citation_excerpt:
- **Incomplete negative assertions** — When testing rejection, assert ALL consequences are absent, not just one.
- **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Avoid Catching Exceptions Instead of Checking Output
description: Testing that code throws an exception in a specific way does not verify that the system handles input correctly. Tests must assert on the actual output of the system under test rather than relying on exception-catching as a proxy for correct behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.
citation_excerpt: - **Catching exceptions instead of checking output** — Testing that code crashes in a specific way isn't testing that it handles input correctly. Test the output.

title: Tests must verify spec-defined output, not implementation mechanism
description: When writing tests for a requirement, the test must target what the spec says should happen in the output, not how the implementation achieves it. For example, if the spec says 'invalid data should not appear in output,' the test must assert the output does not contain the invalid data — not that the validation layer rejected it.
acceptance_criteria: Test the spec, not the implementation. The spec says 'invalid data should not appear in output' — not 'validation layer should reject it.'
citation_excerpt: Ask: "What does the *spec* say should happen?" The spec says "invalid data should not appear in output" — not "validation layer should reject it." Test the spec, not the implementation.

title: Testing a mandated mechanism is appropriate when the spec explicitly requires it
description: By exception to the general rule of testing specs rather than implementations, when a spec explicitly mandates a specific mechanism (e.g., 'must fail-fast at the schema layer'), a test that verifies that mechanism is appropriate and valid.
acceptance_criteria: When a spec explicitly mandates a specific mechanism (e.g., 'must fail-fast at the schema layer'), testing that mechanism is appropriate.
citation_excerpt: **Exception:** When a spec explicitly mandates a specific mechanism (e.g., "must fail-fast at the schema layer"), testing that mechanism is appropriate. But this is rare.

title: One fitness-to-purpose test per QUALITY.md scenario
description: The fitness-to-purpose test suite must implement a 1:1 mapping to the scenarios defined in QUALITY.md. Every scenario in QUALITY.md must have exactly one corresponding test; no scenario may be omitted and no extra tests may exist without a corresponding scenario.
acceptance_criteria: For each scenario in QUALITY.md, a test exists. This is a 1:1 mapping.
citation_excerpt:
Fitness Scenarios
    — One test per QUALITY.md scenario (1:1 mapping)
    — Named to match: test_scenario_N_memorable_name (or equivalent convention)

title: Fitness-to-purpose tests include traceability annotation citing the scenario
description: Each fitness-to-purpose test must include a traceability annotation that explicitly cites the corresponding QUALITY.md scenario. The annotation format must follow the pattern shown in the section, e.g., `[Req: formal — QUALITY.md Scenario 1]`.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should include a traceability annotation citing the scenario, e.g., `[Req: formal — QUALITY.md Scenario 1]`.
citation_excerpt: For each scenario in QUALITY.md, write a test. This is a 1:1 mapping. Each test should include a traceability annotation citing the scenario, e.g., `[Req: formal — QUALITY.md Scenario 1]`, and be named to match the scenario's memorable name.

title: Fitness-to-purpose tests are named to match the scenario's memorable name
description: Each fitness-to-purpose test must be named in a way that matches the memorable name of the corresponding QUALITY.md scenario. This naming convention enables traceability and makes it immediately clear which scenario a test covers.
acceptance_criteria: Each test should be named to match the scenario's memorable name.
citation_excerpt: — Named to match: test_scenario_N_memorable_name (or equivalent convention)

title: Boundary Tests Must Achieve 1:1 Ratio With Defensive Patterns
description: The boundary and negative test suite must contain one test per defensive pattern identified in Step 5. If 10 or more defensive patterns were found but fewer than a proportional number of boundary tests were written, additional tests must be added to approach a 1:1 ratio.
acceptance_criteria: If you found 10+ defensive patterns but wrote only 4 boundary tests, go back and write more. Target a 1:1 ratio.
citation_excerpt: If you found 10+ defensive patterns but wrote only 4 boundary tests, go back and write more. Target a 1:1 ratio.

title: Each Boundary Test Includes a Traceability Annotation Citing the Defensive Pattern
description: Every boundary test must include a traceability annotation that cites the specific defensive pattern it exercises, following the format '[Req: inferred — from function_name() guard] guards against X'.

citation_excerpt: The `all` strategy is a runner-level convenience that executes gap → unfiltered → parity → adversarial in order, each as a separate agent session. A single agent session cannot run multiple strategies (context budget), so `all` is implemented by the orchestrator agent or benchmark runner as a loop of iteration calls. If any strategy finds zero new bugs, stop early (diminishing returns).

title: All strategy stops early when a strategy finds zero new bugs

Usage (orchestrator agent): "Run all iterations" — the agent runs gap → unfiltered → parity → adversarial sequentially.

2. **Unfiltered exploration.** Explore the codebase from scratch using pure domain knowledge. No required sections, no pattern applicability matrix, no gate self-check. Instead:
   - Read source code deeply — entry points, hot paths, error handling, edge cases

title: Explicitly consider all comparison sub-types for every parallel group
description: For each parallel group, the model must explicitly consider every comparison sub-type in the checklist — not just the most obvious ones. The section states that explicitly checking each sub-type prevents the strategy from missing structural discrepancies. Skipping sub-types that seem inapplicable without considering them is non-compliant.
acceptance_criteria: Not every sub-type applies to every parallel group — but explicitly considering each one prevents the strategy from only finding 'obvious' discrepancies while missing structural ones.
citation_excerpt: 2. **Pairwise comparison.** For each parallel group, read the code paths side by side and systematically check each comparison sub-type below. Not every sub-type applies to every parallel group — but explicitly considering each one prevents the strategy from only finding "obvious" discrepancies while missing structural ones.

title: Perform resource lifecycle parity check for each parallel group
description: For each parallel group, the model must compare what setup/init does with a resource versus what teardown/reset/cleanup does with the same resource. Every resource acquired in setup must be released in teardown — in the same order, with the same scope. The model must look for resources that setup creates but reset forgets.
acceptance_criteria: Resource lifecycle parity: Compare what setup/init does with a resource vs. what teardown/reset/cleanup does with the same resource. Every resource acquired in setup must be released in teardown — and in the same order, with the same scope.
citation_excerpt:
- **Resource lifecycle parity:** Compare what setup/init does with a resource vs. what teardown/reset/cleanup does with the same resource. Every resource acquired in setup must be released in teardown — and in the same order, with the same scope. Look for resources that setup creates but reset forgets (e.g., a list populated during probe but not drained during reset).
   - **Allocation context parity:** Compare allocation flags, lock context, and interrupt state across parallel paths. If one path allocates with `GFP_KERNEL` (sleepable) but runs under a spinlock that another path doesn't hold, that's a bug. Check: what locks are held? What allocation flags are valid in that context? Do parallel paths agree?
   - **Identifier and index parity:** Compare how parallel paths compute indices, offsets, or identifiers for the same logical entity. If setup uses `queue_index + admin_offset` but reset uses `raw_queue_index`, the mismatch is a bug candidate.
   - **Capability/feature-bit parity:** Compare which feature bits, flags, or capabilities each parallel path checks or sets. If the MSI-X path checks a slow-path vector list but the INTx fallback path doesn't, vectors may be misrouted after fallback.
   - **Error/exception parity:** Compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust than the primary — which is backwards.
   - **Iteration/collection parity:** Compare what collections each path iterates over. If setup iterates over `all_queues` but reset iterates over `active_queues`, resources for inactive queues leak.

   - **Error/exception parity:** Compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust than the primary — which is backwards.

title: Perform capability/feature-bit parity check for each parallel group
description: For each parallel group, the model must compare which feature bits, flags, or capabilities each parallel path checks or sets. If one path checks a feature flag that another path skips, that is a discrepancy warranting investigation.
acceptance_criteria: Capability/feature-bit parity: Compare which feature bits, flags, or capabilities each parallel path checks or sets.
citation_excerpt:
- **Capability/feature-bit parity:** Compare which feature bits, flags, or capabilities each parallel path checks or sets. If the MSI-X path checks a slow-path vector list but the INTx fallback path doesn't, vectors may be misrouted after fallback.
   - **Error/exception parity:** Compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust than the primary — which is backwards.
   - **Iteration/collection parity:** Compare what collections each path iterates over. If setup iterates over `all_queues` but reset iterates over `active_queues`, resources for inactive queues leak.

title: Perform error/exception parity check for each parallel group
description: For each parallel group, the model must compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust — which is backwards and warrants a bug finding.
acceptance_criteria: Error/exception parity: Compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust than the primary — which is backwards.
citation_excerpt:
- **Error/exception parity:** Compare error handling between paths. If the primary path handles an error gracefully but the fallback path lets it propagate, the fallback is less robust than the primary — which is backwards.
   - **Iteration/collection parity:** Compare what collections each path iterates over. If setup iterates over `all_queues` but reset iterates over `active_queues`, resources for inactive queues leak.

title: Perform iteration/collection parity check for each parallel group
description: For each parallel group, the model must compare what collections each path iterates over. If setup iterates over `all_queues` but reset iterates over `active_queues`, resources for inactive queues leak. This sub-type must be explicitly checked.
acceptance_criteria: Iteration/collection parity: Compare what collections each path iterates over. If setup iterates over `all_queues` but reset iterates over `active_queues`, resources for inactive queues leak.

title: Trace both code paths with file:line citations for each discrepancy
description: For each discrepancy found during pairwise comparison, the model must trace both code paths and provide file:line citations for each. A finding that references only one side of the discrepancy, or that omits source citations, does not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: For each discrepancy found, trace both code paths with file:line citations.
citation_excerpt:
- For each suspicious finding, trace the code path across 2+ functions with file:line citations
   - Generate bug hypotheses directly — not "areas to investigate" but "this specific code at file:line produces wrong behavior because [reason]"
   - Write findings to `quality/EXPLORATION_ITER{N}.md` as a flat list of findings, each with file:line references and a bug hypothesis. No structural template required — depth and specificity matter, not section formatting.
   - Minimum: 10 concrete findings with file:line references, at least 5 of which trace code paths across 2+ functions

title: Determine intentionality of each discrepancy found
description: For each discrepancy found, the model must explicitly determine whether the difference is intentional (documented, tested, or structurally necessary) or a bug. A discrepancy report that does not reach this determination is incomplete.
acceptance_criteria: For each discrepancy found, trace both code paths with file:line citations and determine whether the difference is intentional (documented, tested, or structurally necessary) or a bug.
citation_excerpt: For each discrepancy found, trace both code paths with file:line citations and determine whether the difference is intentional (documented, tested, or structurally necessary) or a bug.

title: Trace call chain across files for most promising discrepancies
description: For the most promising discrepancies identified during pairwise comparison, the model must trace the call chain across files (not just within a single file). This cross-file tracing is required to verify lock/interrupt context, allocation flag validity, and contract requirements.
acceptance_criteria: For the most promising discrepancies, trace the call chain across files to verify: What lock/interrupt context each path runs in; What allocation flags are valid in that context; Whether the contract requires parity.

3. **Cross-file contract tracing.** For the most promising discrepancies, trace the call chain across files to verify:

   - Write findings to `quality/EXPLORATION_ITER{N}.md` with both code paths cited for each finding.

title: Verify lock/interrupt context during cross-file tracing

title: Apply lower evidentiary bar for adversarial re-investigation
description: During adversarial re-investigation, the runner must apply a deliberately lower evidentiary standard than used in earlier strategies. A code-path trace showing observable semantic drift is sufficient to confirm a finding; a runtime crash or dramatic failure is not required.
acceptance_criteria: A code-path trace showing observable semantic drift (output differs from what spec or contract requires) is sufficient to confirm — you do not need a runtime crash or dramatic failure
citation_excerpt:
- A code-path trace showing observable semantic drift (output differs from what spec or contract requires) is sufficient to confirm — you do not need a runtime crash or dramatic failure
   - "Permissive behavior" is not automatically a design choice — check whether the spec, docs, or API contract defines the expected behavior. If the code deviates from a documented contract, it's a bug regardless of whether the deviation is "permissive"
   - If the Demoted Candidates Manifest includes re-promotion criteria, attempt to satisfy those criteria specifically. Each criterion was written to be actionable — follow it
   - Read the specific code location cited in the finding
   - Trace the code path independently — do not rely on the previous run's analysis
   - Make an explicit CONFIRMED/FALSE-POSITIVE determination with fresh evidence
   - Update the Demoted Candidates Manifest: change status to RE-PROMOTED or FALSE POSITIVE with the iteration attribution

title: Prohibit invented harness-restriction rationalizations
description: The orchestrator must not produce text claiming any invented harness restriction as justification for skipping file writes, such as stating 'per system constraint: no report .md files'. Producing such text is a recognized signal that the orchestrator is about to collapse into single-context execution and must be treated as a hard stop.

title: Read DEVELOPMENT_CONTEXT.md Before Phase 1 If Present
description: If the file `ai_context/DEVELOPMENT_CONTEXT.md` exists in the skill repo or the working directory, the orchestrator must read it before Phase 1 begins. This grounding step is conditional on the file's existence and must occur prior to any phase execution, not during or after.

title: Assess Disk State After Sub-Agent Failure
description: When a sub-agent fails or runs out of context, the orchestrator must assess what was saved to disk before taking any other recovery action. Specifically, it must examine PROGRESS.md and the `quality/` directory to determine the state of completed work.
acceptance_criteria: Assess what was saved to disk (PROGRESS.md and the `quality/` directory) when a sub-agent fails or runs out of context.
citation_excerpt:
1. Assess what was saved to disk (PROGRESS.md and the `quality/` directory).
2. Report the failure with specifics.
3. Suggest retrying in a fresh sub-agent — phase writes are preserved incrementally, so a retry can pick up where the previous attempt left off.
4. Never skip phases — each depends on prior output.

title: Phase Writes Are Preserved Incrementally for Resume

title: Never Skip Phases During Error Recovery
description: Even in error recovery scenarios, the orchestrator must never skip any phase. Each phase depends on prior phase output, so skipping a phase — even to recover faster — is prohibited.

title: A contract is covered only when a requirement's conditions of satisfaction explicitly test the behavior
description: Coverage requires explicit testing of the specific behavior in a requirement's conditions of satisfaction. Implicit or tangential treatment does not qualify as coverage.
acceptance_criteria: A contract is 'covered' if a requirement's conditions of satisfaction explicitly test the behavior.
citation_excerpt: [file]: [contract summary] → REQ-NNN (conditions of satisfaction #M)

title: Tangential mention, implication, or partial same-file coverage does not constitute coverage
description: Three situations explicitly disqualify a contract from being considered covered: (1) the behavior is only tangentially mentioned, (2) the behavior is implied but not stated, (3) a different aspect of the same file is covered but the specific contract is not.
acceptance_criteria: A contract is NOT covered if it's only tangentially mentioned, implied but not stated, or if a different aspect of the same file is covered but this specific contract isn't.
citation_excerpt: For every contract in CONTRACTS.md, determine whether it is covered by a requirement. A contract is "covered" if a requirement's conditions of satisfaction explicitly test the behavior. A contract is NOT covered if it's only tangentially mentioned, implied but not stated, or if a different aspect of the same file is covered but this specific contract isn't.

title: Phase C loops until uncovered count reaches zero or three iterations are exhausted
description: Phase C is a loop: after producing the coverage matrix it checks whether any contracts remain uncovered. If the uncovered count is zero, the phase terminates and control passes to Phase D. Otherwise the matrix is regenerated and the check is repeated. The loop may not exceed three total iterations.
acceptance_criteria: Loop termination: If uncovered count reaches 0, proceed to Phase D. Otherwise, regenerate the matrix and check again. Maximum 3 iterations.
citation_excerpt: **Loop termination:** If uncovered count reaches 0, proceed to Phase D. Otherwise, regenerate the matrix and check again. Maximum 3 iterations.

title: Phase C iteration count is capped at three
description: Regardless of remaining uncovered contracts, Phase C must not perform more than three iterations of the coverage verification loop. The loop terminates after the third iteration even if uncovered contracts remain.
acceptance_criteria: Maximum 3 iterations.

citation_excerpt: Run all iterations.

title: Phase E adds explanatory tissue for top-to-bottom readability
description: Phase E must transform the requirements specification into a readable guide by adding explanatory prose (explanatory tissue) such that a new team member, code reviewer, or AI agent can read quality/REQUIREMENTS.md from top to bottom and understand the software. The output document must not be a bare list of requirements but must include connecting narrative.

title: Skill Provides Exactly Two Standalone Post-Pipeline Tools
description: After the four-pass pipeline completes, the skill must provide exactly two standalone tools for iterative improvement of the results. These tools exist because AI is not 100% reliable and the pipeline output is a baseline that may require further refinement. The two tools must be independently runnable outside the pipeline.

title: Review protocol creates and maintains REFINEMENT_HINTS.md with standard headings
description: The review protocol described in `quality/REVIEW_REQUIREMENTS.md` must create and maintain `quality/REFINEMENT_HINTS.md`, which starts with a `# Refinement Hints` heading and includes a `## Review Progress` section.
acceptance_criteria: The review protocol creates and maintains this file with headings: `# Refinement Hints` and `## Review Progress`.
citation_excerpt: The review protocol creates and maintains this file:

title: Review Progress Uses Checkbox List Per Use Case
description: The Review Progress section of REFINEMENT_HINTS.md MUST represent each use case as a separate checkbox list entry. The format uses Markdown task-list syntax so that each use case's completion state is individually trackable.
acceptance_criteria: Each use case appears as a distinct line in the form `- [x] Use Case N: [name] — ...` or `- [ ] Use Case N: [name]`, with one entry per use case.
skill_section: Review Progress section, lines 131–135
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Reviewed Use Cases Marked with [x]
description: A use case that has been reviewed MUST be marked with a filled checkbox `[x]` in the Review Progress section. An empty checkbox `[ ]` indicates the use case has not yet been reviewed.
acceptance_criteria: `- [x] Use Case 1: [name] — reviewed, no issues` and `- [x] Use Case 2: [name] — reviewed, see feedback below` demonstrate that completed reviews carry a checked box.
citation_excerpt:
- [x] Use Case 1: [name] — reviewed, no issues
- [x] Use Case 2: [name] — reviewed, see feedback below
- [ ] Use Case 3: [name]
- [ ] Use Case 4: [name]
...

title: Unreviewed Use Cases Marked with [ ]
description: A use case that has not yet been reviewed MUST be marked with an unchecked checkbox `[ ]` in the Review Progress section, with no trailing status note required.
acceptance_criteria: `- [ ] Use Case 3: [name]` and `- [ ] Use Case 4: [name]` show that pending use cases carry an empty box and may omit the status note.
citation_excerpt:
- [ ] Use Case 3: [name]
- [ ] Use Case 4: [name]
...

title: No-Issue Reviews Use 'reviewed, no issues' Status
description: When a reviewed use case has no defects or feedback to record, its Review Progress entry MUST append the status note `reviewed, no issues` after the use-case name.
acceptance_criteria: `- [x] Use Case 1: [name] — reviewed, no issues` is the required format for a clean review.
citation_excerpt:

- [x] Use Case 2: [name] — reviewed, see feedback below
- [ ] Use Case 3: [name]
- [ ] Use Case 4: [name]
...

title: Use Cases with Feedback Use 'reviewed, see feedback below' Status
description: When a reviewed use case has feedback recorded in the Feedback section of the same REFINEMENT_HINTS.md file, its Review Progress entry MUST append the status note `reviewed, see feedback below` so readers are directed to the corresponding feedback subsection.
acceptance_criteria: `- [x] Use Case 2: [name] — reviewed, see feedback below` is the required format when a Feedback subsection for that use case exists.
citation_excerpt:
- [x] Use Case 2: [name] — reviewed, see feedback below
- [ ] Use Case 3: [name]
- [ ] Use Case 4: [name]
...

title: Show Pre-flight Check Before Running Tests
description: Before executing any integration tests, the agent must display a pre-flight check that verifies dependencies, API keys, and environment configuration. This pre-flight step is the first phase of the execution UX and must appear before the test plan table or any test execution begins.
acceptance_criteria: Pre-flight check is displayed before any tests run, covering dependencies, API keys, and environment.
citation_excerpt:
**Pre-flight:** Checking dependencies, API keys, and environment
**Tests to run:**

title: Display Test Plan Table Before Execution
description: Before running any tests, the agent must show the user a plan table listing each test by number, test name, a one-line description of what it checks, and an estimated run time. This table allows the user to understand what is about to happen before any execution begins.
acceptance_criteria: Plan table is shown before any test runs, with columns for test number, test name, what it checks, and estimated time per row.
skill_section: Integration Test Plan section, Phase 1: The Plan table template
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Include Total Count and Estimated Time in Plan
description: The pre-execution plan must include a summary line showing the total number of tests and the aggregate estimated run time (e.g., 'Total: N tests, estimated M minutes'). This gives the user a complete upfront picture of scope.
acceptance_criteria: Plan footer shows 'Total: N tests, estimated M minutes' (or equivalent) before execution starts.
citation_excerpt:
**Total:** N tests, estimated M minutes
```

title: Allow User to Skip Tests Before Execution
description: After displaying the test plan and before running any tests, the agent must pause to give the user an opportunity to opt out of specific tests (e.g., 'skip test 4' or 'don't run the live API tests'). No test execution should begin until this interaction window has passed.
acceptance_criteria: User is given a chance to skip specific tests (e.g., 'skip test 4' or 'don't run the live API tests') before anything starts.
citation_excerpt: This gives the user a chance to say "skip test 4" or "actually, don't run the live API tests" before anything starts.

title: Report One-Line Status Per Test During Execution
description: As each test runs, the agent must emit exactly one line of status output per test. The status line must be compact — a heartbeat, not a log dump — and must include the test name and current state.
acceptance_criteria: As each test runs, a one-line status update is reported; the output is compact, not a log dump.
citation_excerpt: As each test runs, report a one-line status update. Keep it compact — the user wants a heartbeat, not a log dump:

title: Use ✓ Symbol for Passed Tests
description: In the progress output during test execution, every test that has passed must be prefixed with the ✓ symbol to allow the user to quickly scan results at a glance.
acceptance_criteria: Passed tests are displayed with the '✓' prefix in the progress stream.
skill_section: Integration Test Plan section, Phase 2: Progress: 'Use ✓ for pass'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Use ✗ Symbol for Failed Tests
description: In the progress output during test execution, every test that has failed must be prefixed with the ✗ symbol to visually distinguish failures from passes and in-progress tests.
acceptance_criteria: Failed tests are displayed with the '✗' prefix in the progress stream.
skill_section: Integration Test Plan section, Phase 2: Progress: 'Use ✗ for fail'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Use ⧗ Symbol for In-Progress Tests
description: In the progress output during test execution, any test currently running must be prefixed with the ⧗ symbol to indicate it is actively executing and not yet complete.
acceptance_criteria: In-progress tests are displayed with the '⧗' prefix in the progress stream.
skill_section: Integration Test Plan section, Phase 2: Progress: 'Use ⧗ for in-progress'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Show One Line of Context on Failure, Not Full Stack Trace
description: When a test fails, the agent must display exactly one line of context — the error message or the assertion that failed — inline with the test status output. The full stack trace must not be shown unless the user explicitly requests more details.
acceptance_criteria: On test failure, one line of context (error message or failed assertion) is shown; the full stack trace is not displayed unless requested.
citation_excerpt: Use `✓` for pass, `✗` for fail, `⧗` for in-progress. If a test fails, show one line of context (the error message or assertion that failed), not the full stack trace. The user can ask for details if they want them.

title: Show Results Summary Table After All Tests Complete
description: After all tests have finished executing, the agent must display a results summary table. The table must include columns for test number, test name, result (pass/fail), elapsed time, and any notes.
acceptance_criteria: After all tests complete, a summary table is shown with columns: test number, test name, result, time, and notes.
citation_excerpt: After all tests complete, show a summary table and a recommendation:

title: Display Passed/Failed Counts After Results Table
description: Following the results summary table, the agent must display aggregate pass and fail counts in the form 'Passed: X/Y | Failed: X/Y', where Y is the total number of tests executed.
acceptance_criteria: After the results table, the output shows 'Passed: X/Y | Failed: X/Y' (or equivalent aggregate counts).
skill_section: Integration Test Plan adjacent context, Results section: 'Passed: 7/8 | Failed: 1/8'
council_review_rationale: Structural reference to SKILL.md but Pass B's mechanical search did not verify; provisional Tier 1 / skill-section.

title: Show Recommendation After Results
description: After displaying the results table and pass/fail counts, the agent must output a recommendation label indicating the disposition of the test run. Valid values are SHIP, FIX BEFORE MERGE, and BLOCK.
acceptance_criteria: A recommendation is displayed after the results table, with value SHIP, FIX BEFORE MERGE, or BLOCK.
citation_excerpt:
### Recommendation
[SHIP / FIX BEFORE MERGE / BLOCK]
```

title: Save Detailed Results to Dated File
description: After completing the integration test run, the agent must save the full results to a file at the path quality/results/YYYY-MM-DD-integration.md, where YYYY-MM-DD is the current date.

title: Schema-Invalid Mutation Values Trigger Validation Errors Not Boundary Test Results
description: A test that assigns a schema-invalid value to a field (one that the validation layer rejects) will throw a validation or decoding error before processing logic is reached. This means the test tells the developer nothing about the actual output or business logic behavior, making it a test of the validation mechanism rather than of the requirement under review.
acceptance_criteria: The WRONG tests fail with a validation/decoding error because the mutation value isn't schema-valid.
citation_excerpt: The WRONG tests fail with a validation/decoding error because the mutation value isn't schema-valid. The RIGHT tests use values the schema accepts (null, None, nil, zero values, empty Option) so the mutation reaches the actual processing logic.

title: Boundary Tests Must Use Schema-Valid Mutation Values
description: For a boundary test to reach and exercise the actual processing logic, the mutation value chosen for any field must be one the schema accepts. Only schema-valid values (null, None, nil, zero values, empty Option) allow the mutation to bypass the validation layer and reach the code under test.
acceptance_criteria: The RIGHT tests use values the schema accepts (null, None, nil, zero values, empty Option) so the mutation reaches the actual processing logic.
citation_excerpt: The WRONG tests fail with a validation/decoding error because the mutation value isn't schema-valid. The RIGHT tests use values the schema accepts (null, None, nil, zero values, empty Option) so the mutation reaches the actual processing logic.

title: RIGHT Boundary Tests Assert Bad Data Is Absent From Output
description: A correctly written boundary test must include an assertion that the bad or omitted field value does not appear in the output. This verifies the actual requirement — that bad data is excluded — rather than merely confirming the schema validator fired.
acceptance_criteria: expect(output).not.toContain(badProperty) / assert field_property not in output / assertFalse(output.contains(badProperty)) / output should not contain badProperty

title: RIGHT Boundary Tests Assert That Rest of Processing Still Works
description: A correctly written boundary test must also assert that the remainder of the processing succeeds — i.e., that the expected type or expected output is still present even when the mutated field is missing or nulled. This confirms the mutation does not break unrelated behavior.
acceptance_criteria: expect(output).toContain(expectedType) / assert expected_type in output / assertTrue(output.contains(expectedType)) — 'Rest still works' assertion present in RIGHT test pattern

title: TypeScript Optional Fields Accept undefined As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In TypeScript projects using Zod schemas, the schema-valid mutation value for an optional field is undefined. Tests must use undefined rather than an arbitrary invalid string so the mutation passes Zod validation and reaches the processing function under test.
acceptance_criteria: fixture.field = undefined;  // Schema accepts undefined for optional fields — TypeScript RIGHT example

title: Python Optional Fields Accept None As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In Python projects using Pydantic models, the schema-valid mutation value for an Optional field is None. Tests must use None rather than an invalid string so the mutation passes Pydantic validation and reaches the processing function under test.
acceptance_criteria: fixture.field = None  # Schema accepts None for Optional fields — Python RIGHT example
citation_excerpt:
fixture.field = None  # Schema accepts None for Optional fields
    output = process(fixture)
    assert field_property not in output  # Bad data absent
    assert expected_type in output  # Rest still works
```

title: Java Nullable Fields Accept null As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In Java projects using Bean Validation annotations, the schema-valid mutation value for a nullable String field is null. Tests must use null rather than a value that triggers @NotNull or @Pattern constraints so the mutation reaches the processing method under test.
acceptance_criteria: fixture.setField(null);  // nullable String field accepts null — Java RIGHT example

title: Scala Option Fields Accept None As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In Scala projects using Circe decoders with case classes, the schema-valid mutation value for an Option[String] field is None. Tests must use None rather than an invalid string literal so the mutation passes decoder validation and reaches the processing logic under test.
acceptance_criteria: val input = fixture.copy(field = None)  // Option[String] accepts None — Scala RIGHT example
citation_excerpt:
val input = fixture.copy(field = None)  // Option[String] accepts None
    val output = process(input)
    output should not contain badProperty
}
```

title: Go Optional String Fields Accept Zero Value As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In Go projects using struct tag validators, the schema-valid mutation value for an optional string field is the zero value (empty string ""). Tests must use the zero value rather than an invalid non-empty string so the mutation passes struct tag validation and reaches the processing function under test.
acceptance_criteria: fixture.Field = ""  // Zero value is valid for optional string fields — Go RIGHT example

title: Rust Option Fields Accept None As Schema-Valid Mutation Value
description: In Rust projects using serde for deserialization, the schema-valid mutation value for an Option<String> field is None. Tests must use None rather than an arbitrary string value so the mutation passes serde deserialization and reaches the processing function under test.
acceptance_criteria: let input = Fixture { field: None, ..default() };  // Option<String> accepts None — Rust RIGHT example

title: Schema map must cover every field with a defensive pattern
description: The schema mapping step requires that every field for which a defensive pattern was identified in Step 5 has a corresponding entry in the schema map. No field with a defensive pattern may be omitted from the map.
acceptance_criteria: For every field you found a defensive pattern for in Step 5, record [an entry in the map].
citation_excerpt: For every field you found a defensive pattern for in Step 5, record:

title: Schema map must have four columns: Field, Schema Type, Accepts, Rejects
description: Each entry in the schema map must record exactly four pieces of information: the field name, its schema type (with language-idiomatic type annotations), the set of values it accepts, and the set of values it rejects. Maps missing any of these columns are incomplete.
acceptance_criteria: Record: Field | Schema Type | Accepts | Rejects for each entry.
citation_excerpt:
| Field | Schema Type | Accepts | Rejects |
|-------|-----------|---------|---------|
| `metadata` | optional object (`Optional[MetadataObject]` / `MetadataObject?` / `MetadataObject \| null`) | valid object, `null`/`undefined` | `string`, `number`, `array` |
| `count_field` | optional integer (`Optional[int]` / `number?` / `Integer`) | integer, `null` | `string`, `object` |
| `child_list` | array of objects (`List[Child]` / `Child[]` / `Seq[Child]`) | array of objects, `[]` | `[null, "invalid"]`, `null` |
| `optional_object` | optional object | `{"key": value}`, `null` | `"bad"`, `[1,2]` |

title: Optional object fields must list valid object and null/undefined in Accepts
description: For any field whose schema type is an optional object (e.g., Optional[MetadataObject], MetadataObject?, or MetadataObject | null), the Accepts column of the schema map must include both a valid object value and null/undefined.
acceptance_criteria: For `metadata` (optional object): Accepts = valid object, `null`/`undefined`.

| `metadata` | optional object (`Optional[MetadataObject]` / `MetadataObject?` / `MetadataObject \| null`) | valid object, `null`/`undefined` | `string`, `number`, `array` |
| `count_field` | optional integer (`Optional[int]` / `number?` / `Integer`) | integer, `null` | `string`, `object` |
| `child_list` | array of objects (`List[Child]` / `Child[]` / `Seq[Child]`) | array of objects, `[]` | `[null, "invalid"]`, `null` |
| `optional_object` | optional object | `{"key": value}`, `null` | `"bad"`, `[1,2]` |

title: Optional object fields must list string, number, and array in Rejects
description: For any field whose schema type is an optional object, the Rejects column of the schema map must enumerate at minimum the following wrong-type values: string, number, and array. These represent the most common incorrect types that callers might pass.
acceptance_criteria: For `metadata` (optional object): Rejects = `string`, `number`, `array`.

| `metadata` | optional object (`Optional[MetadataObject]` / `MetadataObject?` / `MetadataObject \| null`) | valid object, `null`/`undefined` | `string`, `number`, `array` |

title: Optional integer fields must list integer and null in Accepts
description: For any field whose schema type is an optional integer (e.g., Optional[int], number?, or Integer), the Accepts column of the schema map must include both a valid integer value and null.
acceptance_criteria: For `count_field` (optional integer): Accepts = integer, `null`.

title: Optional integer fields must list string and object in Rejects
description: For any field whose schema type is an optional integer, the Rejects column of the schema map must enumerate at minimum string and object as rejected values, since these are wrong-type inputs that bypass integer validation.
acceptance_criteria: For `count_field` (optional integer): Rejects = `string`, `object`.

title: Array-of-objects fields must list array of objects and empty array in Accepts
description: For any field whose schema type is an array of objects (e.g., List[Child], Child[], or Seq[Child]), the Accepts column of the schema map must include both a populated array of objects and an empty array, because the empty array is a valid boundary value.

title: Array-of-objects fields must list mixed-null array and null in Rejects
description: For any field whose schema type is an array of objects, the Rejects column of the schema map must include arrays containing null or invalid elements (e.g., [null, "invalid"]) and a bare null value, because these are the common invalid inputs for list fields.

title: Optional object fields must list string and array literals in Rejects
